# AI Workflow Setup

## Overview
- **Date**: 2026-03-17 00:27:50
- **Conversation ID**: `69b8e60f-fd1c-8329-8734-044228d54470`
- **Source**: `Chat-Export-28513-2026/conversations-005.json`

## Chat History

### User
> girl you want to give me the file to ai workflow we were talkign about earlier and where i should build out the local directory, starting with the python tool module for the local parts, which llm where to install it and the edge functions, api (we can use the same fast api we already have i assume)

### Assistant
> IвҖҷm going to pin this down as an actual build layout instead of hand-wavy вҖңAI workflowвҖқ fluff: flow, folders, local Python module, model placement, and how FastAPI + edge functions split responsibilities.

### Assistant
> Yes вҖ” use the same FastAPI you already have. Do **not** spin up a second API unless you enjoy unnecessary chaos.
> 
> ## The actual workflow
> 
> This is the clean path:
> 
> **1. File arrives**
> - upload from client portal
> - drop into watched local folder
> - manual import
> - Drive sync pull
> - email attachment intake later if you want
> 
> **2. Intake layer**
> - assign immutable file ID
> - calculate sha256
> - capture source path, client ID, timestamps, mime type
> - write one row to database
> - copy or move file into local hot storage
> 
> **3. Processing layer**
> - OCR if needed
> - text extraction
> - metadata extraction
> - chunking
> - embeddings
> - optional classification:
>   - tax doc
>   - legal doc
>   - medical
>   - client admin
>   - unknown
> 
> **4. AI layer**
> - local LLM does cheap/private tasks:
>   - classify
>   - summarize
>   - suggest destination folder
>   - extract fields
> - cloud LLM only for heavier reasoning when needed
> 
> **5. Storage/index layer**
> - original file in blob/file storage
> - extracted text in DB or sidecar text files
> - embeddings in pgvector
> - structured entities in DB
> - suggested folder + confidence score stored
> 
> **6. Action layer**
> - user reviews suggestion
> - approve move/rename/tag
> - system logs final disposition
> - optional sync back to Drive / vault
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the backbone. Keep it boring. Boring scales.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What goes where
> 
> ## Use this split
> 
> ### Local machine
> This is where your **Python tool module** lives.
> 
> Handle:
> - file watching
> - OCR / parsing
> - hashing
> - local classification
> - embedding generation if local
> - folder suggestion
> - queue jobs
> - temp processing cache
> - hot directory management
> 
> ### FastAPI
> This is your **control plane**.
> 
> Handle:
> - API endpoints
> - job orchestration
> - auth
> - dashboard data
> - review queue
> - search endpoints
> - webhook handling
> - model routing decisions
> - handoff between local worker and cloud services
> 
> ### Edge functions
> Use them for **small secure server-side actions near the app**, not heavy processing.
> 
> Handle:
> - signed upload URLs
> - auth/session helpers
> - webhook receivers
> - lightweight event triggers
> - notification dispatch
> - audit logging glue
> - tiny transforms
> 
> Do **not** run OCR pipelines or big AI jobs in edge functions. Wrong tool.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Recommended architecture
> 
> ## Monorepo structure
> 
> Something like this:
> 
> ```text
> qione/
> в”ңв”Җ apps/
> в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җ api/                         # existing FastAPI app
> в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җ app/
> в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җ api/

> в”Ӯ  в””в”Җ worker-local/                # optional runner wrapper if separate
> в”Ӯ
> в”ңв”Җ packages/

> в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җ pyproject.toml
> в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җ src/

> ---
> 
> # Where to build the local directory
> 
> Build the **real working local directory outside the repo**, and keep only code/config templates inside the repo.
> 
> Why:
> - model files get huge
> - hot folders change constantly
> - you do not want your repo polluted with runtime junk
> 
> ## Do this
> 
> ### In repo
> ```text
> packages/pytools_local/
> ```
> 
> ### On machine
> ```text
> D:\QiOneData\
> в”ңв”Җ hot\
> в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җ inbox\
> в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җ processing\
> в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җ reviewed\
> в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җ failed\
> в”Ӯ  в””в”Җ export\
> в”ңв”Җ vault_mirror\
> в”ңв”Җ models\
> в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җ llm\
> в”Ӯ  в”ңв”Җ embeddings\
> в”Ӯ  в””в”Җ rerank\
> в”ңв”Җ cache\
> в”ңв”Җ ocr\
> в”ңв”Җ logs\
> в””в”Җ manifests\
> ```
> 
> If you prefer Google Drive-backed workspace, fine, but the **hot processing area should still be local SSD**, not directly inside synced Drive folders. Otherwise youвҖҷll create file lock/sync nonsense for yourself.
> 
> So the pattern is:
> 
> - **local SSD hot path**
> - then sync approved outputs back to Drive/Vault
> 
> ---
> 
> # Python tool module: start here
> 
> Your first module should be this:
> 
> ```text
> packages/pytools_local/src/pytools_local/
> ```
> 
> And the first files to build are:
> 
> ```text
> config.py
> cli.py
> watcher.py
> hashing.py
> ingest.py
> extractor/pdf.py
> extractor/ocr.py
> classify.py
> routing.py
> db_sync.py
> ```
> 
> ## Minimal responsibility of v1
> - watch inbox
> - hash file
> - assign ID
> - extract text from PDF
> - save extracted text
> - call local LLM for classification
> - suggest destination path
> - POST result to FastAPI
> 
> ThatвҖҷs enough to prove the pipeline without overbuilding.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Which LLM to install locally
> 
> For your case, stop trying to make the local model do everything.
> 
> ## Best local role
> Local model should do:
> - document classification
> - extraction to JSON
> - filename normalization
> - folder suggestion
> - short summaries
> - quick QA on extracted text
> 
> ## Not ideal local role
> - deep legal reasoning
> - large multi-document synthesis
> - advanced writing
> - high-stakes final outputs
> 
> That should stay cloud-side.
> 
> ## Good local setup
> Use **Ollama** as the local model runner unless you have a strong reason not to. It is the least annoying path.
> 
> ### Install
> - Ollama on your main workstation
> - model files stored in your local `models/llm` area if you want custom placement, otherwise default is fine initially
> 
> ## Model recommendation
> For local document work in your environment:
> 
> ### Primary local model
> **Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct** or its current equivalent in Ollama  
> Good balance of:
> - extraction
> - classification
> - decent structured output
> - lighter hardware demands
> 
> ### If you have more VRAM / patience
> **Qwen 2.5 14B Instruct**
> - better reasoning
> - slower
> - heavier
> 
> ### For tiny/fast fallback
> **Llama 3.1 8B Instruct** if available in your setup
> - decent
> - widely supported
> - not my first pick for structured extraction, but workable
> 
> ## My recommendation
> Install:
> - **Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct** for general local tasks
> - **nomic-embed-text** or similar local embedding model if you want fully local embeddings
> - optional reranker later
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the sane stack.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Embeddings
> 
> You have two options:
> 
> ## Option A вҖ” easier
> Use API embeddings from the cloud for consistency and better quality.
> 
> Good if:
> - you care more about result quality
> - your volume is manageable
> - you donвҖҷt want local embedding maintenance
> 
> ## Option B вҖ” privacy/local-first
> Use local embeddings via Ollama or sentence-transformers.
> 
> Good starter local choice:
> - **bge-small-en-v1.5** if mostly English
> - multilingual model if you need bilingual support often
> 
> For your actual reality, IвҖҷd do:
> 
> - **local classification + extraction**
> - **cloud embeddings at first**
> - move embeddings local later only if cost/privacy truly justifies it
> 
> Because otherwise youвҖҷll overcomplicate v1.
> 
> ---
> 
> # FastAPI role
> 
> Yes, use the same FastAPI app.
> 
> Add these route groups:
> 
> ```text
> /api/files
> /api/intake
> /api/jobs
> /api/search
> /api/review
> /api/llm
> /api/admin
> ```
> 
> ## Example flow
> Local worker posts:
> 
> ```json
> POST /api/intake/result
> {
>   "file_id": "BBR-20260317-0012",
>   "sha256": "...",
>   "source_path": "D:/QiOneData/hot/inbox/scan123.pdf",
>   "extracted_text_path": "D:/QiOneData/cache/text/scan123.txt",
>   "classification": "tax_document",
>   "confidence": 0.93,
>   "suggested_client_id": "BBR0001",
>   "suggested_destination": "40.Clients/BBR0001/Tax/2025/",
>   "entities": {
>     "tax_year": 2025,
>     "document_type": "1099-NEC"
>   }
> }
> ```
> 
> FastAPI then:
> - writes DB records
> - creates review task
> - exposes results to UI
> - triggers notifications
> 
> ---
> 
> # Edge functions
> 
> Use them for these:
> 
> ## 1. `signed-upload`
> Generate secure upload URLs from portal/client side.
> 
> ## 2. `intake-webhook`
> Receive upload-complete event and create intake job.
> 
> ## 3. `notify-review`
> Ping you when files need review.
> 
> ## 4. `search-proxy`
> If you need thin secure search helpers from frontend to DB.
> 
> ThatвҖҷs it. Keep them skinny.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Suggested DB tables
> 
> At minimum:
> 
> ```text
> files
> file_versions
> file_text
> file_entities
> file_embeddings
> file_jobs
> file_routing_suggestions
> file_review_queue
> ```
> 
> ## Core fields for `files`
> - id
> - client_id
> - original_filename
> - normalized_filename
> - sha256
> - mime_type
> - source
> - source_path
> - storage_path
> - status
> - created_at
> - processed_at
> 
> ## Core fields for `file_routing_suggestions`
> - id
> - file_id
> - suggested_path
> - suggested_client_id
> - reason
> - confidence
> - approved_by
> - approved_at
> 
> ---
> 
> # Recommended local processing tools
> 
> ## Core Python libs
> - `watchdog` for file watching
> - `pydantic` for config/schema
> - `sqlalchemy` if talking directly to DB
> - `httpx` for FastAPI calls
> - `pypdf` for PDFs
> - `unstructured` only if you really need it later
> - `pytesseract` or better OCR stack if needed
> - `python-magic` for mime detection
> - `sentence-transformers` if local embeddings
> - `orjson` for fast JSON
> 
> ## OCR
> If you need local OCR:
> - Tesseract is acceptable for v1
> - PaddleOCR can be better in some cases
> - do not start with three OCR engines at once
> 
> ---
> 
> # The real split IвҖҷd use for you
> 
> ## Local
> - watcher
> - parser
> - OCR
> - local LLM classify/extract
> - temp cache
> - folder suggestion
> - push results to API
> 
> ## FastAPI
> - system of record
> - review UI backend
> - search backend
> - job state
> - permissions
> - connectors
> - downstream automation triggers
> 
> ## Supabase / DB
> - metadata
> - structured entities
> - search records
> - embeddings
> - review status
> - audit trail
> 
> ## Edge
> - upload glue
> - notification glue
> - auth glue
> 
> ---
> 
> # Order to build this without getting lost
> 
> ## Phase 1
> Build only:
> - local inbox watcher
> - file hashing
> - text extraction
> - local LLM classification
> - POST to FastAPI
> - review queue in DB
> 
> ## Phase 2
> Add:
> - destination suggestion
> - client matching
> - rename suggestion
> - approval action
> 
> ## Phase 3
> Add:
> - embeddings
> - semantic search
> - cross-file QA
> - intake from portal uploads
> 
> ## Phase 4
> Add:
> - Drive sync
> - background retries
> - confidence thresholds
> - automated moves for high-confidence low-risk docs
> 
> Do **not** start with auto-moving files blindly. That is how you create your own future cleanup job.
> 
> ---
> 
> # My blunt recommendation
> 
> If you want the cleanest starting point, do this:
> 
> ## Install locally
> - Python 3.11 or 3.12
> - Ollama
> - Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct
> - Tesseract only if OCR is needed immediately
> 
> ## Build in repo
> - `packages/pytools_local/`
> - add endpoints to your existing `apps/api/`
> 
> ## Build on disk
> - `D:\QiOneData\hot\...`
> - `D:\QiOneData\models\...`
> - `D:\QiOneData\cache\...`
> 
> ## Use FastAPI as
> - orchestration API
> - review API
> - search API
> - system-of-record API
> 
> ## Use edge functions only for
> - signed uploads
> - upload callbacks
> - notifications
> 
> That is the right split.
> 
> If you want, IвҖҷll turn this into the exact folder tree plus starter `pyproject.toml`, `config.py`, and `FastAPI` endpoint skeleton so you can drop it straight into QiOne.

> 
> This is where entropy is breeding.

> 
> ---
> 
> # The correct placement, based on this repo
> 
> ## Canonical homes going forward
> 
> ### A. Local Python tools and file-processing logic
> **Canonical home:**
> ```text
> python_core/
> ```
> 
> That is where the local file-to-AI pipeline belongs.
> 
> Not inside:
> - `apps/qione/src/features/desktop/python_tools`
> - not root `scripts/` except wrappers
> - not `supabase/functions`
> - not `workers/`
> 
> Because the moment it touches:
> - filesystem
> - OCR
> - local watch folders
> - Python libraries
> - long-running jobs
> - local LLMs
> 
> вҖҰit belongs in `python_core`.
> 
> ---
> 
> ### B. UI and review screens
> **Canonical home:**
> ```text
> apps/qione/
> ```
> 
> This is where:
> - inbox review UI
> - document search UI
> - worker health UI
> - admin queue UI
> - upload panels
> should live.
> 
> You already have:
> - `src/app/(app)/documents/*`
> - `src/app/api/documents/*`
> - `components/documents/*`
> 
> ThatвҖҷs exactly where the frontend/API surface should stay.
> 
> ---
> 
> ### C. Shared TS schemas/adapters
> **Canonical home:**
> ```text
> packages/
> ```
> 
> Use this for:
> - shared types
> - AI adapters
> - DB adapters
> - QiNode shared code
> - reusable UI
> 
> Do **not** put Python workflow logic here.
> 
> ---
> 
> ### D. Edge/cloud worker glue
> **Canonical home:**
> ```text
> workers/
> supabase/functions/
> ```
> 
> Use for:
> - orchestration
> - queue fanout
> - remote triggers
> - edge-safe transforms
> - cloud-side metadata steps
> 
> Do **not** put local filesystem crawling or OCR here.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Your current problem: root scripts vs nested scripts
> 
> You noticed the repo has scripts in multiple places. ThatвҖҷs because they are serving **different layers**, but the naming doesnвҖҷt make that obvious.
> 
> HereвҖҷs the clean distinction you should enforce:
> 
> ## 1. Root `/scripts`
> Use only for **repo/operator commands**.
> 
> Examples from your tree that belong there:
> - `scripts/inbox/*`
> - `scripts/pdf/*`
> - `scripts/rules/*`
> - `scripts/sync/*`
> - `scripts/tools/*`
> 
> These should be:
> - orchestration wrappers
> - audits
> - one-off runners
> - repo maintenance
> - developer utilities
> 
> Think: **entrypoints and maintenance**, not core business logic.
> 
> So root `scripts/` should call into real modules, not become the module.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 2. `python_core/local_services/scripts`
> Use for **service-level runner scripts** only.
> 
> Examples:
> - `api_ingest.py`
> - `api_query.py`
> - `check_status.py`
> - `check_workers.py`
> 
> These are okay there because theyвҖҷre tied to the Python local services domain.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 3. Nested app-specific scripts
> Like:
> - `apps/qione/src/features/desktop/python_tools/build.bat`
> - various site-specific `scripts/`
> 
> These should be treated as **local to that subproject only**.  
> Not canonical for platform-wide ingestion.
> 
> That means your desktop Python tool folder is **not** where the real future ingestion pipeline should grow.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Blunt architecture call
> 
> ## The canonical file-to-AI workflow should be split like this:
> 
> ### Local processing engine
> ```text
> python_core/local_services/
> ```
> 
> ### Platform API/UI
> ```text
> apps/qione/
> ```
> 
> ### Shared DB/schema/types
> ```text
> packages/database
> packages/ai
> packages/qinode
> ```
> 
> ### Remote worker fanout/orchestration
> ```text
> workers/
> supabase/functions/
> ```
> 
> That is the cleanest fit to the repo you already have.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Exact recommendation for the local parts
> 
> You asked where to start with the Python tool module for the local parts.
> 
> ## Do not create a new random module.
> Use this as the canonical base:
> 
> ```text
> python_core/local_services/
> ```
> 
> And inside it, add a focused subdomain for document/file ingestion.
> 
> ## Create this
> ```text
> python_core/local_services/file_pipeline/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ __init__.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ config.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ models.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ queue.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ watcher.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ ingest.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ classify.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ extract.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ route.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ llm_client.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ embeddings.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ api_client.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ manifest.py
> в””в”Җв”Җ rules.py
> ```
> 
> That should become the real engine.
> 
> ### Why here
> Because `python_core/local_services` already contains:
> - jobs
> - worker
> - rag
> - memory
> - tools
> - migrations
> - integrations
> 
> So the file pipeline is clearly part of that world, not an isolated desktop gadget.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What to do with `apps/qione/src/features/desktop/python_tools`
> 
> Do **not** kill it immediately. Reclassify it.
> 
> Treat it as:
> 
> ```text
> apps/qione/src/features/desktop/python_tools
> = desktop utility shell / legacy tool UI / operator launcher
> ```
> 
> Meaning:
> - keep `main_ui.py`
> - keep tool wrappers if useful
> - keep packaging/build files if you still need desktop executable behavior
> 
> But stop putting **new canonical ingestion logic** there.
> 
> Instead, that layer should call into:
> ```text
> python_core/local_services/file_pipeline/
> ```
> 
> So this folder becomes a **launcher/client**, not the brain.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What to do with root `scripts/inbox` and `scripts/pdf`
> 
> These are actually useful. DonвҖҷt delete them blindly.
> 
> But they should become thin wrappers around canonical logic.
> 
> ## Good future state
> - `scripts/inbox/run_inbox.ts` calls platform or worker orchestration
> - `scripts/pdf/process_inbox_pdfs.ts` calls canonical APIs or canonical Python service
> - complex logic moves into `python_core/local_services/file_pipeline/*`
> 
> So the rule is:
> 
> ### root scripts = commands
> ### python_core = implementation
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the missing boundary.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Use the same API?
> 
> Yes, but with one correction.
> 
> You said:
> > we can use the same fast api we already have i assume
> 
> From this tree, your **platform-facing API reality** looks more like:
> - `apps/qione/src/app/api/*` for Next app routes
> - `workers/*` for worker processes
> - `supabase/functions/qivault-ingest`
> - plus Python local services in `python_core/local_services`
> 
> I do **not** see a clean standalone FastAPI app root in the tree you pasted.
> 
> So either:
> 1. you have a FastAPI app elsewhere not shown, or
> 2. the Python service layer is acting like your local API backbone already, but not formalized as a FastAPI app yet
> 
> ## My recommendation
> If you already have a working FastAPI instance outside this slice, yes, reuse it.
> 
> If not, then the smarter move is:
> 
> ### Keep external/UI-facing API in `apps/qione/src/app/api/*`
> ### Add internal local-service API in `python_core/local_services/api/`
> 
> That internal API can be FastAPI.

> 
> # Best actual shape for your repo
> 
> ## 1. Local Python service

> в”ңв”Җв”Җ routes_query.py
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ routes_workers.py
> в””в”Җв”Җ routes_health.py
> ```
> 
> Then:

> - qione UI can call this API for local machine status if needed
> - worker scripts can call it too
> 
> This fits your repo much better than shoving FastAPI into `apps/qione`.

> Not local watch folders.

> # LLM placement: what to install and where
> 
> ## Local runner
> Install **Ollama** on the workstation.
> 
> Use it from the Python local service.
> 
> ## Recommended local model
> For your use case:
> - file classification
> - extraction
> - routing
> - lightweight summaries
> 
> Use:
> - **Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct** as primary local model
> 
> Optional:
> - **Qwen 2.5 14B** if your machine can handle it and you want better extraction/reasoning
> - local embedding model later if needed
> 
> ## Why not shove the model into repo?
> Because that would be dumb. Too big, too brittle.
> 
> Model files belong in:
> ```text
> C:\QiData\model_cache\
> ```
> or OllamaвҖҷs managed storage.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The exact workflow, mapped to your repo
> 
> ## Step 1 вҖ” file lands
> From:
> - `apps/qione` upload route
> - local watched folder
> - sync script
> - manual import
> 
> ## Step 2 вҖ” local pipeline handles it

> Tasks:
> - hash
> - inspect mime
> - OCR/text extract
> - classify
> - suggest routing
> - write manifest
> - send result onward

> ## Step 3 вҖ” platform stores state
> In:
> - `packages/database`
> - `supabase/migrations`
> - `apps/qione/src/app/api/documents/*`
> 
> ## Step 4 вҖ” worker fanout
> In:
> - `workers/ingestion`
> - `workers/metadata_naming`
> - `workers/embedder`
> - `workers/orchestrator`
> 
> ## Step 5 вҖ” user reviews in Qione
> In:
> - `apps/qione/src/app/(app)/documents/inbox/page.tsx`
> - `components/documents/*`
> 
> That is the real map.
>

> # What I would declare as repo policy right now

> 
> ## Canonical rules
> - **All local Python business logic** goes under `python_core/`
> - **All platform UI** goes under `apps/qione/`
> - **All shared TS libs** go under `packages/`
> - **All cloud/edge async workers** go under `workers/` or `supabase/functions/`
> - **Root `scripts/` are wrappers, audits, and operator commands only**
> - **No new canonical logic in `apps/qione/src/features/desktop/python_tools`**
> - **No runtime processing data inside repo**
> 
> That would stop the bleeding.
> 
> ---
> 
> # My practical recommendation for you

> ## Keep

> python_core/local_services/api/

> - `workers/ingestion`

> That is the cleanest move without pretending the repo is something it isnвҖҷt.
> 
> ## If you want the blunt truth
> You do **not** need another architecture idea. You need a **canonicality decision**:
> - `python_core` is the local engine
> - `apps/qione` is the app
> - `workers` are async glue
> - `scripts` are wrappers

### User
> give em a python commnad to add those file pipeline files.

### Assistant
> Yep. Use this from the repo root:
> 
> ```bash
> python -c "from pathlib import Path; base=Path('python_core/local_services/file_pipeline'); dirs=['', 'tests']; files=['__init__.py','config.py','models.py','queue.py','watcher.py','ingest.py','classify.py','extract.py','route.py','llm_client.py','embeddings.py','api_client.py','manifest.py','rules.py','tests/__init__.py']; [ (base/d).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) for d in dirs ]; [ (base/f).touch(exist_ok=True) for f in files ]; print(f'Created file pipeline scaffold at {base.resolve()}')"
> ```
> 
> If you want the same thing as a tiny reusable script with cleaner output, use this:
> 
> ```python
> from pathlib import Path
> 
> base = Path("python_core/local_services/file_pipeline")
> 
> dirs = [
>     "",
>     "tests",
> ]
> 
> files = [
>     "__init__.py",
>     "config.py",
>     "models.py",
>     "queue.py",
>     "watcher.py",
>     "ingest.py",
>     "classify.py",
>     "extract.py",
>     "route.py",
>     "llm_client.py",
>     "embeddings.py",
>     "api_client.py",
>     "manifest.py",
>     "rules.py",
>     "tests/__init__.py",
> ]
> 
> for d in dirs:
>     (base / d).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
> 
> for f in files:
>     path = base / f
>     path.touch(exist_ok=True)
>     print(f"created: {path}")
> 
> print(f"\nDone: {base.resolve()}")
> ```
> 
> If you also want the `python_core/local_services/api/` scaffold in one shot, run this:
> 
> ```bash
> python -c "from pathlib import Path; specs={'python_core/local_services/file_pipeline':['__init__.py','config.py','models.py','queue.py','watcher.py','ingest.py','classify.py','extract.py','route.py','llm_client.py','embeddings.py','api_client.py','manifest.py','rules.py','tests/__init__.py'],'python_core/local_services/api':['__init__.py','main.py','routes_ingest.py','routes_query.py','routes_workers.py','routes_health.py']}; [Path(k).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) for k in specs]; [ (Path(k)/f).parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) or (Path(k)/f).touch(exist_ok=True) for k,v in specs.items() for f in v ]; print('Created file_pipeline and api scaffolds.')"
> ```

### User
> ok so it sound like we really should have at root, app/gione, python_local, python_core (my tool box), scripts, workers, packages, docs,

### Assistant
> Yes. ThatвҖҷs the cleaner top-level split.
> 
> IвҖҷd tighten the names a bit though, because **`python_local` and `python_core` can blur together fast** if you donвҖҷt define the boundary hard.
> 
> ## Best root layout
> 
> ```text
> /
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ apps/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ python_local/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ python_core/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ scripts/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ workers/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ packages/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ docs/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ supabase/
> ```
> 
> And if you want to keep it brutally clear:
> 
> - **`apps/`** = user-facing apps and app APIs
> - **`python_local/`** = machine-local services, watchers, ingestion, OCR, local LAG/RAG helpers, local API
> - **`python_core/`** = reusable Python toolbox, shared libraries, utilities, base classes, processors, agent primitives
> - **`scripts/`** = thin runner scripts, audits, maintenance, wrappers
> - **`workers/`** = async/edge/cloud workers
> - **`packages/`** = shared TS packages/libs
> - **`docs/`** = architecture, ADRs, repo rules, maps
> - **`supabase/`** = DB migrations, functions, config
> 
> ## The real distinction that matters
> 
> ### `python_core`
> This should be your **Python library layer**.
> 
> Stuff like:
> - base tool classes
> - text extraction primitives
> - shared models
> - common connectors
> - utility functions
> - reusable processors
> - common config loaders
> - manifest helpers
> 
> Think: **importable building blocks**
> 
> ### `python_local`
> This should be your **runtime / local services layer**.
> 
> Stuff like:
> - file watchers
> - local ingestion pipeline
> - queue runners
> - local FastAPI app
> - local job processor
> - ollama client wiring
> - local filesystem sync
> - machine-specific config
> 
> Think: **things that run on your machine**
> 
> That split is actually good.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## What not to do
> 
> Do **not** let `python_core` become:
> - another dump folder
> - random scripts
> - machine-specific logic
> - half app / half library soup
> 
> Do **not** let `scripts/` contain:
> - real business logic
> - extraction engines
> - routing brains
> - duplicate ingestion code
> 
> ThatвҖҷs where repos start smelling like old onions.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Even cleaner version
> 
> If you want this sharper, use:
> 
> ```text
> /
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ apps/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ python_local/
> в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ api/
> в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ file_pipeline/
> в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ services/
> в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ jobs/
> в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ config/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ python_core/
> в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ tools/
> в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ document_processors/
> в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ agents/
> в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ audio/
> в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ repo/
> в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ shared/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ scripts/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ workers/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ packages/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ docs/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ supabase/
> ```
> 
> That gives you:
> 
> - **python_core = toolkit**
> - **python_local = operational runtime**
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Mapping your current mess into that shape
> 
> ### Move toward `python_local`
> Current stuff like:
> - `python_core/local_services/*`
> - watcher logic
> - ingest jobs
> - local worker runners
> - local API scripts
> 
> These are better conceptually under **`python_local/`**.
> 
> ### Keep in `python_core`
> Current stuff like:
> - `agents/`
> - `audio/`
> - `document_processors/`
> - `repo/`
> - reusable tool classes
> - extractors
> - shared helpers
> 
> These belong in **`python_core/`**.
> 
> So yes вҖ” your instinct is right. The issue is not the root categories. The issue is making sure **runtime local services** stop living inside `python_core/local_services` forever if you want the tree to stay sane.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## My recommendation
> 
> Use this:
> 
> ```text
> /
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ apps/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ python_local/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ python_core/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ scripts/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ workers/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ packages/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ docs/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ supabase/
> ```
> 
> And define the rule as:
> 
> - **If it runs as a local service/process, it goes in `python_local`**
> - **If itвҖҷs reusable Python logic, it goes in `python_core`**
> - **If itвҖҷs a one-shot command, it goes in `scripts`**
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the right architecture call.
> 
> The only typo fix: `app/gione` should be `apps/qione`.

### Assistant
> Yes вҖ” add `sites/` too.
> 
> That gives you a sane root:
> 
> ```text
> /
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ apps/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ sites/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ python_local/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ python_core/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ scripts/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ workers/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ packages/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ docs/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ supabase/
> ```
> 
> And on the filename question: **yes, add a short human-visible ID suffix**, but do **not** make it the primary identity of the file.
> 
> ## The right answer
> 
> Use **two IDs**:
> 
> ### 1. System identity
> A full immutable internal ID in metadata/database/manifest:
> - UUID
> - ULID
> - or your QiDecimal/file registry ID
> 
> This is the real identity.
> 
> ### 2. Human filename token
> A short visible token in the filename:
> - for eyeballing
> - for search
> - for conflict reduction
> - for quick verbal reference
> 
> That part can be short.
> 
> ## Your idea: `Q` + 6 hex chars
> 
> That is actually pretty solid.
> 
> Example:
> 
> ```text
> 2025_tax_return_BuiltByRays_QA3F91C.pdf
> bank_statement_march_2026_Q1C8E2A.pdf
> notice_of_hearing_QF09B77.pdf
> ```
> 
> That stays readable without making filenames look like a hostage note.
> 
> ## Why 6 hex can work
> 
> 6 hex chars gives you:
> 
> - `16^6 = 16,777,216` possible values
> 
> That is enough for human-visible suffix use in your environment.
> 
> ## But hereвҖҷs the catch
> 
> If itвҖҷs generated randomly, 6 hex is **probably fine**, but not collision-proof forever across a huge archive.
> 
> For your use case, thatвҖҷs okay **if**:
> 
> - the real canonical ID lives in metadata/database
> - the filename token is just a short display/search key
> - collisions are checked before finalizing
> 
> So the rule should be:
> 
> > short visible code for humans, full canonical ID for system truth
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the move.
> 
> ---
> 
> # My recommendation
> 
> ## Visible suffix format
> Use:
> 
> ```text
> _QXXXXXX
> ```
> 
> Where `XXXXXX` is uppercase hex.
> 
> Examples:
> - `_Q1A2B3C`
> - `_QF0E91D`
> 
> ThatвҖҷs clean and visually scannable.
> 
> ## Why leading `Q`
> Good:
> - easy to spot
> - easy to grep/search
> - visually distinguishes from years and account numbers
> - matches your branding
> 
> ## Why uppercase
> Better visual separation.
> `Qa91ef` looks mushy.
> `QA91EF` is cleaner.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Best filename pattern
> 
> Do **not** put it at the front.
> 
> Put it at the end before extension:
> 
> ```text
> {clean_base_name}_{short_id}.{ext}
> ```
> 
> Example:
> 
> ```text
> W2_2025_Blanca_Valverde_QA19F3C.pdf
> IRS_notice_CP14_Q7C22AE.pdf
> Rays_Construction_Bank_Statement_2026_03_Q4D88B1.pdf
> ```
> 
> That keeps alphabetical grouping useful while still making the ID visible.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What not to do
> 
> Do not rely on the short hex as:
> - the only ID
> - the database primary key
> - the sole dedupe key
> - proof of uniqueness without checking
> 
> That would be sloppy.
> 
> Also do not make filenames like:
> 
> ```text
> QA19F3C_W2_2025_Blanca_Valverde_final_FINAL2.pdf
> ```
> 
> Ugly and less sortable.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Even better: pair it with hash/manifest
> 
> For every ingested file, store:
> 
> - `file_uid` = full canonical ID
> - `short_code` = `QA19F3C`
> - `sha256`
> - `original_filename`
> - `normalized_filename`
> - `ingested_at`
> - `source_path`
> - `client_id`
> - `document_type`
> 
> Then the filename suffix is just the visible layer.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Suggested ingestion filename policy
> 
> ## During intake
> Keep original in metadata, normalize filename, append short code:
> 
> ```text
> {slugified_base}_{short_code}.{ext}
> ```
> 
> ## Example transform
> Original:
> ```text
> Scan 0034.pdf
> ```
> 
> Normalized:
> ```text
> scan_0034_QC42A91.pdf
> ```
> 
> Original:
> ```text
> IMG_9921.JPG
> ```
> 
> Normalized:
> ```text
> img_9921_Q8F1D2B.jpg
> ```
> 
> Original:
> ```text
> 2025 W2 Blanca Valverde.pdf
> ```
> 
> Normalized:
> ```text
> 2025_w2_blanca_valverde_QA73C4E.pdf
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # Collision strategy
> 
> Simple rule:
> 
> 1. generate `Q` + 6 hex
> 2. check registry / manifest
> 3. if exists, regenerate
> 4. assign
> 5. store permanently
> 
> ThatвҖҷs enough.
> 
> If you want extra safety later, move to 7 or 8 hex, but honestly 6 is fine for the visible token.
> 
> ## Capacity math
> - 6 hex = 16.7 million
> - 7 hex = 268 million
> - 8 hex = 4.29 billion
> 
> For readability, IвҖҷd start with **6** unless you expect absurd scale.
> 
> ---
> 
> # My call
> 
> ## Yes, do it
> Use:
> ```text
> _QXXXXXX
> ```
> 
> ## But do it as:
> - **short visible suffix**
> - backed by a **real canonical ID** in the system
> 
> That gives you:
> - human readability
> - easy searchability
> - cleaner filenames
> - low clutter
> - safe architecture
> 
> ## For the ingestion flow
> The filename step should happen here:
> 
> ### In pipeline order
> 1. detect file
> 2. hash file
> 3. create canonical file record
> 4. generate short visible code
> 5. normalize base filename
> 6. append suffix
> 7. extract/classify/index
> 8. route/review/store
> 
> So the short code is assigned **early**, before downstream references start.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Naming rule IвҖҷd use
> 
> ```text
> normalized_base + "_" + short_code + extension
> ```
> 
> Where:
> - `normalized_base` = lowercase, underscores, cleaned
> - `short_code` = `Q` + 6 uppercase hex
> - extension preserved lowercase
> 
> Example final:
> ```text
> builtbyrays_2025_tax_return_QA3F91C.pdf
> ```
> 
> ThatвҖҷs clean.
> 
> Next thing we should lock is the actual ingestion record schema: what fields get written the moment a file hits the pipeline.

### Assistant
> Exactly. The short code alone is not enough. The filename needs to reflect **tenant/domain + human token + archive coherence**.
> 
> ## Updated naming logic
> 
> For anything client- or domain-bound, the filename should carry:
> 
> 1. **domain/client prefix**  
>    Example: `BBR4821`
> 2. **clean descriptive base**
> 3. **short visible archive token**
> 4. extension
> 
> So the pattern becomes:
> 
> ```text
> {prefix}_{normalized_base}_{short_code}.{ext}
> ```
> 
> Example:
> 
> ```text
> BBR4821_2025_tax_return_QA3F91C.pdf
> BBR4821_bank_statement_2026_03_Q7C22AE.pdf
> BBR4821_notice_of_deficiency_QF09B77.pdf
> ```
> 
> ThatвҖҷs way better because:
> - human can instantly see ownership/domain
> - search by `BBR4821` pulls the whole universe
> - short code still gives file-level uniqueness
> - QiArchive can index coherently
> 
> ## The hierarchy of identity
> 
> You now basically have **three layers**:
> 
> ### 1. Domain identity
> This is the client/system namespace.
> 
> Examples:
> - `BBR4821`
> - later maybe `QIH####`, `CASE####`, whatever your rules are
> 
> This groups all related records.
> 
> ### 2. File identity
> This is the short visible code.
> 
> Example:
> - `QA3F91C`
> 
> This identifies the individual file in a human-friendly way.
> 
> ### 3. Canonical system identity
> This lives in QiArchive metadata/DB.
> 
> Examples:
> - UUID / ULID / registry ID / file record ID
> 
> This is the real truth underneath.
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the right stack.
> 
> ---
> 
> # How QiArchive fits now
> 
> Since `qid` rolled into `qiarchive`, then QiArchive should become the **system of record for ingested files**, not just a passive storage concept.
> 
> Meaning QiArchive owns:
> 
> - canonical file record
> - checksum
> - short visible code
> - prefix/domain mapping
> - original filename
> - normalized filename
> - source path
> - current storage path
> - ingestion timestamp
> - routing status
> - extraction/classification metadata
> - lineage/history
> 
> So in the workflow:
> 
> ## New ingestion flow
> 
> ### 1. File detected
> From inbox/upload/sync/manual drop
> 
> ### 2. Resolve domain prefix
> Example:
> - BuiltByRays вҶ’ `BBR4821`
> 
> ### 3. Create QiArchive record
> Generate/store:
> - canonical archive ID
> - domain prefix
> - short visible code
> - sha256
> - original filename
> 
> ### 4. Normalize final filename
> Example:
> ```text
> BBR4821_2025_w2_blanca_valverde_QA73C4E.pdf
> ```
> 
> ### 5. Save manifest + move to processing
> QiArchive now knows this file exists before downstream AI touches it.
> 
> ### 6. Extract / classify / route
> Then your file pipeline continues.
> 
> That is the coherent way to roll QiArchive into it.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The rule you want
> 
> ## QiArchive should be the contract
> 
> Every ingested file should get a record like:
> 
> ```json
> {
>   "archive_id": "qar_xxxxxxxx",
>   "domain_prefix": "BBR4821",
>   "short_code": "QA73C4E",
>   "sha256": "вҖҰ",
>   "original_filename": "2025 W2 Blanca Valverde.pdf",
>   "normalized_filename": "BBR4821_2025_w2_blanca_valverde_QA73C4E.pdf",
>   "extension": ".pdf",
>   "source": "local_inbox",
>   "status": "ingested"
> }
> ```
> 
> That makes QiArchive the spine.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Best filename policy now
> 
> Use this:
> 
> ```text
> {domain_prefix}_{normalized_base}_{short_code}.{ext}
> ```
> 
> ## Optional future variant
> If you want even tighter coherence later:
> 
> ```text
> {domain_prefix}_{docclass}_{normalized_base}_{short_code}.{ext}
> ```
> 
> Example:
> 
> ```text
> BBR4821_tax_2025_w2_blanca_valverde_QA73C4E.pdf
> ```
> 
> But I would not force docclass into v1 unless classification is very reliable. Otherwise youвҖҷll create ugly wrong names.
> 
> So for now:
> 
> ```text
> BBR4821_2025_w2_blanca_valverde_QA73C4E.pdf
> ```
> 
> is the sweet spot.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Prefix resolution needs its own registry
> 
> You need a small registry table or config for domain prefixes.
> 
> Example fields:
> 
> - `domain_prefix`
> - `entity_type`
> - `entity_id`
> - `client_name`
> - `business_name`
> - `active`
> - `notes`
> 
> Example:
> 
> | domain_prefix | entity_type | entity_id | client_name | business_name |
> |---|---|---:|---|---|
> | BBR4821 | client | 123 | Blanca Valverde | Rays Construction LLC dba BuiltByRays |
> 
> That way the pipeline can resolve the prefix cleanly instead of guessing.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Concrete policy decision
> 
> For your repo/workflow, IвҖҷd lock this:
> 
> ## QiArchive responsibilities
> - assign canonical archive record
> - assign short file token
> - enforce filename normalization
> - store lineage/history
> - track moves/renames
> - expose lookup by prefix and short code
> 
> ## File pipeline responsibilities
> - detect file
> - call QiArchive registration
> - extract text
> - classify
> - suggest route
> - update QiArchive record
> 
> So:
> - **QiArchive = identity + registry**
> - **file pipeline = processing + routing**
> 
> That split is coherent.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What not to do
> 
> Do not let:
> - filename generation happen in five places
> - prefix logic live in random scripts
> - short code generation happen outside QiArchive registration
> - routing rename files independently of archive identity
> 
> ThatвҖҷs how drift starts again.
> 
> One file, one registration point, one naming contract.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Recommended record fields at ingest
> 
> Minimum fields:
> 
> - `archive_id`
> - `domain_prefix`
> - `short_code`
> - `sha256`
> - `original_filename`
> - `normalized_filename`
> - `extension`
> - `source_type`
> - `source_path`
> - `processing_status`
> - `created_at`
> - `client_id` or `domain_entity_id`
> 
> Then later add:
> - `mime_type`
> - `doc_class`
> - `tax_year`
> - `date_on_document`
> - `entities_json`
> - `suggested_route`
> - `final_route`
> 
> ---
> 
> # Bottom line
> 
> Yes:
> - client/domain prefix belongs in the filename
> - QiArchive should be folded into the workflow now
> - QiArchive should become the registration spine before AI processing
> - filename format should be:
> 
> ```text
> {domain_prefix}_{normalized_base}_{short_code}.{ext}
> ```
> 
> Example:
> 
> ```text
> BBR4821_2025_tax_return_QA3F91C.pdf
> ```
> 
> That is coherent, searchable, human-readable, and scalable.
> 
> The next thing to lock is the **exact ingest contract**: what function gets called first, what it returns, and what metadata file or DB row gets created before extraction starts.

> Your target stack has six major moving parts:

> If you try to stand up all six together, youвҖҷll build a pretty mess and still not have a usable system.
> 
> # Where to begin
> 
> Begin with this:
> 
> ## Phase 1 вҖ” Canonical ingest spine
> This is the first thing to lock.
> 
> You need one workflow that can do this reliably:
>

> 
> # The actual build order
> 
> ## Step 1 вҖ” Finalize the ingest contract
> Before code, define the exact record created when a file enters the pipeline.
> 
> This is the foundation.
> 
> ### Minimum ingest contract
> Every ingested file should produce:
> 
> - `archive_id`
> - `domain_prefix`
> - `short_code`
> - `original_filename`
> - `normalized_filename`
> - `sha256`
> - `mime_type`
> - `source_type`
> - `source_path`
> - `storage_path`
> - `status`
> - `created_at`
> - `extracted_text`
> - `embedding_status`
> - `graph_status`
> - `client_id` or `domain_entity_id`
> 
> That contract has to be stable before anything else.
> 
> If this is fuzzy, the whole system stays fuzzy.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 2 вҖ” Pick one canonical pipeline path
> Do not start with email, Drive sync, portal uploads, scans, and manual import all at once.
> 
> Pick one.
> 
> Best choice:
> **local watched inbox folder**
> 
> Why:
> - easiest to control
> - easiest to debug
> - no auth chaos
> - no connector chaos
> - fastest feedback loop
> 
> So your first real pipeline is:
> 
> ```text
> local inbox folder
> вҶ’ python_local watcher
> вҶ’ QiArchive registration
> вҶ’ text extraction
> вҶ’ local embedding
> вҶ’ cloud DB write
> вҶ’ cloud vector upsert
> вҶ’ searchable in UI
> ```
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the first end-to-end loop.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 3 вҖ” Lock the storage roles
> You need each layer to have one job.
> 
> ### Local processing
> Owns:
> - watcher
> - OCR
> - extraction
> - chunking
> - local embeddings
> - file manifests
> - temporary processing state
> 
> ### Supabase / Noco / cloud DB
> Owns:
> - canonical metadata
> - archive registry
> - file records
> - chunk records
> - status tracking
> - review queue
> - relations you need immediately
> 
> ### Vector DB on cloud
> Owns:
> - chunk embeddings
> - semantic search retrieval
> 
> ### Neo4j on cloud
> Owns:
> - derived relationships
> - entity graph
> - cross-object links
> - lineage graph if needed later
> 
> ### Cloud chatbot
> Owns:
> - retrieval orchestration
> - query planning
> - answer composition
> - memory interfaces
> - tool calling
> 
> That separation matters.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The biggest strategic choice you need to make now
> 
> ## NocoDB or Supabase as the canonical system of record?
> 
> You said вҖңcloud capture using Noco database / Supabase.вҖқ
> 
> Do **not** let both become equal truth sources. ThatвҖҷs stupid and will rot fast.
> 
> Pick one canonical source of truth.
> 
> ## My recommendation
> Use **Supabase as the canonical system of record**.
> 
> Use NocoDB only if:
> - you want spreadsheet-style ops views
> - manual admin interfaces
> - temporary business-facing CRUD views
> 
> But the system truth should live in **Supabase**.
> 
> Why:
> - better fit for auth
> - better fit for app integration
> - better fit for vector workflows
> - better fit for structured app backend
> - easier to keep coherent with QiOne
> 
> So:
> 
> - **Supabase = truth**
> - **NocoDB = optional operational surface**, if used at all
> 
> That needs to be decided now.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What the first MVP should do
> 
> Your first MVP is **not** вҖңtrue second brain.вҖқ
> 
> Your first MVP is:
> 
> ## MVP-0: Searchable registered archive
> A file goes in, and then you can:
> - find it by prefix
> - find it by filename
> - find it by semantic search
> - inspect extracted text
> - inspect metadata
> - see archive ID and short code
> 
> If you cannot do that cleanly, the chatbot will just be a hallucination layer on top of chaos.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Recommended first architecture slice
> 
> ## Local
> `python_local/file_pipeline`
> 
> Responsibilities:
> - watch inbox
> - register file
> - extract text
> - chunk text
> - embed locally
> - push metadata + chunks + vectors upstream
> 
> ## Cloud DB
> Supabase tables:
> - `archive_files`
> - `archive_chunks`
> - `archive_entities`
> - `archive_routes`
> - `prefix_registry`
> - `ingest_jobs`
> 
> ## Vector store
> You have two sane options:
> - pgvector in Supabase
> - separate vector DB
> 
> ### My blunt recommendation
> Start with **pgvector in Supabase** unless you have a real reason not to.
> 
> Why:
> - fewer systems
> - simpler auth
> - simpler sync
> - simpler schema
> - faster MVP
> 
> A separate vector DB is only worth it if:
> - scale demands it
> - performance proves it
> - you need specific features
> 
> Right now you need coherence more than elegance.
> 
> So I would **not** add a separate vector DB first unless you already committed hard to one.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Neo4j: when does it enter?
> 
> Not first.
> 
> Neo4j should come **after** you have:
> - stable file records
> - stable extracted chunks
> - stable entities
> - stable prefix/domain model
> - stable retrieval
> 
> Because otherwise the graph will just mirror unstable garbage.
> 
> ## Bring Neo4j in at Phase 3
> Use it for:
> - entity relationships
> - people вҶ” cases вҶ” files вҶ” companies вҶ” tasks
> - lineage and dependency mapping
> - вҖңshow me what connects to thisвҖқ
> - graph-assisted recall
> 
> But do **not** block ingestion on graph.
> 
> Graph should be **derived**, not foundational, at first.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Chatbot: when does it enter?
> 
> After semantic retrieval works.
> 
> The cloud chatbot should not be built first.
> It should be layered on top of:
> 
> 1. archive registry
> 2. extracted chunks
> 3. vector retrieval
> 4. optional graph augmentation
> 
> Then the chatbot becomes useful instead of decorative.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The actual sequence IвҖҷd use
> 
> ## Phase 1 вҖ” Ingest and register
> Deliverables:
> - prefix registry
> - QiArchive file registration
> - normalized filenames
> - local watcher
> - extraction pipeline
> - cloud DB write
> 
> ## Phase 2 вҖ” Retrieval
> Deliverables:
> - local chunking
> - local embeddings
> - vector upsert
> - semantic search endpoint
> - search UI in QiOne
> 
> ## Phase 3 вҖ” Intelligence structure
> Deliverables:
> - entity extraction
> - structured metadata
> - graph projection into Neo4j
> - relationship queries
> 
> ## Phase 4 вҖ” Second brain interface
> Deliverables:
> - chatbot retrieval orchestration
> - hybrid retrieval: metadata + vector + graph
> - memory / recall workflows
> - answer provenance
> - action hooks
> 
> That is the sane order.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Your true first task
> 
> If weвҖҷre being real, the first task is not code.
> 
> ItвҖҷs this:
>

> 1. **Supabase is canonical truth source**
> 2. **Local inbox folder is the first ingest path**
> 3. **QiArchive is the registration spine**
> 4. **Local embeddings, cloud pgvector first**
> 5. **Neo4j is phase 3, not phase 1**

> ---
>

> 
> ThatвҖҷs the beginning.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Simplified answer
> 
> Where do we begin?
> 
> **Begin with one controlled local inbox pipeline feeding one canonical cloud database.**
> 
> Not with graph.
> Not with chatbot.
> Not with multi-source ingestion.
> Not with a dozen connectors.

>

> 
> # The real moving parts
> 
> ## 1. Local ingest worker
> Runs on your machine. Watches inbox, hashes files, registers them, extracts text, chunks, embeds, pushes upstream.
> 
> **Home:**
> `python_local/file_pipeline/`
> 
> ## 2. Local API
> Optional but smart. Gives your local machine a stable interface for status, manual re-run, health, and intake commands.
> 
> **Home:**
> `python_local/api/`
> 
> ## 3. Cloud metadata + retrieval DB
> Stores file records, chunk records, statuses, prefix registry, and vectors.
> 
> **Home:**
> `supabase/` + app DB access layer
> 
> ## 4. Cloud graph projector
> Takes stable metadata/entities and projects relationships into Neo4j.
> 
> **Home:**
> `workers/graph_projector/` or similar
> 
> ## 5. Cloud chatbot / recall service
> Queries metadata + vector + graph and answers with provenance.
> 
> **Home:**
> `apps/qione` API routes or a worker-backed service
> 
> ---
> 
> # What the codebase should look like
> 
> ## Local side
> ```text
> python_local/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ api/
> в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ main.py
> в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ routes_health.py
> в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ routes_ingest.py

>

> 
> ---
> 
> # What each program actually does
> 
> ## Program 1: `watcher.py`
> Watches a folder and emits jobs.
> 
> ```python
> from pathlib import Path
> from time import sleep
> from watchdog.observers import Observer
> from watchdog.events import FileSystemEventHandler
> from .ingest import ingest_file
> 
> class InboxHandler(FileSystemEventHandler):
>     def on_created(self, event):
>         if event.is_directory:
>             return
>         path = Path(event.src_path)
>         if path.suffix.lower() in {".pdf", ".png", ".jpg", ".jpeg", ".docx"}:
>             ingest_file(path)
> 
> def run_watcher(inbox_path: str) -> None:
>     observer = Observer()
>     observer.schedule(InboxHandler(), inbox_path, recursive=False)
>     observer.start()
>     try:
>         while True:
>             sleep(1)
>     finally:
>         observer.stop()
>         observer.join()
> ```
> 
> ThatвҖҷs it. Boring is good.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Program 2: `ingest.py`
> This is the pipeline entrypoint.
> 
> ```python
> from pathlib import Path
> from .archive import register_file
> from .extract import extract_text
> from .chunk import chunk_text
> from .embed import embed_chunks
> from .api_client import upsert_archive_record, upsert_chunk_records
> 
> def ingest_file(path: Path) -> dict:
>     archive_record = register_file(path)
> 
>     extracted = extract_text(path)
>     chunks = chunk_text(extracted["text"])
>     vectors = embed_chunks([c["text"] for c in chunks])
> 
>     upsert_archive_record({
>         **archive_record,
>         "extracted_text": extracted["text"],
>         "status": "embedded",
>     })
> 
>     chunk_records = []
>     for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
>         chunk_records.append({
>             "archive_id": archive_record["archive_id"],
>             "chunk_index": i,
>             "text": chunk["text"],
>             "embedding": vectors[i],
>         })
> 
>     upsert_chunk_records(chunk_records)
>     return {"ok": True, "archive_id": archive_record["archive_id"]}
> ```
> 
> This is the core flow in plain sight:
> - register
> - extract
> - chunk
> - embed
> - push
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Program 3: `archive.py`
> This is where QiArchive registration logic lives locally before cloud persistence.
> 
> ```python
> import hashlib
> import secrets
> from pathlib import Path
> from .models import normalize_filename
> 
> def sha256_file(path: Path) -> str:
>     h = hashlib.sha256()
>     with path.open("rb") as f:
>         for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(8192), b""):
>             h.update(chunk)
>     return h.hexdigest()
> 
> def generate_short_code() -> str:
>     return "Q" + secrets.token_hex(3).upper()
> 
> def register_file(path: Path) -> dict:
>     domain_prefix = resolve_prefix(path)
>     short_code = generate_short_code()
>     checksum = sha256_file(path)
>     normalized_filename = normalize_filename(
>         domain_prefix=domain_prefix,
>         original_filename=path.name,
>         short_code=short_code,
>     )
> 
>     return {
>         "archive_id": f"qar_{checksum[:16]}",
>         "domain_prefix": domain_prefix,
>         "short_code": short_code,
>         "original_filename": path.name,
>         "normalized_filename": normalized_filename,
>         "sha256": checksum,
>         "source_path": str(path),
>         "status": "registered",
>     }
> 
> def resolve_prefix(path: Path) -> str:
>     # placeholder logic
>     return "BBR4821" if "rays" in path.name.lower() else "UNSORTED"
> ```
> 
> This is where your filename contract starts getting real.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Program 4: `extract.py`
> Text extraction layer.
> 
> ```python
> from pathlib import Path
> from pypdf import PdfReader
> 
> def extract_text(path: Path) -> dict:
>     if path.suffix.lower() == ".pdf":
>         reader = PdfReader(str(path))
>         text = "\n".join(page.extract_text() or "" for page in reader.pages)
>         return {"text": text, "method": "pypdf"}
> 
>     return {"text": "", "method": "unsupported"}
> ```
> 
> Later you add OCR fallback.
> Not first.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Program 5: `chunk.py`
> Simple chunker first. Fancy later.
> 
> ```python
> def chunk_text(text: str, chunk_size: int = 1200, overlap: int = 150) -> list[dict]:
>     chunks = []
>     start = 0
>     while start < len(text):
>         end = min(len(text), start + chunk_size)
>         chunks.append({"text": text[start:end]})
>         if end == len(text):
>             break
>         start = end - overlap
>     return chunks
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Program 6: `embed.py`
> Local embeddings.
> 
> ```python
> from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
> 
> _model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5")
> 
> def embed_chunks(texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:
>     return _model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True).tolist()
> ```
> 
> That is a perfectly sane starting point.
> 
> If you use Ollama embeddings instead, this file changes, not the whole architecture.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Program 7: `api_client.py`
> Pushes to cloud.
> 
> ```python
> import os
> import httpx
> 
> API_BASE = os.getenv("QI_CLOUD_API_BASE", "http://localhost:3000/api")
> API_KEY = os.getenv("QI_SERVICE_KEY", "")
> 
> def upsert_archive_record(record: dict) -> None:
>     with httpx.Client(timeout=30) as client:
>         client.post(
>             f"{API_BASE}/archive/register",
>             json=record,
>             headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}"},
>         ).raise_for_status()
> 
> def upsert_chunk_records(records: list[dict]) -> None:
>     with httpx.Client(timeout=60) as client:
>         client.post(
>             f"{API_BASE}/archive/chunks",
>             json={"records": records},
>             headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}"},
>         ).raise_for_status()
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # What the cloud API looks like
> 
> If you keep this inside `apps/qione`, it looks something like this.
> 
> ## `apps/qione/src/app/api/archive/register/route.ts`
> 
> ```typescript
> import { NextRequest, NextResponse } from "next/server";
> import { db } from "@/lib/db/server";
> 
> export async function POST(req: NextRequest) {
>   const body = await req.json();
> 
>   await db.from("archive_files").upsert({
>     archive_id: body.archive_id,
>     domain_prefix: body.domain_prefix,
>     short_code: body.short_code,
>     original_filename: body.original_filename,
>     normalized_filename: body.normalized_filename,
>     sha256: body.sha256,
>     source_path: body.source_path,
>     status: body.status,
>     extracted_text: body.extracted_text ?? null,
>   });
> 
>   return NextResponse.json({ ok: true });
> }
> ```
> 
> ## `apps/qione/src/app/api/archive/chunks/route.ts`
> 
> ```typescript
> import { NextRequest, NextResponse } from "next/server";
> import { db } from "@/lib/db/server";
> 
> export async function POST(req: NextRequest) {
>   const body = await req.json();
> 
>   const rows = body.records.map((r: any) => ({
>     archive_id: r.archive_id,
>     chunk_index: r.chunk_index,
>     text: r.text,
>     embedding: r.embedding,
>   }));
> 
>   await db.from("archive_chunks").insert(rows);
>   return NextResponse.json({ ok: true, inserted: rows.length });
> }
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # What the database looks like
> 
> ## `archive_files`
> 
> ```sql
> create table if not exists prefix_registry (
>   domain_prefix text primary key,
>   entity_type text not null,
>   entity_id text not null,
>   display_name text not null,
>   is_active boolean default true,
>   created_at timestamptz default now()
> );
> 
> create table if not exists archive_files (
>   archive_id text primary key,
>   domain_prefix text references prefix_registry(domain_prefix),
>   short_code text not null,
>   original_filename text not null,
>   normalized_filename text not null,
>   sha256 text not null unique,
>   mime_type text,
>   source_type text,
>   source_path text,
>   storage_path text,
>   status text not null default 'registered',
>   extracted_text text,
>   created_at timestamptz default now(),
>   updated_at timestamptz default now()
> );
> ```
> 
> ## `archive_chunks`
> 
> If using pgvector:
> 
> ```sql
> create extension if not exists vector;
> 
> create table if not exists archive_chunks (
>   chunk_id bigserial primary key,
>   archive_id text not null references archive_files(archive_id) on delete cascade,
>   chunk_index int not null,
>   text text not null,
>   embedding vector(384),
>   created_at timestamptz default now()
> );
> 
> create index if not exists archive_chunks_archive_id_idx
>   on archive_chunks (archive_id);
> ```
> 
> If your embedding model outputs 768 dims, use `vector(768)`.
> DonвҖҷt guess. Match the model.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What graph code looks like
> 
> Not first, but this is the shape.
> 
> ## `workers/graph_projector/src/worker.ts`
> 
> ```typescript
> type ArchiveEntity = {
>   archive_id: string;
>   entity_type: string;
>   entity_value: string;
> };
> 
> export async function projectEntitiesToGraph(entities: ArchiveEntity[]) {
>   for (const entity of entities) {
>     // pseudo-code
>     // MERGE (:Document {archive_id: ...})
>     // MERGE (:Entity {type: ..., value: ...})
>     // MERGE (:Document)-[:MENTIONS]->(:Entity)
>   }
> }
> ```
> 
> Neo4j should consume already-stable entities.
> It should not invent raw archive identity.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What chatbot retrieval looks like
> 
> Again, not first, but this is the real shape.
> 
> ## retrieval flow
> 1. query metadata filters
> 2. vector search over chunks
> 3. optional graph expansion
> 4. assemble evidence
> 5. answer with cited archive references
> 
> ## pseudo-code
> ```typescript
> async function retrieveForChat(query: string) {
>   const vectorHits = await searchArchiveChunks(query);
>   const archiveIds = [...new Set(vectorHits.map(hit => hit.archive_id))];
>   const files = await getArchiveFilesByIds(archiveIds);
>   const graph = await getRelatedGraphNodes(archiveIds);
> 
>   return {
>     files,
>     chunks: vectorHits,
>     graph,
>   };
> }
> ```
> 
> That is the second brain backbone:
> - metadata
> - vectors
> - graph
> - provenance
> 
> Not vibes. Records.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What programs you actually run day to day
> 
> ## Local machine
> You will likely run:
> 
> ### watcher
> ```bash
> python -m python_local.file_pipeline.watcher
> ```
> 
> ### local API
> ```bash
> uvicorn python_local.api.main:app --reload --port 8090
> ```
> 
> ### one-shot ingest test
> ```bash
> python -m python_local.file_pipeline.ingest "C:/QiData/inbox/test.pdf"
> ```
> 
> ## Cloud
> ### app
> ```bash
> pnpm dev
> ```
> 
> ### workers
> ```bash
> pnpm --filter graph_projector dev
> ```
> 
> ### database migrations
> ```bash
> supabase db push
> ```
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the operational picture.
>

> 
> # What this looks like as a first end-to-end MVP
> 
> ## Drop a file:
> `C:/QiData/inbox/2025 W2 Blanca Valverde.pdf`
> 
> ## Local worker does:
> - detect file
> - resolve prefix = `BBR4821`
> - generate short code = `QA73C4E`
> - rename logically to:
>   `BBR4821_2025_w2_blanca_valverde_QA73C4E.pdf`
> - register archive record
> - extract text
> - chunk text
> - embed chunks
> - POST file + chunk records to cloud
> 
> ## Cloud now has:
> - one `archive_files` row
> - many `archive_chunks` rows
> - searchable vectors
> - stable archive identity
> - future-ready graph projection target
> 
> ThatвҖҷs what вҖңactual code/programsвҖқ looks like.
> 
> ---

> # My blunt recommendation
> 
> For now, build only these first:
> 
> ## local
> - `watcher.py`
> - `ingest.py`
> - `archive.py`
> - `extract.py`
> - `chunk.py`
> - `embed.py`
> - `api_client.py`
> 
> ## cloud
> - `archive/register` API
> - `archive/chunks` API
> - `archive_files` table
> - `archive_chunks` table
> - `prefix_registry` table
> 
> That is enough to produce a real Spine-backed RAG foundation.
> 
> The next useful move is for me to turn this into a **copy-paste starter scaffold** for:
> - `python_local/file_pipeline`
> - `python_local/api`
> - and the first Supabase migration files.

### User

Also you have an error we decided we would use postgres, redis, neo4j, in railway via the api or do you think supabase is the better way, i honestly like railway for this becuase of the nocodb admin portal is intuitive and just works.

### Assistant
> Yep вҖ” put it in the document, but **not as one giant dump**. Split it where it actually belongs.
> 
> Also, youвҖҷre right: I drifted back toward Supabase too hard. Given **your actual preference and workflow**, I think **Railway is the better primary backend path right now**.
> 
> ## My recommendation on backend truth
> 
> Use this stack:
> 
> - **Postgres on Railway** = canonical metadata/system-of-record
> - **Redis on Railway** = queues, job state, cache, ephemeral memory
> - **Neo4j on Railway** = knowledge graph
> - **NocoDB on Railway** = admin/ops surface over Postgres
> - **Local embeddings** = generated on your machine
> - **Vector storage** = Postgres + pgvector on Railway
> - **QiOne app / API** = talks to Railway services
> 
> That is cleaner for *you* than Supabase right now.
> 
> ### Why Railway wins here
> - you already like it
> - NocoDB over Railway Postgres gives you the intuitive admin surface you want
> - Redis + Postgres + Neo4j in one hosting mindset is coherent
> - less вҖңplatform opinionationвҖқ than Supabase
> - better fit for custom API-first architecture
> 
> ### When Supabase would be better
> Supabase is better if you want:
> - built-in auth
> - instant CRUD-ish APIs
> - storage/auth/realtime bundled
> - less custom backend work
> 
> But for **QiOS as a true custom system**, Railway + Postgres/Redis/Neo4j is a stronger fit if youвҖҷre okay owning more of the backend yourself.
> 
> ## So correct the blueprint like this
> 
> ### Replace these assumptions
> Anywhere the document currently implies:
> - вҖңSupabase is canonicalвҖқ
> - вҖңSupabase with pgvector firstвҖқ
> - вҖңsupabase/ is the main backend centerвҖқ
> 
> Change that to:
> 
> - **Railway-hosted Postgres is the canonical system of record**
> - **Redis handles queueing/state/caching**
> - **Neo4j handles graph relationships**
> - **NocoDB is the admin/ops interface over Postgres**
> - **Supabase remains optional/integrational, not foundational**
>

> ## Put this in **17.5 Cloud Database and Service Setup**
> Use this structure:
> 
> ### 17.5 Cloud Database and Service Setup
> - Railway Postgres = canonical truth
> - Railway Redis = queue/cache/state
> - Railway Neo4j = graph
> - NocoDB = admin layer over Postgres
> - app/API connects to these services
> - Postgres schemas/tables:
>   - `prefix_registry`

> ---
> 
> ## Put this in **17.6 Embedding and Vector Setup**
> Use this structure:
> 
> ### 17.6 Embedding and Vector Setup
> - embeddings generated locally
> - vectors stored in Railway Postgres via pgvector
> - link all vectors to `archive_id` + `chunk_id`
> - do not use Neo4j as vector store
> - do not make NocoDB the vector/query layer
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Put this in **17.7 Knowledge Graph Setup**
> Use this structure:
> 
> ### 17.7 Knowledge Graph Setup
> - Neo4j is downstream and derived
> - graph nodes come from stable archive/entity records
> - graph edges are projections, not truth
> - graph supports relationship exploration and recall augmentation
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Put this in **17.8 Chatbot / RAG / Memory Setup**
> Use this structure:
> 
> ### 17.8 Chatbot / RAG / Memory Setup

> - chatbot queries pgvector chunk matches
> - chatbot optionally expands through Neo4j relationships
> - memory is not random chat sludge; memory should be structured and provenance-backed
> - every answer should be traceable to archive/chunk/entity records
