### Assistant
> Hell yes. HereвҖҷs the **clean 7-section QiOS Master Blueprint** in blueprint-ready form, based on the decisions we clarified after the canvas draft.
> 
> ---
> 
> # QiOS Master Blueprint
> 
> > **IвҖҷm not building another app. IвҖҷm building an operating system for memory, structure, continuity, and action.**  
> > QiOS is the canonical architecture for turning fragmented files, notes, workflows, and systems into one coherent ecosystem.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 1. Genesis & Constitution
> 
> ### 1.1 What QiOS Is
> QiOS is a **cognitive-operational system**. It is the architectural model behind QiOne and the related modules that make up the ecosystem. It exists to unify identity, memory, workflow, retrieval, routing, and modular application logic across personal, business, legal, client, and knowledge domains.
> 
> QiOS is not just a dashboard, database, or app suite. It is a system for:
> - organizing reality
> - assigning stable identity
> - preserving lineage
> - coordinating action
> - supporting modular growth without losing coherence
> 
> ### 1.2 Why QiOS Exists
> The real problem is not вҖңbuild an app.вҖқ The real problem is fragmentation:
> - files live in too many places
> - systems do not share identity
> - workflows get duplicated across tools
> - search is disconnected from storage
> - naming is inconsistent
> - metadata is incomplete
> - AI becomes shallow because the underlying records are unstable
> 
> QiOS exists to solve that fragmentation with one coherent operating model.
> 
> ### 1.3 Core Goal
> The goal of QiOS is to create a system where:
> - every meaningful object has stable identity
> - every file can be traced
> - metadata accumulates instead of being lost
> - retrieval is trustworthy
> - automation happens on top of structure, not chaos
> - local and cloud services cooperate instead of competing
> 
> ### 1.4 Founding Principles
> 1. **One canonical identity per object**
> 2. **One clear home per concern**
> 3. **Metadata before automation**
> 4. **Lineage must survive rename, move, and transformation**
> 5. **Short human-readable names with full machine identity underneath**
> 6. **Local-first where useful; cloud-backed where strategic**
> 7. **Modules should compose, not collide**
> 8. **Graph and AI layers are downstream consumers, not truth-makers**
> 9. **If it matters repeatedly, it must become canonical**
> 10. **The system must stay understandable**
> 
> ### 1.5 Non-Negotiable Rules
> - No important file enters the system without registration.
> - No downstream layer gets to redefine identity.
> - No logic should be silently duplicated across scripts, apps, and workers.
> - No advanced automation should happen before minimum metadata exists.
> - No chatbot or graph layer should become a shadow source of truth.
> 
> ### 1.6 System Family
> - **QiOS** = philosophy, operating model, governing architecture
> - **QiOne** = primary implementation platform and monorepo
> - **QiArchive** = file registration and archival identity spine
> - **QiNode** = structural and semantic intelligence layer
> - **QiNote** = local-first note and knowledge layer
> - **QiPortals / QiSuite / related surfaces** = delivery layers built on the model
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 2. Ontology & Structure
> 
> ### 2.1 What This Section Covers
> This section defines what exists in the system and how it is structured.
> 
> ### 2.2 Core Object Families
> The system should support, at minimum, these object families:
> - files
> - chunks
> - notes
> - documents
> - entities
> - clients
> - contacts
> - cases
> - tasks
> - events
> - contracts
> - prompts
> - memories
> - routes
> - projects
> - modules
> - tenants or domains
> 
> ### 2.3 Metadata Philosophy
> Metadata is not decoration. Metadata is the minimum structure required for the system to understand what something is, where it belongs, how it relates, and whether it can be trusted.
> 
> Metadata should be:
> - incremental
> - auditable
> - structured
> - attached to canonical identity
> - preserved through movement and transformation
> 
> ### 2.4 Minimum Metadata Classes
> Every important object should eventually carry some combination of:
> 
> #### Identity metadata
> - canonical ID
> - domain prefix
> - short visible code
> - checksum or content fingerprint
> 
> #### Source metadata
> - source type
> - source path
> - origin event
> - original filename
> - imported by
> - ingest timestamp
> 
> #### Structural metadata
> - MIME type
> - extension
> - chunk count
> - page count
> - parser method
> - extraction method
> 
> #### Semantic metadata
> - document type
> - inferred entities
> - tax year
> - matter or case association
> - note type
> - tags
> - confidence values
> 
> #### Lifecycle metadata
> - status
> - review state
> - route state
> - current storage path
> - created_at
> - updated_at
> 
> ### 2.5 Schema Philosophy
> The schema must reflect the fact that the system is:
> - modular
> - identity-driven
> - archive-aware
> - retrieval-oriented
> - graph-projectable
> 
> The schema should be relational at the core, with vector and graph layers derived from stable canonical records.
> 
> ### 2.6 Structural Layers
> The ontology should support these structural layers:
> - **archive layer** for files and records
> - **semantic layer** for entities and enriched meaning
> - **workflow layer** for routing, review, and actions
> - **knowledge layer** for notes, memory, and recall
> - **graph layer** for derived relationships
> - **delivery layer** for portals, reports, and chatbot output
> 
> ### 2.7 Domain and Namespace Model
> Objects should exist within a domain, namespace, or ownership context.
> 
> Examples:
> - client domain
> - case domain
> - tenant domain
> - internal ops domain
> - public content domain
> 
> A namespace must be machine-usable and human-recognizable.
> 
> ### 2.8 Prefix Registry
> A canonical prefix registry should map:
> - domain prefix
> - entity type
> - entity ID
> - display name
> - status
> - notes
> 
> Example:
> - `BBR4821` вҶ’ BuiltByRays
> 
> ### 2.9 Core Early Tables
> At minimum, the first coherent implementation should define:
> - `prefix_registry`
> - `archive_files`
> - `archive_chunks`
> - `archive_entities`
> - `archive_routes`
> - `ingest_jobs`
> - `memory_records` later
> - `graph_projection_jobs` later
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 3. Identity & Naming
> 
> ### 3.1 Identity Philosophy
> Identity is the heart of the system. If identity is unstable, everything downstream becomes suspect.
> 
> Identity must survive:
> - renaming
> - moving
> - reclassification
> - export/import
> - derived transformation
> - user-facing label changes
> 
> ### 3.2 Identity Layers
> The system should use **layered identity**, not one overloaded ID for everything.
> 
> #### Canonical system identity
> The machine-truth record key. Examples:
> - `archive_id`
> - object UUID or ULID
> - internal registry key
> 
> #### Domain or namespace identity
> The grouping prefix that tells you what world the record belongs to.
> Example:
> - `BBR4821`
> 
> #### Human-visible file token
> A short readable suffix for filenames and search.
> Recommended current pattern:
> - `Q` + 6 uppercase hexadecimal characters  
> Example:
> - `QA3F91C`
> 
> ### 3.3 Naming Contract
> The canonical filename format for ingested files should be:
> 
> ```text
> {domain_prefix}_{normalized_base}_{short_code}.{ext}
> ```
> 
> Example:
> 
> ```text
> BBR4821_2025_tax_return_QA3F91C.pdf
> ```
> 
> ### 3.4 Naming Rules
> - use lowercase normalized base
> - preserve extension in lowercase
> - include domain prefix where known
> - include short visible code before extension
> - keep filenames readable
> - avoid stuffing unstable metadata into filenames
> 
> ### 3.5 Identity Responsibilities
> #### QiArchive owns:
> - archive registration
> - canonical file record
> - short visible code assignment
> - normalized filename contract
> - checksum linkage
> - lineage continuity
> 
> #### Prefix registry owns:
> - namespace mapping
> - ownership lookup
> - entity association
> 
> ### 3.6 Chunk and Derived Identity
> Derived records must not invent their own disconnected truth.
> Examples:
> - chunks must reference `archive_id`
> - graph nodes should reference canonical object IDs
> - chatbot evidence must reference archive/chunk/entity IDs
> - memories should reference source provenance where possible
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 4. Spine & Runtime
> 
> ### 4.1 What the Spine Is
> The Spine is the canonical identity-and-flow backbone of QiOS. It is the contract that determines how information enters the system, becomes registered, accumulates metadata, becomes retrievable, and remains coherent across layers.
> 
> The Spine is not one program. It is the cross-layer operational contract.
> 
> ### 4.2 Spine Objectives
> The Spine must ensure that every important object can:
> - be uniquely identified
> - be placed in the correct namespace
> - be registered before advanced processing
> - preserve lineage through change
> - become searchable and retrievable
> - support graph projection and AI recall without drift
> 
> ### 4.3 Spine Components
> The Spine includes:
> - prefix/domain registry
> - QiArchive registration
> - canonical identity
> - human-visible identity
> - naming contract
> - metadata contract
> - lineage tracking
> - retrieval readiness
> 
> ### 4.4 Spine Rules
> 1. One canonical registration point per ingested object
> 2. One canonical system identity per object
> 3. One namespace resolution path at intake
> 4. One naming contract for normalized filenames
> 5. Metadata must exist before advanced automation
> 6. Search and AI consume Spine-backed records only
> 7. Derived layers must not redefine truth
> 
> ### 4.5 Runtime Stack
> This is the corrected stack based on your actual direction:
> 
> #### Canonical backend stack
> - **Postgres on Railway** = canonical metadata and system of record
> - **Redis on Railway** = queues, cache, ephemeral state, job orchestration support
> - **Neo4j on Railway** = derived knowledge graph
> - **NocoDB on Railway** = human-friendly admin and ops surface over Postgres
> - **pgvector in Postgres** = vector retrieval layer
> - **local Python services** = ingestion, extraction, chunking, local embeddings, machine-side processing
> 
> ### 4.6 Why Railway-First
> Railway fits this architecture better because:
> - you want custom backend control
> - Postgres/Redis/Neo4j belong in one coherent operational environment
> - NocoDB gives you a usable admin portal without fighting the system
> - QiOS is becoming a custom architecture, not a canned backend app
> 
> ### 4.7 What Lives Where
> #### Local runtime
> - watcher
> - ingest pipeline
> - OCR
> - extraction
> - chunking
> - local embeddings
> - local API
> - machine-local state
> 
> #### Cloud runtime
> - Postgres metadata truth
> - Redis queues/state/cache
> - Neo4j graph
> - admin through NocoDB
> - app-facing APIs
> - retrieval orchestration
> - review/search/chat surfaces
> 
> ### 4.8 API Categories
> - **local service APIs** for machine-local control
> - **app APIs** for frontend interaction
> - **integration APIs** for outside systems
> - **worker APIs/events** for async continuation
> 
> ### 4.9 Subprocess Categories
> - OCR subprocess
> - extraction subprocess
> - embedding subprocess
> - file scanner
> - sync subprocess
> - graph projection job
> - retrieval orchestration job
> 
> Each subprocess must have:
> - defined input
> - defined output
> - clear failure handling
> - reference to canonical IDs
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 5. Flows & Pipelines
> 
> ### 5.1 Ingestion Flow
> Canonical ingestion flow:
> 
> ```text
> source
> вҶ’ detect
> вҶ’ resolve domain
> вҶ’ register in QiArchive
> вҶ’ assign canonical identity
> вҶ’ assign short visible code
> вҶ’ normalize filename
> вҶ’ extract / inspect
> вҶ’ enrich metadata
> вҶ’ chunk
> вҶ’ embed
> вҶ’ index
> вҶ’ route / review / act
> ```
> 
> ### 5.2 Supported Input Paths
> Initial supported sources:
> - watched local inbox
> - manual import
> - synced storage
> - app upload
> - future email/connector intake
> 
> ### 5.3 File Registration Flow
> At minimum:
> 1. detect file
> 2. calculate checksum
> 3. resolve prefix/domain
> 4. create archive record
> 5. assign short code
> 6. normalize filename
> 7. persist record
> 
> ### 5.4 Extraction Flow
> 1. inspect MIME/extension
> 2. choose parser
> 3. extract text
> 4. OCR if needed
> 5. store extraction method
> 6. store raw extracted text
> 7. flag failures if extraction is weak
> 
> ### 5.5 Chunking and Embedding Flow
> 1. take extracted text
> 2. chunk deterministically
> 3. assign chunk indices
> 4. embed locally
> 5. push vectors to Postgres/pgvector
> 6. preserve linkage to `archive_id`
> 
> ### 5.6 Routing and Review Flow
> 1. infer doc type/entities
> 2. suggest route
> 3. assign confidence
> 4. mark review state
> 5. confirm or override route
> 6. finalize placement
> 7. update archive record
> 
> ### 5.7 Graph Projection Flow
> Graph is downstream and derived.
> 
> 1. start from stable archive/entity records
> 2. project nodes into Neo4j
> 3. project relationships
> 4. preserve source references
> 5. never let graph become canonical truth
> 
> ### 5.8 Chatbot / RAG / Memory Flow
> 1. query metadata filters in Postgres
> 2. run semantic retrieval in pgvector
> 3. optionally expand context through Neo4j
> 4. assemble evidence
> 5. answer with provenance-aware references
> 
> ### 5.9 Pipeline States
> Likely states:
> - detected
> - registered
> - normalized
> - extracted
> - enriched
> - chunked
> - embedded
> - indexed
> - review_pending
> - routed
> - finalized
> - failed
> 
> ### 5.10 Failure and Retry Philosophy
> Failures must be:
> - visible
> - stateful
> - retryable
> - tied to canonical IDs
> 
> Do not silently drop files, overwrite state, or advance objects with weak provenance.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 6. Build Surfaces
> 
> ### 6.1 Root Repo Model
> Canonical top-level structure:
> 
> ```text
> /
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ apps/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ sites/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ python_local/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ python_core/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ scripts/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ workers/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ packages/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ docs/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ supabase/   # transitional/integrational only if retained
> ```
> 
> ### 6.2 Local vs Cloud Build Boundary
> #### Local builds
> - `python_local`
> - `python_core`
> - machine-local OCR/model/runtime setup
> - hot file processing
> - embeddings
> - developer utilities
> 
> #### Cloud builds
> - apps
> - workers
> - Railway services
> - NocoDB
> - retrieval APIs
> - graph projection jobs
> 
> ### 6.3 python_local
> This is the machine-local runtime layer.
> 
> Recommended shape:
> 
> ```text
> python_local/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ api/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ file_pipeline/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ services/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ jobs/
> в””в”Җв”Җ config/
> ```
> 
> ### 6.4 python_core
> This is the reusable Python toolbox.
> 
> Use for:
> - extractors
> - processors
> - shared helpers
> - tool abstractions
> - reusable utilities
> 
> ### 6.5 apps
> This is where user-facing app surfaces live:
> - review UI
> - search UI
> - archive UI
> - chatbot UI
> - admin controls
> 
> ### 6.6 workers
> This is where async continuation lives:
> - graph projection
> - retrieval orchestration
> - repair/retry jobs
> - metadata enrichment
> - background processing
> 
> ### 6.7 Local Runtime Setup
> Local machine should provide:
> - Python 3.11 or 3.12
> - virtual environment
> - OCR tooling
> - local model runner or embedding model setup
> - local hot storage path
> - machine-local environment variables
> 
> Recommended machine-local working directory:
> 
> ```text
> C:/QiData/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ inbox/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ processing/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ reviewed/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ failed/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ manifests/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ extracted_text/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ embeddings_cache/
> в”ңв”Җв”Җ logs/
> в””в”Җв”Җ model_cache/
> ```
> 
> ### 6.8 First Working Milestone
> The first meaningful milestone is not вҖңtrue second brain.вҖқ
> It is:
> 
> ```text
> Local Inbox вҶ’ QiArchive Registration вҶ’ Extraction вҶ’ Local Embedding вҶ’ Railway Postgres/pgvector вҶ’ App Search/View
> ```
> 
> That proves the Spine is alive.
> 
> ### 6.9 Immediate Build Checklist
> - create `python_local/file_pipeline/`
> - create watched inbox
> - define `prefix_registry`
> - define `archive_files`
> - define `archive_chunks`
> - define state model
> - implement checksum + registration
> - implement normalization
> - implement extraction
> - implement local embedding
> - implement Postgres/pgvector upsert
> - implement search/review surface
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 7. Governance & Working Memory
> 
> ### 7.1 Purpose
> This section holds the systemвҖҷs active operational memory:
> - decisions
> - open questions
> - next actions
> - known constraints
> - implementation notes
> - unresolved drift
> 
> ### 7.2 What Belongs Here
> - ADRs
> - summaries
> - working notes
> - open design questions
> - TODO
> - milestone logs
> - assumptions
> - risk notes
> - known debt
> - current confirmed points
> 
> ### 7.3 Current Confirmed Points
> - root should include: `apps`, `sites`, `python_local`, `python_core`, `scripts`, `workers`, `packages`, `docs`, and likely a transitional `supabase` area only if still needed
> - BuiltByRays domain prefix is `BBR4821`
> - qid has rolled into QiArchive
> - QiArchive is the registration spine for ingested files
> - short visible file suffix should be `Q` + 6 uppercase hex
> - filename format is:
>   - `{domain_prefix}_{normalized_base}_{short_code}.{ext}`
> - canonical backend stack is:
>   - Railway Postgres
>   - Railway Redis
>   - Railway Neo4j
>   - Railway NocoDB
>   - local embeddings
>   - pgvector in Postgres
> 
> ### 7.4 Immediate Next Decisions
> 1. lock QiArchive ingest record schema
> 2. lock prefix registry schema
> 3. lock `python_local/file_pipeline` folder structure
> 4. lock local API endpoints
> 5. lock review state machine
> 6. lock Postgres table definitions
> 7. lock storage path rules
> 8. lock minimum metadata required before AI classification
> 9. lock graph projection schema
> 10. lock chatbot retrieval contract
> 
> ### 7.5 Open Design Areas
> - exact archive ingest contract
> - final local API shape
> - final memory schema
> - entity extraction strategy
> - route confidence model
> - exact graph node/edge model
> - what becomes durable memory vs ephemeral retrieval context
> - how QiNote and QiNode divide responsibility at maturity
> 
> ### 7.6 Drafting Rule
> This blueprint is living architecture, not frozen scripture.  
> But every change should make the system **more coherent, not more clever**.
>

> ## The biggest issue: the sources still contradict themselves
> This is the one that actually matters.
> 
> Some parts of the project sources still say the canonical backend is **Supabase**:
> - вҖңSupabase PostgresвҖқ as canonical metadata/system of record
> - Supabase-native migrations as structural truth
> - Supabase Auth as identity layer
> - app APIs reading/writing from Supabase
> - runtime env vars centered on `SUPABASE_*` оҲҖfileciteоҲӮturn2file1оҲҒ

> But newer parts of the same source now say the canonical runtime is **Railway-first**:
> - Railway Postgres
> - Railway Redis
> - Railway Neo4j
> - Railway NocoDB
> - local embeddings
> - pgvector in Postgres оҲҖfileciteоҲӮturn1file5оҲӮturn1file9оҲҒ

> 
> ### What to change
> You need a **formal ADR** that locks one of these two positions:
> 
> 1. **Supabase is canonical**, Railway is non-canonical / legacy / implementation-specific  
> or  
> 2. **Railway is canonical**, Supabase is transitional / optional / only for specific services
> 
> Right now the blueprint is trying to hold both at once. That is not doctrine; that is drift.
>

> - pgvector = derived retrieval layer
> - Neo4j = derived relationship layer
> - NocoDB = operator/admin surface only
> - chatbot = retrieval consumer, never truth source

> Ran command: `Get-ChildItem -Path "c:\QiLabs\_QiOne_MonoRepo_v0.4\__QiOS_Master_Blueprint_v0.4\docs" -Recurse -File | Select-Object FullName`

> 
> ### 1. Summary of Changes
> 
> The QiOS Master Blueprint has been updated to fully digest email as a canonical integration. Email is not modeled as an isolated island (a new schema), but mapped explicitly to existing domain boundaries: integration settings go to `qione`, individual payloads and threads register physically in `qiarchive`, events/jobs track their state in `qisys`, context gets projected to `qially` and robust files vault in `qivault`. Every derived email component respects strict provenance and tenant isolation. 
> 
> ### 2. File-by-File Patch Plan
> 
> The following blueprint documents have been patched to solidify these rules:
> 
> * **`03_structure/object_model.md`**: Expanded the `Core Object Families` table to codify where email primitives (accounts, messages, cursors) map to schemas.
> * **`03_structure/placement_rules.md`**: Implemented strict domain-specific guardrails explicitly prohibiting a new `qiemail` schema and enforcing pipeline ownership.
> * **`04_data/schema.md`**: Added a dedicated `Email Intake Schema Contract` subsystem that maps out the boundaries across five canonical schemas.
> * **`04_data/storage.md`**: Established canonical email storage strategies linking back to explicit `archive_id` metadata.
> * **`05_compute/integrations.md`**: Added a thorough integration and intake pipeline flow enforcing sequential processing over direct insertion.
> * **`01_governance/decisions.md`**: Appended key unresolved topics into the "Open Decisions" block (e.g. normalization, promotion mechanisms, cursor structures) so implementation doesn't stall.
> 
> ***
> 
> ### 3. Exact Markdown Sections Inserted (By Section)
> 
> *(These have been directly patched into your v0.4 blueprint repo)*
> 
> #### `03_structure/object_model.md`
> ```markdown
> | email_accounts | `qione` | Email platform configuration and ownership |
> | email_messages | `qiarchive` | Canonical registered email records |
> | email_threads | `qiarchive` | Grouped email conversations |
> | email_participants | `qiarchive` | Canonical sender/recipient records |
> | email_attachments| `qiarchive` | Files attached to registered emails |
> | email_sync_jobs | `qisys` | Connector sync processing |
> | email_connector_events | `qisys` | Integration ingress events |
> | email_sync_cursors | `qisys` | Provider sync state tracking |
> ```
> 
> #### `03_structure/placement_rules.md`
> ```markdown
> ## Domain-Specific Guardrails
> 
> * **Email**: Do not create a top-level `qiemail` schema unless a future ADR explicitly approves it. Email must be modeled as an integration path into the canonical Spine, relying on `qione` for configuration, `qiarchive` for content, `qisys` for sync state, and `qially` for semantic memory. Durable, secure email attachments may be promoted to `qivault`.
> ```
> 
> #### `04_data/schema.md`
> ```markdown
> ## Email Intake Schema Contract
> 
> Email is modeled as an integration feeding the master archival spine, avoiding a separate schema. It is bounded by these core table definitions:
> 
> * `qione.email_accounts` вҖ” tenant-scoped integration ownership and IMAP/Gmail/connector configuration.
> * `qiarchive.archive_messages` вҖ” canonical registration for individual email payloads.
> * `qiarchive.archive_message_threads` вҖ” canonical grouping of related messages.
> * `qiarchive.archive_message_participants` вҖ” sender/recipient edge metadata.
> * `qiarchive.archive_message_attachments` вҖ” canonical attachment metadata. Email attachments processed as robust files must receive an `archive_id`.
> * `qisys.email_sync_cursors` вҖ” connector synchronization state, jobs, and webhook checkpoints.
> * `qially` tracking вҖ” provenance-aware memory and embeddings for email-derived knowledge chunks must link back to their canonical archive IDs.
> ```
> 
> #### `04_data/storage.md`
> ```markdown
> ## Email Intake Storage Rules
> 
> * Raw message payloads and attachments land in connector/object storage or a local staging inbox.
> * They are **not canonical** until registered in `qiarchive`.
> * Canonical storage path metadata must live completely on archive-linked records.
> * Email attachments that become canonical files receive `archive_id` lineage.
> * Promoted secure attachments may be represented as `qivault.documents` while tracking their initial archive lineage.
> 
> **Canonical Path Convention (Example Concept)**
> tenant/{tenant_id}/email/{account_id}/{archive_id}/raw.eml
> tenant/{tenant_id}/email/{account_id}/{archive_id}/attachments/{attachment_archive_id}_{filename}
> ```
> 
> #### `05_compute/integrations.md`
> ```markdown
> ## Email Pipeline Flow Contract
> 
> Email intake is considered a complex integration that must strictly obey the canonical registration pipeline:
> 
> * **Source**: Gmail / Outlook / IMAP / webhook providers
> * **Entry**: Integration API or background connector worker
> * **Flow**:
>   1. `Connector Pull or Webhook`
>   2. `вҶ’ qisys Job / Event`
>   3. `вҶ’ qiarchive Registration`
>   4. `вҶ’ Extraction & Attachments`
>   5. `вҶ’ Chunking & Embedding`
>   6. `вҶ’ Routing/Review/Action`
> * **Worker Rule**: All asynchronous worker inputs and outputs must pass and reference canonical IDs.
> * **Failure Rule**: Failures must be visible, stateful, and cleanly retryable via `qisys`.
> * **Provenance Rule**: The integration must NEVER bypass explicit `qiarchive` registration.
> * **Isolation Rule**: Every derived email record must preserve `tenant_id` and obey Row-Level Security.
> ```
> 
> ### 4. Architect Consistency Check
> 
> This proposed update respects the constitutional bounds of the QiOS Master Blueprint because it enforces:
> 
> 1. **The Single Domain Rule**: Instead of creating a Frankenstein schema where synced data handles its own jobs and AI logic, email functionality is parsed based on "concern." Jobs go to `qisys`, objects go to `qiarchive`, and smart graphs go to `qially`.
> 2. **The Band Model Alignment**: Authentication properties stay in the Platform tier (`qione`), whereas storage properties remain securely inside the Canonical tier (`qiarchive`).
> 3. **Archive-First Registration**: External intake via IMAP or Webhooks never writes straight into operational databases; they wait sequentially as raw bits until they get legally ingested and stamped with `archive_id` IDs. 
> 4. **Downstream-Derived Constraints**: Features like Vector recall, chat contexts, or chunked logic rely upon `archive` records exclusively. They don't generate separate "truth."
> 5. **Tenant Isolation Model**: All cross-boundary tables mapped in the `Email Intake Schema Contract` demand compliance with standard RLS checks relying on `tenant_id` mappings.
> 
> ### 5. Rejected Alternatives
> 
> - **Why not `qiemail` right now?**
>   Introducing it risks creating parallel pipelines that duplicate `qiarchive` mechanics (chunking, embedding, extraction, searching). Until a specific domain requires non-standard routing completely untethered from general storage, standard pipelines should serve it. 
> - **Why not store email directly inside `qially`?**
>   AI and Vector elements are by definition derived and ephemeral. Placing raw email payloads in an AI layer violates the "derived layers are never truth sources" mandate and creates huge security risks during retrieval iterations.
> - **Why not let integrations write directly to tables?**
>   This completely removes the chronological registration audit. If webhooks map directly into `qicase` databases, they avoid semantic extraction tools, pipeline sanitization, and lineage documentation (who and when added it to the workspace). Integrity and pipeline flows are prioritized over speed.

> 
> ### 1. Summary of Changes
> 
> The QiOS Master Blueprint has been updated to fully digest email as a canonical integration. Email is not modeled as an isolated island (a new schema), but mapped explicitly to existing domain boundaries: integration settings go to `qione`, individual payloads and threads register physically in `qiarchive`, events/jobs track their state in `qisys`, context gets projected to `qially` and robust files vault in `qivault`. Every derived email component respects strict provenance and tenant isolation. 
> 
> ### 2. File-by-File Patch Plan
> 
> The following blueprint documents have been patched to solidify these rules:
> 
> * **`03_structure/object_model.md`**: Expanded the `Core Object Families` table to codify where email primitives (accounts, messages, cursors) map to schemas.
> * **`03_structure/placement_rules.md`**: Implemented strict domain-specific guardrails explicitly prohibiting a new `qiemail` schema and enforcing pipeline ownership.
> * **`04_data/schema.md`**: Added a dedicated `Email Intake Schema Contract` subsystem that maps out the boundaries across five canonical schemas.
> * **`04_data/storage.md`**: Established canonical email storage strategies linking back to explicit `archive_id` metadata.
> * **`05_compute/integrations.md`**: Added a thorough integration and intake pipeline flow enforcing sequential processing over direct insertion.
> * **`01_governance/decisions.md`**: Appended key unresolved topics into the "Open Decisions" block (e.g. normalization, promotion mechanisms, cursor structures) so implementation doesn't stall.
> 
> ***
> 
> ### 3. Exact Markdown Sections Inserted (By Section)
> 
> *(These have been directly patched into your v0.4 blueprint repo)*
> 
> #### `03_structure/object_model.md`
> ```markdown
> | email_accounts | `qione` | Email platform configuration and ownership |
> | email_messages | `qiarchive` | Canonical registered email records |
> | email_threads | `qiarchive` | Grouped email conversations |
> | email_participants | `qiarchive` | Canonical sender/recipient records |
> | email_attachments| `qiarchive` | Files attached to registered emails |
> | email_sync_jobs | `qisys` | Connector sync processing |
> | email_connector_events | `qisys` | Integration ingress events |
> | email_sync_cursors | `qisys` | Provider sync state tracking |
> ```
> 
> #### `03_structure/placement_rules.md`
> ```markdown
> ## Domain-Specific Guardrails
> 
> * **Email**: Do not create a top-level `qiemail` schema unless a future ADR explicitly approves it. Email must be modeled as an integration path into the canonical Spine, relying on `qione` for configuration, `qiarchive` for content, `qisys` for sync state, and `qially` for semantic memory. Durable, secure email attachments may be promoted to `qivault`.
> ```
> 
> #### `04_data/schema.md`
> ```markdown
> ## Email Intake Schema Contract
> 
> Email is modeled as an integration feeding the master archival spine, avoiding a separate schema. It is bounded by these core table definitions:
> 
> * `qione.email_accounts` вҖ” tenant-scoped integration ownership and IMAP/Gmail/connector configuration.
> * `qiarchive.archive_messages` вҖ” canonical registration for individual email payloads.
> * `qiarchive.archive_message_threads` вҖ” canonical grouping of related messages.
> * `qiarchive.archive_message_participants` вҖ” sender/recipient edge metadata.
> * `qiarchive.archive_message_attachments` вҖ” canonical attachment metadata. Email attachments processed as robust files must receive an `archive_id`.
> * `qisys.email_sync_cursors` вҖ” connector synchronization state, jobs, and webhook checkpoints.
> * `qially` tracking вҖ” provenance-aware memory and embeddings for email-derived knowledge chunks must link back to their canonical archive IDs.
> ```
> 
> #### `04_data/storage.md`
> ```markdown
> ## Email Intake Storage Rules
> 
> * Raw message payloads and attachments land in connector/object storage or a local staging inbox.
> * They are **not canonical** until registered in `qiarchive`.
> * Canonical storage path metadata must live completely on archive-linked records.
> * Email attachments that become canonical files receive `archive_id` lineage.
> * Promoted secure attachments may be represented as `qivault.documents` while tracking their initial archive lineage.
> 
> **Canonical Path Convention (Example Concept)**
> tenant/{tenant_id}/email/{account_id}/{archive_id}/raw.eml
> tenant/{tenant_id}/email/{account_id}/{archive_id}/attachments/{attachment_archive_id}_{filename}
> ```
> 
> #### `05_compute/integrations.md`
> ```markdown
> ## Email Pipeline Flow Contract
> 
> Email intake is considered a complex integration that must strictly obey the canonical registration pipeline:
> 
> * **Source**: Gmail / Outlook / IMAP / webhook providers
> * **Entry**: Integration API or background connector worker
> * **Flow**:
>   1. `Connector Pull or Webhook`
>   2. `вҶ’ qisys Job / Event`
>   3. `вҶ’ qiarchive Registration`
>   4. `вҶ’ Extraction & Attachments`
>   5. `вҶ’ Chunking & Embedding`
>   6. `вҶ’ Routing/Review/Action`
> * **Worker Rule**: All asynchronous worker inputs and outputs must pass and reference canonical IDs.
> * **Failure Rule**: Failures must be visible, stateful, and cleanly retryable via `qisys`.
> * **Provenance Rule**: The integration must NEVER bypass explicit `qiarchive` registration.
> * **Isolation Rule**: Every derived email record must preserve `tenant_id` and obey Row-Level Security.
> ```
> 
> ### 4. Architect Consistency Check
> 
> This proposed update respects the constitutional bounds of the QiOS Master Blueprint because it enforces:
> 
> 1. **The Single Domain Rule**: Instead of creating a Frankenstein schema where synced data handles its own jobs and AI logic, email functionality is parsed based on "concern." Jobs go to `qisys`, objects go to `qiarchive`, and smart graphs go to `qially`.
> 2. **The Band Model Alignment**: Authentication properties stay in the Platform tier (`qione`), whereas storage properties remain securely inside the Canonical tier (`qiarchive`).
> 3. **Archive-First Registration**: External intake via IMAP or Webhooks never writes straight into operational databases; they wait sequentially as raw bits until they get legally ingested and stamped with `archive_id` IDs. 
> 4. **Downstream-Derived Constraints**: Features like Vector recall, chat contexts, or chunked logic rely upon `archive` records exclusively. They don't generate separate "truth."
> 5. **Tenant Isolation Model**: All cross-boundary tables mapped in the `Email Intake Schema Contract` demand compliance with standard RLS checks relying on `tenant_id` mappings.
> 
> ### 5. Rejected Alternatives
> 
> - **Why not `qiemail` right now?**
>   Introducing it risks creating parallel pipelines that duplicate `qiarchive` mechanics (chunking, embedding, extraction, searching). Until a specific domain requires non-standard routing completely untethered from general storage, standard pipelines should serve it. 
> - **Why not store email directly inside `qially`?**
>   AI and Vector elements are by definition derived and ephemeral. Placing raw email payloads in an AI layer violates the "derived layers are never truth sources" mandate and creates huge security risks during retrieval iterations.
> - **Why not let integrations write directly to tables?**
>   This completely removes the chronological registration audit. If webhooks map directly into `qicase` databases, they avoid semantic extraction tools, pipeline sanitization, and lineage documentation Perfect вҖ” hereвҖҷs your **clean, atomic patch pack**.
> Everything you need, **no guessing, no drift**.
> 
> YouвҖҷll merge this across files manually.

> # рҹ“Ұ QIOS AGENT GOVERNANCE PATCH (COPY ALL)
> 
> ```markdown
> в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ

> 
> # Agent Governance Layer (AGL)
> Version: 1.0
> Status: Active
> Scope: All AI-assisted systems (IDE agents, copilots, automation workers, code generators)
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Purpose
> 
> This document defines the **mandatory behavioral constraints for all AI agents** operating within QiOS.
> 
> QiOS is a governed system.  
> Agents are **not allowed to act autonomously outside doctrine alignment**.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Core Principle
> 
> > Agents do not create architecture.  
> > Agents enforce architecture.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Agent Operating Modes
> 
> ### 1. Doctrine Alignment Mode (DEFAULT)
> - Load and interpret blueprint documentation
> - Validate constraints before action
> 
> ### 2. Implementation Mode
> - Execute within approved structure
> - No structural invention
> 
> ### 3. Audit Mode
> - Detect violations, drift, inconsistencies
> 
> ### 4. Escalation Mode
> - Triggered when rules are violated or unclear
> - MUST halt execution
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Mandatory Pre-Action Validation
> 
> Agents MUST determine:
> 
> - Band вҶ’ Core / Platform / Domain
> - Owning schema
> - Canonical vs Derived
> - tenant_id requirement
> - archive_id requirement
> - Spine ingestion path
> 
> If ANY are unclear:
> 
> вқ— HARD STOP вҶ’ Escalate
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Non-Negotiable System Laws
> 
> 1. 3-Band Model Integrity  
> 2. Single Domain Ownership  
> 3. No domain logic in public schema  
> 4. Tenant isolation required (tenant_id + RLS)  
> 5. Spine-first ingestion (QiArchive required)  
> 6. Derived systems cannot define truth  
> 7. No parallel systems (schemas, pipelines, logic)  
> 8. Supabase migrations = canonical truth  
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Additional Enforcement Rules (NEW)
> 
> ### Schema Drift Detection
> Agents MUST flag:
> - Tables not defined in migrations
> - Columns added outside migrations
> 
> ---
> 
> ### Naming Consistency Enforcement
> Agents MUST enforce:
> - Domain prefixes
> - ID standards (archive_id, QiDecimal, etc.)
> 
> ---
> 
> ### Pipeline Visibility Rule
> Agents MUST:
> - Trace data origin
> - Reject direct writes bypassing Spine
> 
> ---
> 
> ### Cross-Domain Boundary Protection
> Agents MUST:
> - Prevent tight coupling across domains
> - Recommend contracts/interfaces instead
> 
> ---
> 
> ### Read vs Write Authority
> Agents MUST distinguish:
> - Canonical writers
> - Derived readers
> 
> ---
> 
> ### Migration-First Rule
> Agents MUST:
> - Propose schema migrations BEFORE code
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Out-of-Bounds Protocol
> 
> When violated, agents MUST return:
> 
> ### рҹҡЁ Out-of-Bounds Alert
> 
> 1. Deviation  
> 2. Ripple Impact  
> 3. Pros & Cons  
> 4. Approval Request  
> 
> PROHIBITED:
> - Partial implementation
> - Temporary workarounds
> - Silent violations
> 
> ---
> 
> ## ADR Trigger Rule
> 
> Agents MUST recommend ADR when:
> 
> - Rules are bypassed
> - New structural patterns emerge
> - Domain ownership changes
> - Governance rules are updated
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Enforcement Priority
> 
> 1. Blueprint Doctrine  
> 2. Schema (Migrations)  
> 3. Code  
> 4. User Request  
> 
> User intent NEVER overrides system integrity.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Success Criteria
> 
> A compliant agent:
> 
> - Preserves system coherence
> - Prevents drift
> - Maintains single source of truth
> - Escalates instead of guessing
> 
> в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ

> 
> ## Agent Governance
> 
> QiOS enforces an **Agent Governance Layer (AGL)**.
> 
> All AI-assisted systems MUST comply with:
> вҶ’ `/docs/01_governance/agent_rules.md`
> 
> Agent outputs are **non-authoritative unless doctrine-aligned**.
> 
> Violations by agents are treated as:
> - Architectural drift risks
> - Governance failures
> 
> All development environments MUST include:
> - Doctrine enforcement prompts
> - Out-of-bounds escalation behavior
> 
> в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ
> FILE: /docs/01_governance/standards.md
> PATCH вҖ” ADD SECTION
> в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ
> 
> ## Agent Compliance Standard
> 
> All agents interacting with QiOS MUST:
> 
> - Perform pre-action validation
> - Respect schema ownership
> - Enforce tenant isolation
> - Follow Spine ingestion rules
> - Reject undefined domain placement
> 
> Agents are NOT permitted to:
> - Infer structure not defined in blueprint
> - Create schema without migration
> - Bypass canonical ingestion flows
> 
> в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ
> FILE: /docs/03_structure/placement_rules.md
> PATCH вҖ” ADD CLARIFICATION
> в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ
> 
> ## Agent Placement Enforcement
> 
> Agents MUST NOT assign domain ownership implicitly.
> 
> If domain placement is unclear:
> 
> вҶ’ HARD STOP

> 
> # update-adr Command
> 
> Purpose: Formalize approved architectural deviations and maintain governance integrity.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Trigger Conditions
> 
> Use when:
> 
> - A QiOS rule is bypassed
> - An Out-of-Bounds Alert is approved
> - A new structural pattern is introduced
> - Agent rules are modified
> - Schema ownership changes
> - Band boundaries shift
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 1 вҖ” Identify Deviation
> 
> Capture:
> 
> - Rule broken
> - Justification
> - Scope of impact
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 2 вҖ” Doctrine Update
> 
> Update:
> 
> - policies.md  
> - standards.md  
> - placement_rules.md  
> - agent_rules.md  
> - schema.md (if applicable)
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 3 вҖ” ADR Numbering
> 
> - Locate latest ADR
> - Increment (4-digit format)
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 4 вҖ” Load Template
> 
> Use:
> ADR-0000_template.md
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 5 вҖ” Generate ADR
> 
> Include:
> 
> - Context  
> - Decision  
> - Consequences  
> - Alternatives  
> - System impact  
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 6 вҖ” Changelog Update
> 
> Append to:
> 
> /docs/appendices/changelog.md
> 
> Include:
> - What changed  
> - Why  
> - ADR reference  
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 7 вҖ” Agent Synchronization (NEW)
> 
> Agents MUST:
> 
> - Reload governance files
> - Apply new rules immediately
> - Reject outdated patterns
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Output Requirement
> 
> Return:
> 
> - ADR file content  
> - Doctrine patches  
> - Sync confirmation  
>

> ok lets lock in supabase, 2, yes, 3, yes, 4, yes, 5, yes, and also we have another problem there is the old "blueprint" in docs and the new one is law we need to merge and align here the current full tree for your reference. # File Tree: _QiOne_MonoRepo_v0.4  ### 1. Summary of Changes The QiOS Master Blueprint has been updated to fully digest email as a canonical integration. Email is not modeled as an isolated island (a new schema), but mapped explicitly to existing domain boundaries: integration settings go to qione, individual payloads and threads register physically in qiarchive, events/jobs track their state in qisys, context gets projected to qially and robust files vault in qivault. Every derived email component respects strict provenance and tenant isolation. ### 2. File-by-File Patch Plan The following blueprint documents have been patched to solidify these rules: * **03_structure/object_model.md**: Expanded the Core Object Families table to codify where email primitives (accounts, messages, cursors) map to schemas. * **03_structure/placement_rules.md**: Implemented strict domain-specific guardrails explicitly prohibiting a new qiemail schema and enforcing pipeline ownership. * **04_data/schema.md**: Added a dedicated Email Intake Schema Contract subsystem that maps out the boundaries across five canonical schemas. * **04_data/storage.md**: Established canonical email storage strategies linking back to explicit archive_id metadata. * **05_compute/integrations.md**: Added a thorough integration and intake pipeline flow enforcing sequential processing over direct insertion. * **01_governance/decisions.md**: Appended key unresolved topics into the "Open Decisions" block (e.g. normalization, promotion mechanisms, cursor structures) so implementation doesn't stall. *** ### 3. Exact Markdown Sections Inserted (By Section) *(These have been directly patched into your v0.4 blueprint repo)* #### 03_structure/object_model.md
> markdown
> | email_accounts | `qione` | Email platform configuration and ownership |
> | email_messages | `qiarchive` | Canonical registered email records |
> | email_threads | `qiarchive` | Grouped email conversations |
> | email_participants | `qiarchive` | Canonical sender/recipient records |
> | email_attachments| `qiarchive` | Files attached to registered emails |
> | email_sync_jobs | `qisys` | Connector sync processing |
> | email_connector_events | `qisys` | Integration ingress events |
> | email_sync_cursors | `qisys` | Provider sync state tracking |
> #### 03_structure/placement_rules.md
> markdown
> ## Domain-Specific Guardrails
> 
> * **Email**: Do not create a top-level `qiemail` schema unless a future ADR explicitly approves it. Email must be modeled as an integration path into the canonical Spine, relying on `qione` for configuration, `qiarchive` for content, `qisys` for sync state, and `qially` for semantic memory. Durable, secure email attachments may be promoted to `qivault`.
> #### 04_data/schema.md
> markdown
> ## Email Intake Schema Contract
> 
> Email is modeled as an integration feeding the master archival spine, avoiding a separate schema. It is bounded by these core table definitions:
> 
> * `qione.email_accounts` вҖ” tenant-scoped integration ownership and IMAP/Gmail/connector configuration.
> * `qiarchive.archive_messages` вҖ” canonical registration for individual email payloads.
> * `qiarchive.archive_message_threads` вҖ” canonical grouping of related messages.
> * `qiarchive.archive_message_participants` вҖ” sender/recipient edge metadata.
> * `qiarchive.archive_message_attachments` вҖ” canonical attachment metadata. Email attachments processed as robust files must receive an `archive_id`.
> * `qisys.email_sync_cursors` вҖ” connector synchronization state, jobs, and webhook checkpoints.
> * `qially` tracking вҖ” provenance-aware memory and embeddings for email-derived knowledge chunks must link back to their canonical archive IDs.
> #### 04_data/storage.md
> markdown
> ## Email Intake Storage Rules
> 
> * Raw message payloads and attachments land in connector/object storage or a local staging inbox.
> * They are **not canonical** until registered in `qiarchive`.
> * Canonical storage path metadata must live completely on archive-linked records.
> * Email attachments that become canonical files receive `archive_id` lineage.
> * Promoted secure attachments may be represented as `qivault.documents` while tracking their initial archive lineage.
> 
> **Canonical Path Convention (Example Concept)**
> tenant/{tenant_id}/email/{account_id}/{archive_id}/raw.eml
> tenant/{tenant_id}/email/{account_id}/{archive_id}/attachments/{attachment_archive_id}_{filename}
> #### 05_compute/integrations.md
> markdown
> ## Email Pipeline Flow Contract
> 
> Email intake is considered a complex integration that must strictly obey the canonical registration pipeline:
> 
> * **Source**: Gmail / Outlook / IMAP / webhook providers
> * **Entry**: Integration API or background connector worker
> * **Flow**:
>   1. `Connector Pull or Webhook`
>   2. `вҶ’ qisys Job / Event`
>   3. `вҶ’ qiarchive Registration`
>   4. `вҶ’ Extraction & Attachments`
>   5. `вҶ’ Chunking & Embedding`
>   6. `вҶ’ Routing/Review/Action`
> * **Worker Rule**: All asynchronous worker inputs and outputs must pass and reference canonical IDs.
> * **Failure Rule**: Failures must be visible, stateful, and cleanly retryable via `qisys`.
> * **Provenance Rule**: The integration must NEVER bypass explicit `qiarchive` registration.
> * **Isolation Rule**: Every derived email record must preserve `tenant_id` and obey Row-Level Security.
> ### 4. Architect Consistency Check This proposed update respects the constitutional bounds of the QiOS Master Blueprint because it enforces: 1. **The Single Domain Rule**: Instead of creating a Frankenstein schema where synced data handles its own jobs and AI logic, email functionality is parsed based on "concern." Jobs go to qisys, objects go to qiarchive, and smart graphs go to qially. 2. **The Band Model Alignment**: Authentication properties stay in the Platform tier (qione), whereas storage properties remain securely inside the Canonical tier (qiarchive). 3. **Archive-First Registration**: External intake via IMAP or Webhooks never writes straight into operational databases; they wait sequentially as raw bits until they get legally ingested and stamped with archive_id IDs. 4. **Downstream-Derived Constraints**: Features like Vector recall, chat contexts, or chunked logic rely upon archive records exclusively. They don't generate separate "truth." 5. **Tenant Isolation Model**: All cross-boundary tables mapped in the Email Intake Schema Contract demand compliance with standard RLS checks relying on tenant_id mappings. ### 5. Rejected Alternatives - **Why not qiemail right now?** Introducing it risks creating parallel pipelines that duplicate qiarchive mechanics (chunking, embedding, extraction, searching). Until a specific domain requires non-standard routing completely untethered from general storage, standard pipelines should serve it. - **Why not store email directly inside qially?** AI and Vector elements are by definition derived and ephemeral. Placing raw email payloads in an AI layer violates the "derived layers are never truth sources" mandate and creates huge security risks during retrieval iterations. - **Why not let integrations write directly to tables?** This completely removes the chronological registration audit. If webhooks map directly into qicase databases, they avoid semantic extraction tools, pipeline sanitization, and lineage documentation Perfect вҖ” hereвҖҷs your **clean, atomic patch pack**. Everything you need, **no guessing, no drift**. YouвҖҷll merge this across files manually. --- # рҹ“Ұ QIOS AGENT GOVERNANCE PATCH (COPY ALL)

> 
> # Agent Governance Layer (AGL)
> Version: 1.0
> Status: Active
> Scope: All AI-assisted systems (IDE agents, copilots, automation workers, code generators)
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Purpose
> 
> This document defines the **mandatory behavioral constraints for all AI agents** operating within QiOS.
> 
> QiOS is a governed system.  
> Agents are **not allowed to act autonomously outside doctrine alignment**.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Core Principle
> 
> > Agents do not create architecture.  
> > Agents enforce architecture.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Agent Operating Modes
> 
> ### 1. Doctrine Alignment Mode (DEFAULT)
> - Load and interpret blueprint documentation
> - Validate constraints before action
> 
> ### 2. Implementation Mode
> - Execute within approved structure
> - No structural invention
> 
> ### 3. Audit Mode
> - Detect violations, drift, inconsistencies
> 
> ### 4. Escalation Mode
> - Triggered when rules are violated or unclear
> - MUST halt execution
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Mandatory Pre-Action Validation
> 
> Agents MUST determine:
> 
> - Band вҶ’ Core / Platform / Domain
> - Owning schema
> - Canonical vs Derived
> - tenant_id requirement
> - archive_id requirement
> - Spine ingestion path
> 
> If ANY are unclear:
> 
> вқ— HARD STOP вҶ’ Escalate
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Non-Negotiable System Laws
> 
> 1. 3-Band Model Integrity  
> 2. Single Domain Ownership  
> 3. No domain logic in public schema  
> 4. Tenant isolation required (tenant_id + RLS)  
> 5. Spine-first ingestion (QiArchive required)  
> 6. Derived systems cannot define truth  
> 7. No parallel systems (schemas, pipelines, logic)  
> 8. Supabase migrations = canonical truth  
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Additional Enforcement Rules (NEW)
> 
> ### Schema Drift Detection
> Agents MUST flag:
> - Tables not defined in migrations
> - Columns added outside migrations
> 
> ---
> 
> ### Naming Consistency Enforcement
> Agents MUST enforce:
> - Domain prefixes
> - ID standards (archive_id, QiDecimal, etc.)
> 
> ---
> 
> ### Pipeline Visibility Rule
> Agents MUST:
> - Trace data origin
> - Reject direct writes bypassing Spine
> 
> ---
> 
> ### Cross-Domain Boundary Protection
> Agents MUST:
> - Prevent tight coupling across domains
> - Recommend contracts/interfaces instead
> 
> ---
> 
> ### Read vs Write Authority
> Agents MUST distinguish:
> - Canonical writers
> - Derived readers
> 
> ---
> 
> ### Migration-First Rule
> Agents MUST:
> - Propose schema migrations BEFORE code
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Out-of-Bounds Protocol
> 
> When violated, agents MUST return:
> 
> ### рҹҡЁ Out-of-Bounds Alert
> 
> 1. Deviation  
> 2. Ripple Impact  
> 3. Pros & Cons  
> 4. Approval Request  
> 
> PROHIBITED:
> - Partial implementation
> - Temporary workarounds
> - Silent violations
> 
> ---
> 
> ## ADR Trigger Rule
> 
> Agents MUST recommend ADR when:
> 
> - Rules are bypassed
> - New structural patterns emerge
> - Domain ownership changes
> - Governance rules are updated
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Enforcement Priority
> 
> 1. Blueprint Doctrine  
> 2. Schema (Migrations)  
> 3. Code  
> 4. User Request  
> 
> User intent NEVER overrides system integrity.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Success Criteria
> 
> A compliant agent:
> 
> - Preserves system coherence
> - Prevents drift
> - Maintains single source of truth
> - Escalates instead of guessing
> 
> в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ

> 
> QiOS enforces an **Agent Governance Layer (AGL)**.
> 
> All AI-assisted systems MUST comply with:
> вҶ’ `/docs/01_governance/agent_rules.md`
> 
> Agent outputs are **non-authoritative unless doctrine-aligned**.
> 
> Violations by agents are treated as:
> - Architectural drift risks
> - Governance failures
> 
> All development environments MUST include:
> - Doctrine enforcement prompts
> - Out-of-bounds escalation behavior

> 
> ## Agent Compliance Standard
> 
> All agents interacting with QiOS MUST:
> 
> - Perform pre-action validation
> - Respect schema ownership
> - Enforce tenant isolation
> - Follow Spine ingestion rules
> - Reject undefined domain placement
> 
> Agents are NOT permitted to:
> - Infer structure not defined in blueprint
> - Create schema without migration
> - Bypass canonical ingestion flows
>

> 
> ## Agent Placement Enforcement
> 
> Agents MUST NOT assign domain ownership implicitly.
> 
> If domain placement is unclear:
> 
> вҶ’ HARD STOP  
> вҶ’ Escalate via Out-of-Bounds Alert  
> 
> No object may exist without a single canonical domain.
>

> 
> ## Agent Schema Enforcement
> 
> Agents MUST treat Supabase migrations as:
> 
> вҶ’ The ONLY source of schema truth
> 
> Agents MUST:
> 
> - Reject schema defined only in code
> - Flag discrepancies between docs and migrations
> - Enforce migration-first workflow
>

> # update-adr Command
> 
> Purpose: Formalize approved architectural deviations and maintain governance integrity.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Trigger Conditions
> 
> Use when:
> 
> - A QiOS rule is bypassed
> - An Out-of-Bounds Alert is approved
> - A new structural pattern is introduced
> - Agent rules are modified
> - Schema ownership changes
> - Band boundaries shift
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 1 вҖ” Identify Deviation
> 
> Capture:
> 
> - Rule broken
> - Justification
> - Scope of impact
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 2 вҖ” Doctrine Update
> 
> Update:
> 
> - policies.md  
> - standards.md  
> - placement_rules.md  
> - agent_rules.md  
> - schema.md (if applicable)
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 3 вҖ” ADR Numbering
> 
> - Locate latest ADR
> - Increment (4-digit format)
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 4 вҖ” Load Template
> 
> Use:
> ADR-0000_template.md
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 5 вҖ” Generate ADR
> 
> Include:
> 
> - Context  
> - Decision  
> - Consequences  
> - Alternatives  
> - System impact  
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 6 вҖ” Changelog Update
> 
> Append to:
> 
> /docs/appendices/changelog.md
> 
> Include:
> - What changed  
> - Why  
> - ADR reference  
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Step 7 вҖ” Agent Synchronization (NEW)
> 
> Agents MUST:
> 
> - Reload governance files
> - Apply new rules immediately
> - Reject outdated patterns
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Output Requirement
> 
> Return:
> 
> - ADR file content  
> - Doctrine patches  
> - Sync confirmation

> # What needs to change during merge
> 
> HereвҖҷs where you need to be ruthless.
> 
> ## A. Stop treating legacy file paths as architecture
> If old docs say things like:
> 
> - `/docs/03_structure/...`
> - `/docs/04_data/...`
> - `/docs/05_compute/...`
> 
> that is fine as **legacy source location**, but not as the new conceptual structure.
> 
> The **concept** gets promoted.
> The **old file path does not become law**.
> 
> So the merge should map old doctrine into the new 7 sections.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## B. Normalize terminology
> You likely have multiple vocab sets floating around:
> - QiOS
> - QiOne
> - Spine
> - Canonical
> - Archive-first
> - Band model
> - Domain ownership
> - schema truth
> - tenant isolation
> 
> You need one glossary and one preferred term set.
>

>   - вҖңSpine-first ingestionвҖқ
>   - вҖңArchive-first registrationвҖқ
>

> 
> ---
> 
> ## C. Kill duplicate governance
> You do not want:
> - governance in old docs
> - governance in agent rules
> - governance in standards
> - governance in master blueprint
> - governance in prompts
> 
> вҖҰall saying slightly different things.
> 
> That is how doctrine forks.
> 
> Instead:
> 
> ### Make this the pattern
> - **Master Blueprint** = constitutional summary
> - **Agent Governance doc** = behavioral enforcement spec
> - **ADR system** = exception process
> - **Migrations** = implementation truth for schema
>

>

> # The merge rule you need right now
> 
> Use this exact rule:
> 
> ## **Legacy docs may contribute doctrine, but never override doctrine.**
> 
> That one line will save you a lot of chaos.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Practical merge workflow
> 
> Do it in this order.
> 
> ## Phase 1 вҖ” Freeze authority
> Mark old docs as:
> - `legacy_reference`
> - `pending_reconciliation`
> - `non-authoritative unless promoted`
> 
> ## Phase 2 вҖ” Build a reconciliation table
> For every useful old section, track:
> 
> - legacy file
> - doctrine/topic
> - keep / revise / reject
> - target section in new blueprint
> - notes
> - ADR needed? yes/no
> 
> ## Phase 3 вҖ” Promote only approved content
> Do not вҖңimport documents.вҖқ
> Promote **rules** and **contracts**.
> 
> ## Phase 4 вҖ” Add conflict notes where needed
> Especially for:
> - terminology
> - folder/path assumptions
> - runtime assumptions
> - old stack assumptions
> - domain ownership wording
> 
> ## Phase 5 вҖ” Archive the old docs cleanly
> Not deleted.
> Archived.
> Something like:
> 
> - `/docs_legacy/`
> or
> - `/archive/blueprint_legacy_v0.4/`
> 
> with a header that says:
> - reference only
> - superseded by master blueprint
> - content must be reconciled before reuse
> 
> ---
>

> 
> You want **strict**, not **useless**.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The three things I would add now

> A short rule set for how legacy doctrine gets promoted into law.

> 
> ## 3. A **Doctrine Status header**
> Every blueprint doc should declare:
> - status: draft / active / legacy / superseded
> - authority level
> - last reconciled date
> - supersedes / superseded by
> 
> That kills ambiguity fast.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Bottom line

> Yes, merge them.

> - agent governance layer

> - domain ownership guardrails

> - legacy content gets promoted into that frame intentionally
> 
> The clean next move is to build a **reconciliation matrix** for the old blueprint against the new 7 sections, then turn that into the master merge pass.

> ## What this means in plain English
> 
> HereвҖҷs the clean cut:
> 
> ### Safe to promote into the official blueprint
> - repo topology doctrine
> - naming and file standards
> - RLS and role model rules
> - canonical/derived separation
> - email integration boundaries
> - identity / lineage / registration principles
> - domain/object placement rules
> - migration-first schema truth
> - source acknowledgment / failure handling rules for agents, if moved into governance docs оҲҖfileciteоҲӮturn7file0оҲӮturn7file1оҲӮturn8file1оҲӮturn8file2оҲҒ
> 
> ### Should **not** be promoted into the official blueprint
> - personal mission and goals
> - provider-specific infra runbooks
> - exact app paths and route examples
> - UI library choices
> - persona/brand narrative
> - current implementation trivia pretending to be doctrine оҲҖfileciteоҲӮturn7file0оҲӮturn7file2оҲӮturn8file0оҲӮturn8file1оҲҒ
>

> | File | Decision | Why |
> |---|---|---|

> | `governance_standard_and_data_schema.md` | **Promote heavily, after cleanup** | Best doctrine-to-noise ratio |
> | `implementation_blueprints.md` | **Do not promote as-is** | Pattern book, not constitution |

> ## The sharp truth
> 
> The two files most worth merging into the official blueprint are:
> 
> 1. **Governance, Standards & Database Schema**  
> 2. **Architecture & Infrastructure Master**
> 
> The other three should be treated as:
> - **implementation playbook**
> - **ops/personal overlay**
> - **agent/persona strategy**
> 
> If you blur those categories, you get a bloated blueprint that canвҖҷt govern anything because itвҖҷs trying to be doctrine, handbook, roadmap, autobiography, and prompt library all at once.
>

> Send me the **first section you want reviewed line-by-line for promotion** from:
> - `governance_standard_and_data_schema.md`, or
> - `architecture_and_infra_master.md`
> 
> Those are the right two to lock first.

> ### рҹ“ң Proposal 1: Governance & Security (Standards vs. Policies)
> 
> I propose adding the following segments to the official blueprint. Please review these for "Promotion to Law":
> 
> #### **Add to: `01_governance/standards.md`**
> ```markdown
> ## Repo Invariants
> 
> The following rules are machine-enforced and must be maintained across all workspace containers (`/apps`, `/packages`, `/workers`, `/python_core`):
> 
> *   **Case Consistency**: All file and directory names must be `lowercase_with_underscores`.
> *   **Path Depth**: Maximum directory depth is 3 levels from the container root (excluding node_modules).
> *   **Filename Constraint**: Filenames must not exceed 50 characters (excluding extension). 
> *   **Sequential Prefixing**: Content artifacts and decision records must use the `YYYY-MM-DD_` prefix standard.
> ```
> 
> #### **Add to: `01_governance/policies.md`**
> ```markdown
> ## Security & Data Access Policy
> 
> QiOS data access is governed by **Operational Role Isolation**:
> 
> *   **Anon Zero-Write Law**: The `anon` role (unauthenticated API access) is restricted to `SELECT` only. Under no circumstances should `anon` have `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE` permissions on any production table.
> *   **System Ingestion Role**: The `service_role` is reserved for automated workers and ingestion pipelines. It bypasses RLS and must NEVER be exposed to a client-side application.
> *   **Authenticated User Role**: The `authenticated` role is scoped by `user_id` via RLS for all application-level interactions.
> ```
>

> 3.  **Roles**: Does the **Anon Zero-Write Law** require any exceptions for "Public Feedback" forms, or should those always use a proxy worker?

> # Decision
> 
> ## `standards.md`
> **Do not promote that block exactly as written.**

> 
> # Line-by-line review
> 
> ## Proposed `standards.md` block
> 
> ### Title: `## Repo Invariants`
> **Approve.**  
> Good label. Clear. Constitutional enough.
> 
> ### `The following rules are machine-enforced and must be maintained across all workspace containers (/apps, /packages, /workers, /python_core):`
> **Revise.**
> 
> Problem:
> - вҖңmachine-enforcedвҖқ is too strong unless it is actually enforced today.
> - you excluded `/docs`, which is weird if this is repo-wide governance.
> - if some rules apply differently to docs vs code, say that explicitly.
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> The following repository invariants govern all canonical workspace containers.  
> Where automated enforcement exists, it should validate these rules rather than redefine them.
> ```
> 
> Or, if you want container specificity:
> ```markdown
> The following repository invariants apply across canonical workspace containers and documentation roots unless an approved exception is documented by ADR.
> ```
> 
> ### `Case Consistency: All file and directory names must be lowercase_with_underscores.`
> **Revise hard.**
> 
> Problem:
> - This is too broad and probably false for directories if you already have content/title-style naming in some places.
> - It also conflicts with prior reconciled text that distinguished content folders from code/logic folders. оҲҖfileciteоҲӮturn7file1оҲҒ
> - вҖңall file and directory namesвҖқ will create needless cleanup churn.
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> * **Code and Logic Naming**: Source code files, automation files, and technical directories must use `lowercase_with_underscores`.
> * **Content Naming**: Content artifacts must follow the canonical date-prefix standard where required by their domain.
> ```
> 
> If you truly want to ban CamelCase everywhere, do it knowingly, but that is a migration decision, not a casual invariant.
> 
> ### `Path Depth: Maximum directory depth is 3 levels from the container root (excluding node_modules).`
> **Reject for now.**
> 
> This is the weakest line in the whole block.
> 
> Problem:
> - Arbitrary.
> - Easy to violate for legitimate reasons.
> - Hard to enforce consistently across apps, docs, migrations, and framework conventions.
> - This is implementation friction disguised as doctrine.
> 
> Unless you already have a real measured reason, kill it.
> 
> If your real goal is вҖңavoid folder spaghetti,вҖқ say that instead:
> ```markdown
> * **Path Discipline**: Directory nesting should remain shallow and intentional. Deep nesting requires explicit justification in module or domain documentation.
> ```
> 
> That gives guidance without creating dumb violations.
> 
> ### `Filename Constraint: Filenames must not exceed 50 characters (excluding extension).`
> **Revise.**
> 
> The idea is good. The global hard cap is too brittle.
> 
> Problem:
> - Fine for content artifacts.
> - Potentially annoying for framework-specific code, migrations, generated files, or integration contracts.
> - You already asked the right question because yes, as written, it is probably too strict.
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> * **Filename Length**: Human-authored files should remain concise and normally stay within 50 characters excluding extension. Exceptions for framework, migration, or generated files must remain deliberate and readable.
> ```
> 
> If you want law, split it:
> - content/docs: hard cap 50
> - code/system files: concise by standard, not absolute cap
> 
> That is the smarter move.
> 
> ### `Sequential Prefixing: Content artifacts and decision records must use the YYYY-MM-DD_ prefix standard.`
> **Revise.**
> 
> Problem:
> - вҖңdecision recordsвҖқ usually should follow ADR numbering, not date prefix, unless your ADR system is date-prefixed.
> - this line mixes two different naming systems.
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> * **Date Prefixing**: Content artifacts that require chronological ordering must use the `YYYY-MM-DD_` prefix standard.
> * **Decision Records**: ADRs and formal decision artifacts must follow the repositoryвҖҷs approved decision-record naming standard.
> ```
> 
> That avoids stepping on ADR numbering.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Recommended replacement for `standards.md`
> 
> This is the version IвҖҷd actually promote:
> 
> ```markdown
> ## Repo Invariants
> 
> The following repository invariants apply across canonical workspace containers and documentation roots unless an approved exception is documented by ADR.
> 
> * **Code and Logic Naming**: Source code files, automation files, and technical directories must use `lowercase_with_underscores`.
> * **Filename Length**: Human-authored files should remain concise and normally stay within 50 characters excluding extension. Exceptions for framework, migration, or generated files must remain deliberate and readable.
> * **Date Prefixing**: Content artifacts that require chronological ordering must use the `YYYY-MM-DD_` prefix standard.
> * **Path Discipline**: Directory nesting should remain shallow and intentional. Deep nesting requires explicit justification in module or domain documentation.
> * **Canonical Containers**: Repository structure must respect the approved top-level workspace hierarchy and must not introduce ad hoc root-level containers without explicit approval.
> ```
> 
> That version is law-grade instead of petty-rule-grade.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Proposed `policies.md` block
> 
> ### Title: `## Security & Data Access Policy`
> **Approve.**
> 
> Good title. Clear scope.
> 
> ### `QiOS data access is governed by Operational Role Isolation:`
> **Approve with tiny edit.**
> 
> Maybe capitalize consistently only if this is a formal doctrine phrase elsewhere. Otherwise:
> 
> ```markdown
> QiOS data access is governed by role isolation and explicit execution boundaries.
> ```
> 
> Cleaner. Less buzzwordy.
> 
> ### `Anon Zero-Write Law: the anon role...`
> **Approve with one important refinement.**
> 
> This should absolutely become law.
> 
> But IвҖҷd tighten it like this:
> ```markdown
> * **Anon Zero-Write Law**: The `anon` role is read-only by default in production and must not receive direct `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE` permissions on canonical tables. Public write flows must terminate in reviewed server-side or worker-controlled ingress paths.
> ```
> 
> Why this is better:
> - вҖңread-only by default in productionвҖқ is slightly more robust
> - вҖңcanonical tablesвҖқ is more precise
> - gives you the correct exception pattern without weakening the rule
> 
> ### `System Ingestion Role: The service_role is reserved...`
> **Approve.**
> 
> Strong. Keep it.
> 
> Suggested refinement:
> ```markdown
> * **System Role Isolation**: The `service_role` is reserved for trusted backend processes, ingestion workers, and controlled automation. Because it bypasses RLS, it must never be exposed to client-side applications or public runtime contexts.
> ```
> 
> ### `Authenticated User Role: The authenticated role is scoped by user_id via RLS...`
> **Revise slightly.**
> 
> Problem:
> - not everything should be scoped purely by `user_id`; some things should be by `tenant_id`, membership, ownership, role, or explicit grant.
> - `user_id` is too narrow as constitutional language.
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> * **Authenticated Access**: The `authenticated` role must be constrained by Row Level Security using approved ownership, tenant, membership, or role-based predicates appropriate to the domain.
> ```
> 
> That is way better law.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Recommended replacement for `policies.md`
> 
> ```markdown
> ## Security & Data Access Policy
> 
> QiOS data access is governed by role isolation and explicit execution boundaries:
> 
> * **Anon Zero-Write Law**: The `anon` role is read-only by default in production and must not receive direct `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE` permissions on canonical tables. Public write flows must terminate in reviewed server-side or worker-controlled ingress paths.
> * **System Role Isolation**: The `service_role` is reserved for trusted backend processes, ingestion workers, and controlled automation. Because it bypasses RLS, it must never be exposed to client-side applications or public runtime contexts.
> * **Authenticated Access**: The `authenticated` role must be constrained by Row Level Security using approved ownership, tenant, membership, or role-based predicates appropriate to the domain.
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # Answers to your review questions
> 
> ## 1. Is the 50-character limit too strict?
> **Yes, if applied repo-wide as hard law.**
> 
> Best answer:
> - **Hard rule for human-authored content/artifact files**
> - **Soft standard for code/system/framework files**
> 
> That gets you the benefit without creating nonsense exceptions every other day.
> 
> ## 2. Should CamelCase be explicitly banned for directories?
> **For code/technical directories: yes.**  
> **For all directories everywhere: no, not unless you want a migration war.**
> 
> So the clean answer is:
> - explicitly require `lowercase_with_underscores` for code/logic/technical directories
> - do not overgeneralize unless the whole repo already follows that
> 
> ## 3. Does the Anon Zero-Write Law need exceptions for public feedback forms?
> **No direct DB exceptions.**  
> Those should go through a **proxy worker / server route / reviewed ingress path**.
> 
> That is exactly the kind of exception you do **not** want to encode as вҖңsometimes anon can write.вҖқ ThatвҖҷs how rules rot.
> 
> So:
> - `anon` stays zero-write
> - public submission flows write through controlled backend ingress
> 
> ---

> - **Failure handling / graceful insufficiency rule**
> 
> Those are good agent-law candidates. Just keep that as a separate promotion packet.

> ### рҹҸ—пёҸ Proposal 2: Architecture & Topology
> 
> Following the [implementation_plan.md](file:///C:/Users/codyr/.gemini/antigravity/brain/eb319adf-3b19-4098-b1f7-0455113e8a05/implementation_plan.md), I am now presenting the **Architecture** section for line-by-line review. 
> 
> The goal is to codify the monorepo hierarchy and the ingestion flow (including email) while stripping out implementation noise (Prisma/specific hostnames).
> 
> #### **Add to: `02_architecture/system_model.md`**
> 
> ```markdown
> ## Canonical Hierarchy (v0.4)
> 
> The QiOS monorepo follows a strict functional container model. No root-level directories are permitted outside this hierarchy without an approved ADR.
> 
> | Container | Role |
> |---|---|
> | `/apps` | Headless or user-facing applications (Next.js, Vite). |
> | `/packages` | Shared logic, UI components, and domain utilities. |
> | `/workers` | Edge intake and background ingestion pipelines (Cloudflare). |
> | `/python_core` | Isolated analysis, RAG, and AI agent logic. |
> | `/docs` | The living Master Blueprint and technical documentation. |
> 
> ## Intake & Ingestion Flow
> 
> QiOS treats external signals as non-authoritative until registered in the Spine.
> 
> ### Standard Intake Flow
> 1. **External Signal**: Webhook, Email, File upload, or API call.
> 2. **Worker Ingestion**: Edge worker validates and sanitizes the payload.
> 3. **Spine Registration**: `QiArchive` issues a canonical ID and registers the event.
> 4. **Actionable State**: The registered data is promoted to the appropriate Domain (`QiCase`, `QiFinance`, etc.).
> 
> ### Email Integration Path
> Email is modeled as an **Input Path**, not a standalone truth island.
> * **Ownership**: Email account configuration resides in `QiOne` (Platform).
> * **Registration**: Canonical message storage and threading reside in `QiArchive` (Spine).
> * **Governance**: Email processing status (`archive`, `active`, `ai-ready`) is strictly managed via standard Spine metadata.
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ### рҹ”Қ Questions for Review:
> 1. **Container Completeness**: Should `/python_local` (for dev-specific scripts) be recognized here, or should it remain a de-facto exception until promoted?
> 2. **Terminology**: Does "External Signal" sufficiently cover physical inputs (like a scan)?
> 3. **Hierarchy**: Are you comfortable locking the `/docs` container officially, even though we just reconciled the root `/docs` into this path?

> 
> Recommended:
> ```markdown
> The QiOS monorepo follows an approved functional container model. No root-level workspace directories may be introduced outside this hierarchy without an approved ADR.
> ```
> 
> Why:
> - вҖңworkspace directoriesвҖқ is more precise than just вҖңdirectoriesвҖқ
> - вҖңapproved functional container modelвҖқ sounds less theatrical and more constitutional

> ## Table rows
> 
> ### `/apps | Headless or user-facing applications (Next.js, Vite).`
> **Revise.**
> 
> Problem:
> - вҖңheadlessвҖқ can get fuzzy here
> - naming frameworks in doctrine is usually implementation smell unless they are truly constitutional
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> | `/apps` | Deployable application surfaces, including user-facing and operational applications. |
> ```
> 
> If you want framework examples, put them in a note, not the law.

> ### `/packages | Shared logic, UI components, and domain utilities.`
> **Approve with tiny refinement.**

> ```markdown
> | `/packages` | Shared libraries, UI components, contracts, and reusable domain utilities. |
> ```
> 
> вҖңcontractsвҖқ matters and should be named.
>

> **Revise.**
> 
> Problem:
> - вҖңCloudflareвҖқ is implementation-specific
> - workers may do more than ingestion
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> | `/workers` | Background workers, ingestion pipelines, and controlled system automation. |

> **Approve with revision.**

> - вҖңAI agent logicвҖқ is okay, but maybe too trendy unless this area is truly defined that way
> 
> Better:

> | `/python_core` | Isolated analysis, retrieval, enrichment, and governed AI-assisted processing. |
