> - вҖңsignalвҖқ is okay, but your question is right: it may not naturally cover physical intake
> - вҖңfile uploadвҖқ and вҖңscanвҖқ should both be represented
> - capitalize consistently
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> 1. **External Input**: Webhook, email, scan, file upload, form submission, or API call.
> ```
> 
> вҖңExternal InputвҖқ is broader and cleaner than вҖңsignal.вҖқ
> 
> ### `2. Worker Ingestion: Edge worker validates and sanitizes the payload.`
> **Revise.**
> 
> Problem:
> - too narrow because not every ingestion path is literally an edge worker
> - вҖңpayloadвҖқ may not fit scans or files cleanly
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> 2. **Ingestion Layer**: A controlled worker, service, or intake process validates, sanitizes, and normalizes the input.
> ```
> 
> This is much better law.
> 
> ### `3. Spine Registration: QiArchive issues a canonical ID and registers the event.`
> **Approve with revision.**
> 
> Problem:
> - вҖңeventвҖқ is too narrow if this can be a document, message, entity, filing, or media object
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> 3. **Spine Registration**: `QiArchive` assigns a canonical identifier and registers the input as a governed record.
> ```
> 
> That line is strong.
> 
> ### `4. Actionable State: The registered data is promoted to the appropriate Domain (QiCase, QiFinance, etc.).`
> **Revise.**
> 
> Problem:
> - вҖңpromotedвҖқ might imply every intake becomes domain data, which may not be true
> - sometimes it remains archive-only, sometimes it spawns derived actions, sometimes it links to multiple domains
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> 4. **Domain Routing**: Registered records may be routed, linked, or promoted into the appropriate domain workflows under explicit ownership rules.
> ```
> 
> That is much more accurate.
> 
> ---

> **Approve.**

> **Revise.**

> - `ai-ready` especially feels like mutable implementation language, not law
> - if status vocabulary changes later, you do not want this section aging badly

> 
> # Recommended replacement block
> 
> This is what IвҖҷd promote:
> 
> ```markdown
> ## Canonical Hierarchy
> 
> The QiOS monorepo follows an approved functional container model. No root-level workspace directories may be introduced outside this hierarchy without an approved ADR.
> 
> | Container | Role |
> |---|---|
> | `/apps` | Deployable application surfaces, including user-facing and operational applications. |
> | `/packages` | Shared libraries, UI components, contracts, and reusable domain utilities. |
> | `/workers` | Background workers, ingestion pipelines, and controlled system automation. |
> | `/python_core` | Isolated analysis, retrieval, enrichment, and governed AI-assisted processing. |
> | `/docs` | The Master Blueprint, ADRs, standards, and supporting technical documentation. |
> 
> ## Intake & Ingestion Flow
> 
> QiOS treats all inbound external inputs as non-authoritative until canonically registered in the Spine.
> 
> ### Standard Intake Flow
> 1. **External Input**: Webhook, email, scan, file upload, form submission, or API call.
> 2. **Ingestion Layer**: A controlled worker, service, or intake process validates, sanitizes, and normalizes the input.
> 3. **Spine Registration**: `QiArchive` assigns a canonical identifier and registers the input as a governed record.
> 4. **Domain Routing**: Registered records may be routed, linked, or promoted into the appropriate domain workflows under explicit ownership rules.
> 
> ### Email Integration Path
> Email is modeled as an input path, not a standalone truth island.
> 
> * **Ownership**: Email account configuration resides in `QiOne` (Platform).
> * **Registration**: Canonical message registration, content storage, and threading reside in `QiArchive` (Spine).
> * **Governance**: Email-derived records must be governed through standard Spine metadata, provenance rules, and lifecycle state controls.
> ```
> 
> That version is cleaner, less fragile, and more law-worthy.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Answers to the review questions
> 
> ## 1. Should `/python_local` be recognized here?
> **No. Not yet.**
> 
> Do **not** promote `/python_local` into canonical hierarchy unless you actually want to bless it as a first-class container.
> 
> Right now it sounds like one of these:
> - local scratch zone
> - developer convenience area
> - transitional workspace
> 
> That is not the same as constitutional architecture.
> 
> Best posture:
> - leave it de facto / non-canonical for now
> - if it keeps surviving and proving useful, promote later through ADR
> 
> Otherwise youвҖҷll bless clutter because it happened to exist.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 2. Does вҖңExternal SignalвҖқ cover physical inputs like a scan?
> **Not well enough.**
> 
> Use **External Input** instead.  
> That solves it cleanly.
> 
> Recommended intake examples:
> - webhook
> - email
> - scan
> - file upload
> - form submission
> - API call
> 
> That covers both digital and physical-origin capture.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 3. Are you comfortable locking `/docs` officially?
> **Yes. Absolutely.**
> 
> You already crossed the threshold where the blueprint itself is a governed system asset, not just incidental prose. So `/docs` should be canonical.
> 
> That is not overreach. That is cleanup.
> 
> ---
>

> - Canonical hierarchy section
> - Intake & ingestion flow
> - Email integration path
> 
> ## Do not lock as written
> - framework-specific examples in doctrine rows
> - вҖңedge workerвҖқ as the only ingestion model
> - вҖңregisters the eventвҖқ language
> - hard-coded email lifecycle labels like `archive`, `active`, `ai-ready`
> - `/python_local` as canonical
>

> ### рҹ—ғпёҸ Proposal 3: Data & Schema Invariants
> 
> Following the [implementation_plan.md](file:///C:/Users/codyr/.gemini/antigravity/brain/eb319adf-3b19-4098-b1f7-0455113e8a05/implementation_plan.md), I am now presenting the **Data & Schema** section for line-by-line review.
> 
> This proposal establishes the "QiNode" shared field standard without forcing a mega-schema, and solidifies the "Migration-as-Truth" doctrine.
> 
> #### **Add to: `04_data/schema.md`**
> 
> ```markdown
> ## The "QiNode" Object Standard
> 
> While QiOS supports modular, domain-specific schemas, all registered objects should follow a shared entity standard to ensure cross-domain portability and AI-ready retrieval.
> 
> ### Common Field Invariants
> Every canonical record in a domain schema (e.g., `qicase`, `qihome`) should ideally implement these standard fields to maintain Spine-alignment:
> 
> | Field | Type | Role |
> |---|---|---|
> | `id` | `uuid` | Canonical unique identifier (Spine-issued). |
> | `status` | `text` | Lifecycle state (e.g., `active`, `archived`). |
> | `visibility` | `text` | Access level (e.g., `private`, `internal`, `public`). |
> | `metadata` | `jsonb` | System-level provenance, lineage, and indexing keys. |
> | `attributes` | `jsonb` | Domain-specific extensible payload (e.g., amount, due_date). |
> 
> ## Schema Governance Invariants
> 
> * **Migration-as-Truth**: Supabase native migrations are the canonical authority. Prisma or other ORMs are strictly for query/client-side mapping and do not define truth.
> * **Relational Core**: The system is relational at the core. Vector embeddings and graph nodes must remain derived from, and strictly link back to, stable canonical records.
> * **Domain Isolation**: No cross-schema joins are permitted at the database level. Inter-domain relationships must be resolved via `qigraph.master_index` or the Application Layer.
> * **Audit Requirements**: Every canonical table must maintain `created_at` and `updated_at` timestamps for automated audit-trail hygiene.
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ### рҹ”Қ Questions for Review:
> 1. **Field Flexibility**: Are you comfortable with `metadata` vs `attributes` as the primary split for system-vs-domain data, or is that distinction too fine-grained for this level?
> 2. **Audit Rules**: Should "deletion" be explicitly banned in favor of `status = 'deleted'` at the constitutional level, or is that an implementation policy?
> 3. **Primary Keys**: Does the `id` field require a specific UUID version (e.g., v4 or v7) to be codified here?

### Assistant
> This one needs more surgery. The intent is right. The draft is still too soft in some places and too rigid in the wrong places.
> 
> # Decision
> 
> **Approve with revisions, not as written.**
> 
> Big issue: youвҖҷre mixing three different layers into one section:
> 
> - cross-domain object doctrine
> - database governance doctrine
> - implementation hints
> 
> Those need cleaner boundaries or this section will age badly.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The main problems
> 
> ## 1. `QiNode` is doing too much
> Right now it reads like you want a universal base object model. That is dangerous unless you actually mean it.
> 
> If you over-constitutionalize this, every domain starts bending around a fake generic abstraction. That is how systems get mushy.
> 
> You want:
> - a **shared canonical record contract**
> - not a mandatory mega-schema
> - not a fake ORM superclass in doctrine form
> 
> So the language needs to say **minimum interoperable invariants**, not вҖңevery object should kind of look like this because portability.вҖқ
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 2. `should ideally implement` is weak law
> Blueprint law should not sound apologetic.
> 
> Either:
> - it is required
> - it is recommended
> - it is conditional
> 
> вҖңshould ideallyвҖқ is mush.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 3. `id` as вҖңSpine-issuedвҖқ is probably too absolute
> That depends on what you mean by вҖңregistered object.вҖқ
> 
> If every canonical record truly gets its identity from Spine, fine.  
> But if some domain tables exist operationally before archival registration or are not archive-native objects, that line becomes false.
> 
> You need to distinguish:
> - canonical platform/domain record identity
> - archive registration identity / lineage
> - external IDs
> 
> Do not blur those.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 4. `attributes jsonb` is risky
> This is the biggest structural red flag.
> 
> If you make `attributes` part of the constitutional minimum for canonical tables, people will dump real schema into JSONB and call it flexible. That becomes lazy-database disease.
> 
> For doctrine, JSONB should be allowed where appropriate, not silently normalized as default object structure.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 5. `No cross-schema joins are permitted at the database level`
> Too absolute. Probably wrong as law.
> 
> This sounds elegant, but it may box you in later for internal service workflows, reporting, secured views, or tightly governed reads.
> 
> What you probably mean is:
> - **cross-domain coupling must not rely on uncontrolled direct relational dependency**
> - **ownership remains within the home schema**
> - **cross-domain resolution should happen through contracts, registry/index patterns, or application logic unless explicitly approved**
> 
> That is very different from вҖңno joins ever.вҖқ
> 
> Do not write yourself into a corner for the sake of sounding pure.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 6. `created_at` and `updated_at` on every canonical table`
> Mostly good, but maybe not universal.
> 
> For most canonical tables: yes.  
> For pure junction tables, append-only logs, ultra-high-volume event tables, or imported mirror tables, maybe not exactly in that form.
> 
> This likely needs вҖңunless exempted by doctrine or ADR.вҖқ
> 
> ---
> 
> # Line-by-line review
> 
> ## `## The "QiNode" Object Standard`
> **Revise.**
> 
> I would drop the quotes unless QiNode is already a formal internal doctrine term.
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> ## QiNode Record Invariants
> ```
> 
> Or:
> ```markdown
> ## Canonical Record Invariants
> ```
> 
> Honestly, **Canonical Record Invariants** is cleaner and less branded.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `While QiOS supports modular, domain-specific schemas, all registered objects should follow a shared entity standard to ensure cross-domain portability and AI-ready retrieval.`
> **Revise heavily.**
> 
> Problems:
> - вҖңregistered objectsвҖқ is vague
> - вҖңshould followвҖқ is weak
> - вҖңAI-ready retrievalвҖқ is implementation-colored and trendy
> - вҖңcross-domain portabilityвҖқ is okay, but could be more precise
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> QiOS supports modular, domain-specific schemas. To preserve interoperability, governance, and lineage across domains, canonical records must follow a shared minimum invariant set where applicable.
> ```
> 
> ThatвҖҷs stronger and cleaner.
> 
> If you want to soften because not every table should have every field:
> ```markdown
> QiOS supports modular, domain-specific schemas. Canonical records should conform to a shared minimum invariant set unless a table's purpose or an approved ADR justifies an exception.
> ```
> 
> That is the version IвҖҷd use.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `### Common Field Invariants`
> **Approve.**
> 
> Good heading.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `Every canonical record in a domain schema (e.g., qicase, qihome) should ideally implement these standard fields to maintain Spine-alignment:`
> **Revise.**
> 
> Too soft, too vague, and вҖңSpine-alignmentвҖқ needs care.
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> Canonical domain records should implement the following common fields unless the table serves a narrowly scoped structural purpose or an approved ADR defines an exception:
> ```
> 
> That gives you room for:
> - junction tables
> - event logs
> - specialized system tables
> 
> ---
> 
> # Table review
> 
> ## `id | uuid | Canonical unique identifier (Spine-issued).`
> **Revise.**
> 
> Do not hard-bind вҖңSpine-issuedвҖқ unless you are absolutely sure that is constitutional truth for all canonical records.
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> | `id` | `uuid` | Canonical unique identifier for the record. |
> ```
> 
> If you want to express registration linkage, add another field later like `archive_id` where relevant. Do not force the same meaning into `id`.
> 
> Also, your question on UUID version: **do not codify version here yet** unless you are ready to enforce it everywhere. That belongs in a lower-level standard or migration rule, not this doctrine section.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `status | text | Lifecycle state (e.g., active, archived).`
> **Approve with revision.**
> 
> Good, but maybe:
> ```markdown
> | `status` | `text` | Lifecycle state for governance, routing, or operational control. |
> ```
> 
> This avoids freezing examples as pseudo-law.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `visibility | text | Access level (e.g., private, internal, public).`
> **Approve with revision.**
> 
> Good.
> ```markdown
> | `visibility` | `text` | Visibility or access classification for governed read exposure. |
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `metadata | jsonb | System-level provenance, lineage, and indexing keys.`
> **Mostly approve.**
> 
> This is the stronger of the two JSONB fields.
> 
> Maybe:
> ```markdown
> | `metadata` | `jsonb` | System-governed provenance, lineage, and auxiliary indexing metadata. |
> ```
> 
> Good.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `attributes | jsonb | Domain-specific extensible payload (e.g., amount, due_date).`
> **Revise hard or remove from invariant set.**
> 
> This is where people start hiding schema instead of designing schema.
> 
> My recommendation:
> - **Do not make `attributes` mandatory constitutional doctrine**
> - make it optional and controlled
> 
> Better options:
> 
> ### Option A вҖ” safest
> Remove `attributes` from the invariants table entirely.
> 
> Then add a note:
> ```markdown
> Domain-specific fields should be modeled as typed relational columns by default. An `attributes` or equivalent extensibility field may be used only where flexibility is justified and does not replace stable schema design.
> ```
> 
> ### Option B вҖ” keep but cage it
> ```markdown
> | `attributes` | `jsonb` | Optional extensibility field for low-volatility domain data that does not warrant dedicated columns. |
> ```
> 
> If you keep it, it must be explicitly subordinate to typed schema.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Schema governance section
> 
> ## `## Schema Governance Invariants`
> **Approve.**
> 
> Good heading.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `Migration-as-Truth: Supabase native migrations are the canonical authority. Prisma or other ORMs are strictly for query/client-side mapping and do not define truth.`
> **Approve with minor revision.**
> 
> Strong. Good.
> 
> Better:
> ```markdown
> * **Migration-as-Truth**: Supabase migrations are the canonical schema authority. ORMs and client models may reflect the schema, but they do not define it.
> ```
> 
> Cleaner and less tool-specific, unless you intentionally want Supabase named.
> 
> If this blueprint is explicitly Supabase-bound, leaving Supabase named is fine.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `Relational Core: The system is relational at the core. Vector embeddings and graph nodes must remain derived from, and strictly link back to, stable canonical records.`
> **Approve strongly.**
> 
> This is good doctrine.
> 
> Minor refinement:
> ```markdown
> * **Relational Core**: The system is relational at its core. Vector, graph, and AI-derived structures must remain downstream of stable canonical records and preserve traceable linkage back to them.
> ```
> 
> Very solid.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `Domain Isolation: No cross-schema joins are permitted at the database level. Inter-domain relationships must be resolved via qigraph.master_index or the Application Layer.`
> **Revise heavily.**
> 
> As written, too absolute and probably too restrictive.
> 
> Recommended:
> ```markdown
> * **Domain Isolation**: Domain ownership must remain local to the owning schema. Cross-domain dependencies must not create hidden coupling or competing sources of truth. Inter-domain resolution should occur through governed registry patterns, approved interfaces, or the application layer unless an ADR explicitly permits tighter database-level integration.
> ```
> 
> That preserves the principle without making future you hate present you.
> 
> Also: naming `qigraph.master_index` here may be too implementation-specific unless that object is already formal constitutional doctrine.
> 
> If it is not fully ratified, do not freeze it here.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `Audit Requirements: Every canonical table must maintain created_at and updated_at timestamps for automated audit-trail hygiene.`
> **Approve with revision.**
> 
> Recommended:
> ```markdown
> * **Audit Requirements**: Canonical tables should maintain `created_at` and `updated_at` timestamps unless the table is append-only, purely structural, or explicitly exempted by doctrine or ADR.
> ```
> 
> That is more accurate.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Your three review questions
> 
> ## 1. `metadata` vs `attributes`
> **Answer: yes, but only if you subordinate `attributes`.**
> 
> `metadata` as system-governed provenance bucket makes sense.  
> `attributes` as domain-flex field is where abuse starts.
> 
> So the real answer is:
> 
> - `metadata` = good constitutional concept
> - `attributes` = dangerous if framed as default
> 
> Best approach:
> - keep `metadata`
> - make `attributes` optional, constrained, and explicitly secondary to typed columns
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 2. Should deletion be banned constitutionally?
> **Not globally. Not as a blanket law in this section.**
> 
> Do **not** say вҖңdeletion is bannedвҖқ across the whole constitution unless you really mean it for every domain and lifecycle.
> 
> Because some data absolutely should be:
> - hard-deleted
> - retention-pruned
> - cryptographically erased
> - legally removed
> - transient by design
> 
> What you can say at constitutional level is:
> 
> ```markdown
> Canonical business records should favor governed lifecycle transitions over silent destructive deletion. Hard deletion must be intentional, policy-backed, and permitted only where retention, privacy, or operational rules allow it.
> ```
> 
> That is much better than вҖңeverything becomes status = deleted.вҖқ
> 
> Soft delete everywhere sounds neat until it becomes garbage retention.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 3. Should `id` specify UUID version?
> **No. Not here.**
> 
> That is too low-level for this doctrine section unless you are already enforcing one UUID version across the entire platform.
> 
> Put UUID version rules in:
> - migration standards
> - database conventions
> - implementation standards
> 
> Not in this constitutional object section.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Recommended replacement block
> 
> This is the version IвҖҷd actually approve:
> 
> ```markdown
> ## Canonical Record Invariants
> 
> QiOS supports modular, domain-specific schemas. Canonical records should conform to a shared minimum invariant set unless the table's purpose or an approved ADR justifies an exception.
> 
> ### Common Field Invariants
> 
> Canonical domain records should implement the following common fields unless the table serves a narrowly scoped structural purpose or an approved ADR defines an exception:
> 
> | Field | Type | Role |
> |---|---|---|
> | `id` | `uuid` | Canonical unique identifier for the record. |
> | `status` | `text` | Lifecycle state for governance, routing, or operational control. |
> | `visibility` | `text` | Visibility or access classification for governed read exposure. |
> | `metadata` | `jsonb` | System-governed provenance, lineage, and auxiliary indexing metadata. |
> 
> Domain-specific fields should be modeled as typed relational columns by default. An `attributes` or equivalent extensibility field may be used only where flexibility is justified and does not replace stable schema design.
> 
> ## Schema Governance Invariants
> 
> * **Migration-as-Truth**: Supabase migrations are the canonical schema authority. ORMs and client models may reflect the schema, but they do not define it.
> * **Relational Core**: The system is relational at its core. Vector, graph, and AI-derived structures must remain downstream of stable canonical records and preserve traceable linkage back to them.
> * **Domain Isolation**: Domain ownership must remain local to the owning schema. Cross-domain dependencies must not create hidden coupling or competing sources of truth. Inter-domain resolution should occur through governed registry patterns, approved interfaces, or the application layer unless an ADR explicitly permits tighter database-level integration.
> * **Audit Requirements**: Canonical tables should maintain `created_at` and `updated_at` timestamps unless the table is append-only, purely structural, or explicitly exempted by doctrine or ADR.
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # Bottom line
> 
> ## Keep
> - migration-as-truth
> - relational core
> - shared minimum invariants
> - audit timestamp expectation
> - domain isolation principle
> 
> ## Change
> - remove вҖңshould ideallyвҖқ
> - stop saying `id` is Spine-issued unless thatвҖҷs truly universal
> - do not normalize `attributes jsonb` as mandatory doctrine
> - do not ban all cross-schema joins as absolute law
> - do not lock UUID version here
> 
> ## My call
> This proposal is **worth promoting**, but only after these revisions. As written, it would quietly create loopholes in one place and unnecessary rigidity in another.
> 
> Send the next packet after this one is tightened.

> | `attributes jsonb` as mandatory | Removed from the invariant table; added as an explicit opt-in with a warning against schema avoidance |

> | `created_at/updated_at` on everything | Made conditional вҖ” exempts append-only, structural, and ADR-exempted tables |

> # What needs tightening
> 
> ## 1. `tenant_id` cannot stay universal
> Your instinct is right. Absolute wording is sloppy here.
> 
> If Law 4 currently reads like tenant isolation is required on everything, that is too broad. Some system tables are platform-scoped, global, or infrastructure-scoped.
> 
> Use this instead:
> 
> ```markdown
> 4. Tenant Isolation Where Applicable
> Agents must enforce tenant-scoped access controls, tenant identifiers, and RLS wherever data is tenant-owned or tenant-addressable. System, structural, and explicitly exempted tables must be governed by their defined ownership and access model.
> ```
> 
> Why:
> - preserves tenant isolation as law
> - avoids forcing fake `tenant_id` columns onto tables that should not have them
> - gives room for ADR-based exceptions without weakening the doctrine
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 2. Spine law needs precision
> This is the bigger one.
> 
> If Law 5 says all canonical data must originate through QiArchive, that is too blunt and will break your architecture language.
> 
> Not all canonical records are ingested externals. Some are directly authored in their owning domain.
> 
> What you actually mean is:
> 
> - **external inputs** must register through the Spine
> - **domain-native authored records** can originate in the owning domain
> - but derived systems still cannot invent truth
> 
> Use this instead:
> 
> ```markdown
> 5. Spine-First for External Intake
> Agents must enforce Spine registration for externally ingested signals, files, messages, and events before downstream derivation or routing. Domain-native records authored directly within an approved owning domain may originate there, but must still obey canonical ownership, audit, and governance rules.
> ```
> 
> That fixes the overreach cleanly.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 3. Operating modes are guidance unless enforced
> Yes. If the system does not actually switch modes programmatically, do not pretend it does.
> 
> That is fake rigor.
> 
> You have two options:
> 
> ### Better option
> Keep them, but relabel them as behavioral frames:
> 
> ```markdown
> ## Agent Behavioral Modes
> 
> These modes describe expected agent behavior patterns. They are governance guidance, not independent sources of authority.
> ```
> 
> Then keep the four modes underneath.
> 
> ### Cleaner option
> If you want the file tighter, remove the section entirely and let the actual law live in:
> - pre-action validation
> - non-negotiable laws
> - out-of-bounds protocol
> - enforcement priority
> 
> My vote: **keep them, but demote them to guidance**. They are useful, just not law.

> ## Allowed decisions
> Only these three:
> 
> - **Adopt** = becomes official canonical taxonomy
> - **Alias** = allowed as legacy/reconciled terminology, but **not** canonical
> - **Reject** = not permitted in official blueprint
> 
> No fuzzy вҖңmergeвҖқ language. ThatвҖҷs where drift hides.
> 
> # Decision rules
> 
> ## Adopt only if
> - it maps cleanly to one existing domain
> - it does not duplicate an existing concept
> - it does not create ambiguity in ownership
> 
> ## Alias if
> - it is useful historically or appears in reconciled drafts
> - but the existing blueprint already has a clearer canonical term
> 
> ## Reject if
> - it overlaps multiple domains
> - it collapses band/domain distinctions
> - it sounds neat but weakens ownership clarity
> 
> # What to watch for
> 
> ## Red flag 1: code families that are conceptual, not domains
> Stuff like `CORE`, `ARC`, `FIN`, `COM` may be useful shorthand, but if your official blueprint already uses explicit domain ownership, these may be **alias material**, not canonical domains.
> 
> ## Red flag 2: mixing bands and domains
> If a label describes architecture tier, platform concern, or storage behavior instead of an owning domain, it should not become a domain code.
> 
> ## Red flag 3: retrofitting the blueprint to match reconciled shorthand
> Do **not** bend the official map to accommodate old convenience labels. The old labels must earn their place.
> 
> # Best likely outcome
> 
> My bias going in:
> 
> - **prioritize the existing `domains.md`**
> - treat reconciled codes as candidates
> - only append missing codes if they represent a real ownership boundary not already covered
> 
> That is the clean move. Anything else risks reopening the constitution you just locked.

> # What IвҖҷd tighten
> 
> ## 1. `OPS -> QiSys` is weaker than the table makes it sound
> I would **not** confidently alias `OPS` to `QiSys` unless the legacy docs explicitly used `OPS` to mean orchestration, jobs, retries, connectors, system state, and worker operations.

> ## What likely got lost
> 
> YouвҖҷre describing a distinct artifact that should have contained:
> 
> - **canonical band names**
> - **band purpose**
> - **allowed aliases / legacy labels**
> - **what is not a band**
> - possibly **mapping from old shorthand to canonical band terms**
> 
> That is **not** the same thing as `domains.md`.  
> `domains.md` handles domain ownership.  
> A **band registry** sits one level above that and defines the constitutional strata.
> 
> ## What the band registry should probably contain
> 
> Based on what youвҖҷve already locked, the canonical bands are almost certainly:
> 
> - **Core**
> - **Platform**
> - **Domain**
> 
> And then maybe legacy/non-canonical references like:
> 
> - `CORE` вҶ’ alias for **Core** band only, **not** a domain
> - `PLT` / `PLATFORM` вҶ’ alias for **Platform** band only, **not** `QiOne` unless explicitly stated
> - `DOM` / `DOMAIN` вҶ’ alias for **Domain** band only
> 
> If there were extra bands in the lost version, they need proof before adoption. DonвҖҷt re-import mystery layers.
> 
> ## Best move
> 
> Create a **new small constitutional file**, something like:
> 
> - `01_governance/band_registry.md`  
> or
> - `02_architecture/band_registry.md`
> 
> IвҖҷd lean **`02_architecture/band_registry.md`**, because bands are structural topology, not just policy language.
> 
> ## Minimal draft to restore it
> 
> Use this as the clean replacement, not a speculative novel:
> 
> ```markdown
> # Band Registry
> 
> Status: Active
> Authority: Constitutional
> Purpose: Define the canonical structural bands of QiOS and prevent band/domain confusion.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Canonical Bands
> 
> | Band | Role | Notes |
> |---|---|---|
> | Core | Canonical spine, system coordination, archive, graph, and derived intelligence foundations | Includes domains such as QiArchive, QiGraph, QiAlly, QiSys |
> | Platform | Platform ownership, tenant/platform configuration, identity-adjacent application control surfaces | Includes QiOne |
> | Domain | Business and subject-area applications built on the governed spine | Includes QiCase, QiHome, QiTax, QiVault, QiCMS, QiChronicle, QiKnowledge |
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Band Rules
> 
> 1. A band is a structural layer, not a schema name.
> 2. A band does not replace domain ownership.
> 3. No shorthand band code is canonical.
> 4. Domains must belong to one canonical band.
> 5. Band assignment changes require ADR approval.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Legacy / Informal Aliases
> 
> | Alias | Canonical Meaning | Status |
> |---|---|---|
> | CORE | Core band | Legacy alias only |
> | PLT | Platform band | Legacy alias only |
> | PLATFORM | Platform band | Legacy alias only |
> | DOM | Domain band | Legacy alias only |
> | DOMAIN | Domain band | Legacy alias only |
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Explicit Non-Rules
> 
> - `CORE` is not a domain.
> - `PLT` is not automatically `QiOne`.
> - Shorthand codes must not appear as schema names, ADR domain names, or canonical ownership labels unless explicitly promoted.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Promotion Rule
> 
> Any new band, renamed band, or imported band alias from reconciled documentation requires explicit review and ADR approval before promotion into official blueprint doctrine.
> ```
> 
> ## My recommendation
> 
> Do **not** waste time hunting the old version unless you think it had materially different bands.  
> At this point, the smarter move is to **reconstruct a clean registry from current law** and move on.
> 
> If you want, IвҖҷll help you turn this into a tighter promotion packet next.

### User
> here is the document i think we were missing to tie togther the banded codes, the only weired thing i saw was clients have this CLT band but clients i would use their initials to is applies indicidually to clients like how we did bbr4821 and zjk4566   Yes вҖ” allowing **3 decimal digits** is the right move.

> That gives you:
>

> 
> ---
> 
> # QiOS Namespace Standard вҖ” Final Final (Stress-Test Version)
> 
> ## 1. Core philosophy
> 
> QiOS uses a **chart-of-accounts style namespace router**.
> 
> It is designed for:
> 
> * object-based thinking
> * chronological artifact naming
> * shallow mental maps
> * easy rollup up/down the tree
> * minimal folder duplication
> * stress-safe retrieval
> 
> The system is **routing-first**, not identity-first.
> 
> * **Namespace code** = where it belongs
> * **QID / QiDecimal** = what exact object it is
> 
> ---
> 
> # 2. Locked syntax
> 
> ## Structural node
> 
> ```text
> ####AAA
> ```
> 
> Examples:
> 
> ```text
> 0000ROOT
> 0100KBS
> 0300LGL
> 0500OPS
> ```
> 
> ## Child / artifact / subgroup
> 
> ```text
> ####AAA.###
> ```
> 
> Examples:
> 
> ```text
> 0330LGL.000
> 0330LGL.010
> 0330LGL.011
> 0331LGL.001
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # 3. Naming patterns
> 
> ## Folder / section nodes
> 
> ```text
> ####AAA_Label_Name
> ```
> 
> Examples:
> 
> ```text
> 0000ROOT
> 0100KBS_0_Start_Here
> 0300LGL_Legal
> 0330LGL_Impact
> ```
> 
> ## Artifact files
> 
> ```text
> ####AAA.###_YYYY-MM-DD_title_detail.ext
> ```
> 
> Examples:
> 
> ```text
> 0331LGL.001_2026-03-27_personal_observational_statement.md
> 0332LGL.001_2026-03-27_supporting_nonclinical_analysis.md
> 0420FIN.001_2026-03-27_household_bills_register.md
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # 4. Decimal rules вҖ” locked
> 
> ## Three-digit decimal structure
> 
> ```text
> .000
> .010
> .011
> .012
> .020
> .021
> ```
> 
> This is intentional.
> 
> ### Meaning
> 
> * `.000` = node default / base container / index for that branch
> * `.010`, `.020`, `.030` = subgroup buckets
> * `.011-.019` = artifacts or children under `.010`
> * `.021-.029` = artifacts or children under `.020`
> 
> This lets you create **soft grouping without new folders**.
> 
> ---
> 
> # 5. Grouping rule with trailing zeroes
> 
> Yes вҖ” **decimals ending in zeroes are grouping buckets**.
> 
> ## Allowed and preferred
> 
> ```text
> 0330LGL.000   вҶҗ base/default under Impact
> 0330LGL.010   вҶҗ Observation cluster
> 0330LGL.020   вҶҗ Analysis cluster
> 0330LGL.030   вҶҗ Log cluster
> 0330LGL.040   вҶҗ Function cluster
> 0330LGL.050   вҶҗ Summary cluster
> ```
> 
> Then artifacts live under those buckets:
> 
> ```text
> 0330LGL.011_2026-03-27_initial_observation.md
> 0330LGL.012_2026-03-28_followup_observation.md
> 
> 0330LGL.021_2026-03-27_supporting_nonclinical_analysis.md
> 
> 0330LGL.031_2026-03-27_acute_stress_episode_log.md
> 
> 0330LGL.041_2026-03-27_functional_impairment_note.md
> 
> 0330LGL.051_2026-03-27_state_of_mind_summary.md
> ```
> 
> That is the cleanest version.
> 
> ---
> 
> # 6. Rollup logic
> 
> Everything rolls up numerically.
> 
> Example:
> 
> ```text
> 0330LGL.011
> вҶ’ 0330LGL.010
> вҶ’ 0330LGL
> вҶ’ 0300LGL
> ```
> 
> This makes:
> 
> * retrieval easy
> * inheritance easy
> * automation easy
> * mental scanning easy
> 
> ---
> 
> # 7. ADHD-safe structural limits
> 
> ## Locked limits
> 
> * Max structural folder depth: **3 levels from root**
> * Anything more specific than that uses **decimal buckets or files**
> * Do **not** solve ambiguity by adding folders
> * Use decimals first
> 
> ## Translation
> 
> Good:
> 
> ```text
> 0300LGL
> 0330LGL_Impact
> 0330LGL.010
> 0330LGL.011_2026-03-27_initial_observation.md
> ```
> 
> Bad:
> 
> ```text
> 0300LGL
> 0330LGL_Case_Harm
> 0331LGL_State_of_Mind
> 0331.01LGL_Observation
> 0331.01.01LGL_Subnote
> ```
> 
> That second version is exactly how ADHD systems rot.
> 
> ---

> # 10. Capacity
> 
> With this system you now have:
> 
> * **10,000 structural trunks** from `0000вҖ“9999`
> * each trunk can support **1,000 decimal slots** from `.000вҖ“.999`
> 
> So immediate address space is:
> 
> ```text
> 10,000 x 1,000 = 10,000,000 routed namespaces
> ```
> 
> That is before adding file names, dates, or QIDs.
> 
> So yes вҖ” you now have **ten million immediate namespace routes**.
> 
> That is more than enough.
