> # 11. Final rules to paste into the blueprint
> 
> ```md
> ## QiOS Namespace Standard вҖ” Final Locked
> 
> QiOS uses a hierarchical accounting-style namespace router.
> 
> ### Structural syntax
> - `####AAA` = structural node
> - `####AAA.###` = grouped child, artifact, or typed sub-route
> 
> ### Naming
> - Folders / sections: `####AAA_Label_Name`
> - Files / artifacts: `####AAA.###_YYYY-MM-DD_title_detail.ext`
> 
> ### Decimal rules
> - `.000` = base/default/index under node
> - `.010`, `.020`, `.030` = grouping buckets
> - `.011-.019` = items under `.010`
> - `.021-.029` = items under `.020`
> - trailing zeroes are reserved for grouping, not leaf artifacts unless intentionally used as indexes
> 
> ### Rollup
> All descendants roll up numerically.
> 
> Example:
> `0330LGL.011` вҶ’ `0330LGL.010` вҶ’ `0330LGL` вҶ’ `0300LGL`
> 
> ### ADHD-safe constraints
> - max structural depth = 3 levels from root
> - use decimal groupings before creating new folders
> - avoid duplicate sibling concepts
> - organize by function, not theme
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # 12. Recommendation
> 
> For the IDE stress test, have it validate these five things:
> 
> 1. **No structural node deeper than 3 levels**
> 2. **No sibling labels with semantic overlap**
> 3. **All artifact files use `####AAA.###_YYYY-MM-DD_title_detail.ext`**
> 4. **All `.000/.010/.020` buckets are treated as group nodes**
> 5. **No new top-level band created without explicit registry entry**
> 
> If you want, next IвҖҷll write the **actual registry artifact** in markdown or YAML format so you can paste it straight into the blueprint and feed it to the IDE.

### Assistant
> Yes. The weirdness is real, and your instinct is right.
> 
> ## The problem
> 
> `1000CLT` is fine as a **functional band for client operations/CRM**.
> 
> It is **not** fine as the identity namespace for actual individual clients if you already use per-client codes like `bbr4821`, `zjk4566`.
> 
> Those are two different things:
> 
> - **Band code** = routing category
> - **Client code** = entity identity
> 
> If you mash them together, the system gets muddy fast.
> 
> ## Clean fix
> 
> Keep `1000CLT` only for **shared client-management functions**, not for individual client identities.
> 
> That means:
> 
> ### `1000CLT` should mean:
> - leads
> - pipeline
> - engagement templates
> - onboarding
> - billing workflows
> - portal ops
> - closeout patterns
> - CRM/admin artifacts
> 
> ### Individual clients should live in:
> - reserved **client/matter/tenant blocks** in the `5000вҖ“7990` range
> - or a separate **client code + matter code** identity layer tied to QIDs
> 
> That is the cleaner architecture.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What to lock
> 
> ## 1. Keep the band registry
> Your band registry is still useful and valid.
> 
> It should define the **routing classes** only:
> 
> - `0300LGL` = legal work
> - `0400FIN` = finance
> - `0500OPS` = ops
> - `1000CLT` = client operations / CRM
> - etc.
> 
> ## 2. Do not let `CLT` mean вҖңthis specific clientвҖқ
> That is where drift starts.
> 
> `CLT` should mean:
> **вҖңclient-facing operational domainвҖқ**, not вҖңclient identity.вҖқ
> 
> ## 3. Put individual clients in the reserved client ecosystem range
> This part of your earlier draft is actually smart:
> 
> - `5000вҖ“7990` = client / matter / tenant blocks
> 
> That gives you a clean split:
> 
> - `1000CLT` = generic client operations
> - `5xxxвҖ“7xxx` = actual client ecosystems
> 
> ---
> 
> # Best-practice model
> 
> ## Layer A вҖ” functional registry
> These are universal routing bands.
> 
> Examples:
> - `1000CLT_Leads`
> - `1030CLT_Engagements`
> - `1060CLT_Billing`
> 
> ## Layer B вҖ” client identity
> Each client gets a stable identity code, like:
> - `BBR4821`
> - `ZJK4566`
> 
> That code is **not a band**. It is an entity key.
> 
> ## Layer C вҖ” client block routing
> If a client needs a dedicated routed workspace, assign them a reserved block:
> 
> - `5100_BBR4821`
> - `5200_ZJK4566`
> 
> Then inside that block, you can mirror functional buckets if needed:
> 
> - `5110_BBR4821_Admin`
> - `5120_BBR4821_Tax`
> - `5130_BBR4821_Billing`
> - `5140_BBR4821_Documents`
> 
> That is much cleaner than pretending `CLT` is both the category and the client.
> 
> ---
> 
> # My recommendation
> 
> Lock this rule:
> 
> ```md
> ## Client Routing Rule
> 
> `1000CLT` is a functional band for shared client operations, CRM workflows, templates, billing operations, portal administration, and engagement management.
> 
> It is NOT the namespace for individual client identity.
> 
> Individual clients must be identified by their own stable client code (for example `BBR4821`, `ZJK4566`) and, where needed, assigned dedicated workspace blocks from the reserved client range (`5000вҖ“7990`).
> 
> Band codes classify work.
> Client codes identify entities.
> They must not be conflated.
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # What I would change in your registry right now
> 
> ## Leave this:
> ```text
> 1000CLT
> 1010CLT_Leads
> 1020CLT_Active
> 1030CLT_Engagements
> 1040CLT_Deliverables
> 1050CLT_Portal
> 1060CLT_Billing
> 1070CLT_Closeout
> 1080CLT_Archives
> 1090CLT_Reserved
> ```
> 
> ## But reinterpret it as:
> **Client Operations / CRM**, not вҖңall client folders live here.вҖқ
> 
> You may even want to rename it for clarity:
> 
> ### Better option:
> ```text
> 1000CRM
> 1010CRM_Leads
> 1020CRM_Active
> 1030CRM_Engagements
> 1040CRM_Deliverables
> 1050CRM_Portal
> 1060CRM_Billing
> 1070CRM_Closeout
> 1080CRM_Archives
> 1090CRM_Reserved
> ```
> 
> That would remove ambiguity immediately.
> 
> Because honestly, `CLT` invites misuse.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Straight answer
> 
> If you want **maximum clarity**, rename the band from `CLT` to `CRM`.
> 
> Because:
> 
> - `CLT` sounds like вҖңthis is where clients goвҖқ
> - `CRM` sounds like вҖңthis is where client-management operations goвҖқ
> 
> That distinction matters.
> 
> ## My vote
> - **Use `CRM` for the functional band**
> - **Use client initials/code for individual identity**
> - **Use `5000вҖ“7990` for actual client ecosystems**
> 
> That is the least confusing version.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Final decision map
> 
> ## Best locked model
> - `1000CRM` = shared client ops band
> - `5000вҖ“7990` = per-client / per-matter blocks
> - `BBR4821`, `ZJK4566` = client identity codes
> - QIDs / QiDecimal = exact object identity and routing
> 
> ## Avoid
> - using `1000CLT` as both CRM band and actual client namespace
> - putting all clients under one giant `CLT` folder tree
> - mixing routing labels with entity identity
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the architectural truth here.
> 
> ## Recommended next artifact
> You need a **Band Registry + Identity Registry rule** as two separate sections, not one merged concept.

### User
> gotcha, that makes sense ok lets lock it in, are we going to have a namespace registry or band registry in supabase or how does this work in the larger scheme of things, what do i tell the ide to udpate etc.

### Assistant
> IвҖҷm tying the naming system to the actual platform layers now so this doesnвҖҷt become a pretty document with no enforcement. The core split is: **registry in doctrine, enforcement in code, truth in migrations/data where needed**.

### Assistant
> Yes. Lock it, but do **not** turn this into an overengineered database monster.
> 
> # The real answer
> 
> You need **both**, but at different levels:
> 
> ## 1. Band Registry
> This is **constitutional**. It belongs in the blueprint first.
> 
> It defines:
> - what bands exist
> - what they mean
> - which are reserved
> - which aliases are allowed
> - which ranges are for future use
> - what is forbidden
> 
> This is **human-governed doctrine**, not runtime truth.
> 
> ## 2. Namespace Registry
> This can exist in Supabase **only if** you need operational validation, search, automation, or UI tooling.
> 
> It defines:
> - registered codes actually in use
> - who owns them
> - status
> - alias mappings
> - reserved ranges
> - whether a code is active, deprecated, blocked, or experimental
> 
> This is **operational metadata**, not constitutional law.
> 
> So the stack is:
> 
> - **Blueprint** = law
> - **Supabase namespace tables** = enforceable operational registry
> - **IDE rules/scripts** = preflight validation and drift detection
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the larger scheme.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What not to do
> 
> Do **not** make Supabase the source of truth for band doctrine.
> 
> Why:
> - bands are architectural law
> - law should not silently change because somebody edited a row
> - DB rows are too easy to mutate
> - doctrine belongs in version control and ADR history
> 
> So:
> 
> ## Source of truth split
> ### Blueprint docs
> - canonical band definitions
> - reserved numeric ranges
> - naming laws
> - routing principles
> - identity vs namespace distinction
> 
> ### Supabase
> - registry tables for actual used namespaces
> - client/workspace block assignments
> - aliases
> - validation support
> - UI/admin tooling
> 
> That is the right separation.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Recommended model
> 
> ## A. In the blueprint, add two sections
> 
> ### `Band Registry`
> Defines the global routing classes.
> 
> Example concepts:
> - `0000вҖ“1990` = system/global bands
> - `2000вҖ“4990` = future major domains
> - `5000вҖ“7990` = client/matter/tenant blocks
> - `8000вҖ“8990` = lab/sandbox
> - `9000вҖ“9990` = archive/legacy
> 
> And then explicit entries like:
> - `0300LGL`
> - `0400FIN`
> - `0500OPS`
> - `0600SYS`
> - `1000CRM`
> 
> ### `Identity Registry Rules`
> Defines that:
> - band codes classify work
> - entity codes identify tenants/clients/matters
> - client codes like `BBR4821` are not band codes
> - reserved client blocks may map to those identities
> - aliases never replace canonical names
> 
> ---
> 
> ## B. In Supabase, create a small registry layer
> 
> Not huge. Just enough to make the system enforceable.
> 
> ### Minimum tables
> 
> #### `qione.namespace_bands`
> Stores operational copies of approved bands for tooling.
> 
> Suggested columns:
> - `id`
> - `band_code` вҶ’ `1000CRM`
> - `band_number` вҶ’ `1000`
> - `label` вҶ’ `Client Operations`
> - `class` вҶ’ `band`
> - `status` вҶ’ `active|deprecated|reserved`
> - `canonical_name`
> - `alias_jsonb`
> - `range_start`
> - `range_end`
> - `notes`
> - `created_at`
> - `updated_at`
> 
> This is basically the runtime mirror of blueprint-approved bands.
> 
> #### `qione.namespace_allocations`
> Tracks allocated namespaces and reserved blocks.
> 
> Suggested columns:
> - `id`
> - `namespace_code` вҶ’ `5100BBR`
> - `namespace_number` вҶ’ `5100`
> - `namespace_type` вҶ’ `client_block|matter_block|system_block|lab_block`
> - `band_code` вҶ’ `5000CLX` or just parent range reference
> - `entity_code` вҶ’ `BBR4821`
> - `tenant_id` nullable if applicable
> - `owner_type` вҶ’ `client|matter|internal|system`
> - `status` вҶ’ `reserved|active|deprecated|blocked`
> - `assigned_to`
> - `approved_by`
> - `adr_ref` nullable
> - `metadata`
> - `created_at`
> - `updated_at`
> 
> This is where actual block assignments live.
> 
> #### `qione.namespace_aliases`
> Only if you truly need alias lookup.
> 
> Suggested columns:
> - `id`
> - `alias_code`
> - `canonical_code`
> - `alias_type` вҶ’ `legacy|shorthand|display`
> - `status`
> - `notes`
> 
> If you do not need alias resolution in apps, skip this table for now.
> 
> ---
> 
> # My actual recommendation
> 
> Start with **two tables max**:
> 
> - `qione.namespace_bands`
> - `qione.namespace_allocations`
> 
> Do not build more until you actually feel pain.
> 
> ---
> 
> # How it works in the larger scheme
> 
> ## Blueprint layer
> You promote and lock:
> - namespace syntax
> - band ranges
> - client identity rule
> - alias policy
> - depth constraints
> - grouping bucket behavior
> 
> ## Migration layer
> You add Supabase migrations for:
> - registry tables
> - constraints
> - unique keys
> - maybe check constraints for code format
> 
> ## IDE / agent layer
> You tell the IDE to:
> - validate names against blueprint rules
> - refuse new top-level bands not in registry
> - refuse use of client codes as band codes
> - enforce decimal format
> - flag semantic overlap
> - require ADR for new band creation
> - require migration-first changes to registry tables
> 
> ## App layer
> Eventually:
> - admin UI can reserve client blocks
> - worker can look up valid namespace ranges
> - file generators can assign proper prefixes
> - search can roll up by band/block/entity
> 
> That is how this becomes a living system instead of just naming cosplay.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What to tell the IDE to update
> 
> Tell it to do **four things**.
> 
> ## 1. Update doctrine docs
> Add or patch these sections:
> 
> - `03_structure/namespace_standard.md` or equivalent
> - `03_structure/band_registry.md` or append into existing structure docs
> - `04_data/schema.md` with registry-table doctrine
> - `01_governance/standards.md` with validation requirements
> - `01_governance/agent_rules.md` so agents cannot invent bands or allocations
> 
> ## 2. Add migration files
> Create migrations for:
> 
> - `qione.namespace_bands`
> - `qione.namespace_allocations`
> 
> With:
> - unique constraints on canonical codes
> - check constraints for format where practical
> - audit timestamps
> - status fields
> - ADR reference fields where needed
> 
> ## 3. Add validator rules/scripts
> Create a lightweight validator that checks:
> 
> - no top-level band outside approved registry
> - no folder depth > 3
> - artifact naming format is valid
> - decimal buckets use `.000`, `.010`, `.020` correctly
> - no individual client code is being treated as a band
> - no new allocation created without registry entry or approval path
> 
> ## 4. Add allocation doctrine
> Document how client blocks are assigned.
> 
> Example:
> - client identities remain `BBR4821`, `ZJK4566`
> - reserved workspace block may be `5100`, `5110`, etc.
> - block assignment is recorded in `namespace_allocations`
> - display names can differ, canonical codes cannot
> 
> ---
> 
> # The instruction to give your IDE
> 
> Use this:
> 
> ```text
> Update the QiOS blueprint and schema to formalize the namespace system as a two-layer model:
> 
> 1. Blueprint law:
> - Add a Band Registry section that defines canonical routing bands, reserved numeric ranges, alias policy, and the rule that band codes classify work but do not identify entities.
> - Add an Identity Registry rule stating that client/entity codes (for example BBR4821, ZJK4566) are separate from band codes and must not be conflated.
> - Lock the client rule that the former 1000CLT concept is a functional CRM/client-operations band only, not the namespace for individual clients.
> - Prefer renaming that functional band to 1000CRM for clarity unless an existing official band already governs that role.
> 
> 2. Supabase operational registry:
> - Add migration-first schema support in qione for:
>   - namespace_bands
>   - namespace_allocations
> - namespace_bands stores blueprint-approved band metadata for runtime validation and admin tooling.
> - namespace_allocations stores assigned blocks for clients, matters, tenants, system ranges, and reserved namespaces.
> - Do not treat database rows as constitutional truth; the blueprint remains the source of law.
> 
> 3. Validation:
> - Add IDE/agent validation rules so no new band, namespace block, or alias can be invented implicitly.
> - Enforce decimal namespace syntax, max depth = 3, grouping bucket rules, and prohibition on using client identity codes as functional band codes.
> - Require ADR or explicit blueprint promotion before adding a new top-level band.
> 
> 4. Deliverables:
> - Proposed blueprint patch text
> - Supabase migration SQL
> - validator script/spec
> - brief note identifying any existing conflicts between current docs and the new registry model
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # My opinion on naming
> 
> Rename `1000CLT` to `1000CRM` unless you have a strong reason not to.
> 
> Because:
> - `CLT` sounds like client identity storage
> - `CRM` sounds like client-management operations
> - less ambiguity = less future mess
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the cleaner move.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Minimal implementation order
> 
> ## Do now
> - lock blueprint rule
> - rename `CLT` to `CRM`
> - define `5000вҖ“7990` as client/matter/tenant allocation range
> - create 2 registry tables
> - add validator rules
> 
> ## Do later
> - admin UI
> - alias resolution tooling
> - automated namespace allocator
> - rollup dashboards
> 
> ---
> 
> # Bottom line
> 
> Use a **band registry in the blueprint** and a **namespace allocation registry in Supabase**.
> 
> Do not choose one or the other.
> They serve different jobs.
> 
> - **Blueprint** tells the truth
> - **Supabase** tracks usage
> - **IDE** enforces behavior
> 
> That is the sane architecture.
> 
> If you want, next move is I write the actual **blueprint patch text + Supabase table spec** in copy-paste form.

### User
> also 2990 client tenants is not goign tot be enough i have already like 13000 contacts and in theory all of them would have a login at some point.

### Assistant
> Correct. **2990 is nowhere near enough** if you were treating that range as вҖңone code per contact/client/user.вҖқ
> 
> That would be a bad design anyway.
> 
> # The mistake
> 
> YouвҖҷre mixing **routing blocks** with **entity population**.
> 
> Those are not the same thing.
> 
> - **Namespace bands / blocks** = structural routing
> - **Clients / contacts / users / tenants** = actual entities in tables
> 
> You do **not** want one top-level namespace block per contact. That would be insane fast.
> 
> If you have 13,000 contacts, the answer is **not** вҖңmake 13,000 top-level bands.вҖқ
> The answer is:
> 
> - keep a **small stable routing registry**
> - store **actual client/contact identities as records**
> - allocate namespace blocks only when a client/matter/workspace truly needs one
> 
> # What should happen instead
> 
> ## Separate these 4 things
> 
> ### 1. Contacts
> Every person/company lead/contact lives as a normal DB record.
> 
> Examples:
> - contact_id
> - client_id
> - tenant_id
> - portal_user_id
> 
> These are **not namespace bands**.
> 
> ### 2. Portal users
> Also normal DB auth/application records.
> 
> Again, **not namespace bands**.
> 
> ### 3. Clients/tenants
> These are business entities/accounts/workspaces.
> Some may need a reserved namespace block.
> Most contacts under them do not.
> 
> ### 4. Namespace allocations
> Only used for:
> - a tenant workspace
> - a client workspace
> - a matter/case
> - a major project
> - a legal/archive segment
> - a system partition
> 
> Not every human with a login.
> 
> # The better model
> 
> ## Keep the structural registry small
> Example:
> - `1000CRM` = client operations band
> - `5000вҖ“7999` = allocatable client / tenant / matter block range
> 
> That range is **not the count of users**.
> It is the count of **allocatable trunks**.
> 
> And even then, you can make it much bigger.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Real capacity options
> 
> If you use only `5000вҖ“7999`, that gives you:
> 
> - **3000 major trunks**
> 
> If each major trunk also supports `.000вҖ“.999`, then that is:
> 
> - **3,000,000 immediate subroutes**
> 
> But even that still should not be your user table.
> 
> That is routing capacity, not user identity capacity.
> 
> # Best practice
> 
> ## Contacts/users
> Unlimited normal relational rows.
> 
> ## Namespaces
> Only allocate when needed.
> 
> So for example:
> 
> - 13,000 contacts
> - 800 active clients
> - 120 tenants
> - 1,400 matters/projects
> - maybe only **a few thousand actual namespace allocations**
> 
> That is sane.
> 
> # What to change in the doctrine
> 
> You need to explicitly define:
> 
> ## Rule 1
> **Bands are not person identifiers.**
> 
> ## Rule 2
> **Client/contact/user counts do not determine namespace band count.**
> 
> ## Rule 3
> **Namespace blocks are assigned to governed containers such as tenants, matters, client workspaces, or major operational partitions.**
> 
> ## Rule 4
> **Contacts, portal users, and individual auth identities are stored as entities, not namespace trunks.**
> 
> # About the old `1000CLT`
> 
> This is exactly why `CLT` was muddy.
> 
> If `CLT` means:
> - clients as people
> - contacts
> - portal users
> - CRM operations
> - tenant workspaces
> 
> then itвҖҷs too overloaded.
> 
> That band should probably become something like:
> 
> - `1000CRM` for relationship / pipeline / service operations
> 
> Then actual client workspaces live in allocated ranges later.
> 
> # Cleaner structure
> 
> ## Functional band
> `1000CRM`
> - leads
> - contacts
> - engagements
> - billing relationships
> - portal status
> - client operations metadata
> 
> ## Allocated workspace range
> `5000вҖ“7999`
> For:
> - tenant blocks
> - client workspaces
> - matter/case blocks
> - project ecosystems
> 
> ## Entity IDs
> Separate codes like:
> - `BBR4821`
> - `ZJK4566`
> 
> These identify the entity.
> They are **not** the routing band.
> 
> # If you expect huge scale
> 
> Then widen the allocation range now.
> 
> Instead of:
> - `5000вҖ“7999`
> 
> use something like:
> 
> - `3000вҖ“8999` = allocatable governed workspaces
> 
> or even:
> 
> - `2000вҖ“8999` = allocatable domain/client/tenant/project space
> - `0000вҖ“1999` = constitutional/system/global
> - `9000вҖ“9999` = legacy/archive/lab special zones
> 
> That gives you a lot more trunks.
> 
> But again, even that is about **workspaces and governed branches**, not raw contacts.
> 
> # My recommendation
> 
> ## Do this now
> 
> ### Keep:
> - small official top-level band registry
> 
> ### Change:
> - rename `1000CLT` вҶ’ `1000CRM`
> 
> ### Define:
> - contacts/users are entity rows, not namespace roots
> - client initials like `BBR4821` are entity identifiers
> - namespace allocation occurs only when an entity needs a governed workspace/block
> 
> ### Expand:
> - allocatable block range beyond just 3000 trunks if it makes you feel boxed in
> 
> A safer draft:
> 
> - `0000вҖ“1999` = system / doctrine / shared functional bands
> - `2000вҖ“8999` = allocatable governed blocks
> - `9000вҖ“9999` = archive / legacy / lab overflow
> 
> That is much better.
> 
> # What to tell the IDE
> 
> Use this:
> 
> ```text
> Update the namespace doctrine to explicitly separate routing namespaces from entity identities.
> 
> Required changes:
> 1. State that contacts, users, leads, and clients are relational entities, not top-level namespace bands.
> 2. Rename the former 1000CLT functional band to 1000CRM unless an existing official domain name overrides that label.
> 3. Define client/entity identifiers like BBR4821 and ZJK4566 as identity codes, not routing bands.
> 4. Clarify that namespace allocations are reserved only for governed workspaces such as tenants, client workspaces, matters, projects, or other approved containers.
> 5. Reassess the allocatable namespace range so it is not interpreted as a cap on contacts or users.
> 6. Propose revised range doctrine that preserves a small constitutional band registry while leaving broad capacity for workspace allocations.
> 7. Flag any current blueprint language that incorrectly conflates client/contact records with namespace trunks.
> ```
> 
> # Bottom line
> 
> You were right to stop that.
> 
> **13,000 contacts should live as rows, not namespace blocks.**
> The namespace system should classify and route **containers of work**, not every person who might log in.

### User
> ok show me some example for my adhd brain to understand what you mean.

### Assistant
> Yep. HereвҖҷs the clean mental model.
> 
> # The split
> 
> There are **two different systems**:
> 
> ## 1. Identity system = who/what something is
> These are records in Supabase.
> 
> Examples:
> - Cody
> - Blanca
> - FCFCU
> - Client #4821
> - Portal user Jane
> - Contact Roberto
> - Matter 2026 tax audit
> - Case 49D03-2501-MF-002559
> 
> These get IDs like:
> - `contact_id`
> - `client_id`
> - `tenant_id`
> - `matter_id`
> - `case_id`
> 
> And maybe human-friendly codes like:
> - `BBR4821`
> - `ZJK4566`
> - `FCFCU001`
> 
> These are **entities**.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 2. Namespace system = where work goes
> These are routing buckets.
> 
> Examples:
> - legal
> - finance
> - operations
> - CRM
> - a tenant workspace
> - a specific case workspace
> - a project workspace
> 
> These get codes like:
> - `0300LGL`
> - `0400FIN`
> - `1000CRM`
> - `5120TNT`
> - `5440MAT`
> 
> These are **containers/routes**.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Super simple analogy
> 
> Think of it like this:
> 
> - **Entity ID** = the personвҖҷs driverвҖҷs license
> - **Namespace** = the room/folder/cabinet where their stuff lives
> 
> A person is not a cabinet.
> A cabinet is not a person.
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the whole issue.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Example 1 вҖ” contact only
> 
> You meet a lead named Roberto.
> 
> ## Identity record
> ```text
> contact_id = cnt_01JX...
> display_code = RMR4821
> name = Roberto Martinez Ramirez
> type = contact
> ```
> 
> ## Namespace
> He gets **no personal namespace block yet**.
> 
> Why?
> 
> Because he is just a contact record inside CRM.
> 
> His data lives under the functional CRM area, conceptually:
> 
> ```text
> 1000CRM
> 1010CRM_Leads
> 1020CRM_Active
> ```
> 
> So Roberto is a row in CRM tables, not his own trunk like `5872ROB`.
> 
> That would be overkill.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Example 2 вҖ” client becomes real and gets a workspace
> 
> Now Roberto becomes an active tax client and needs:
> 
> - documents
> - portal
> - billing
> - engagement notes
> - deliverables
> 
> Now he might deserve a governed workspace block.
> 
> ## Identity
> ```text
> client_id = cli_01JX...
> display_code = RMR4821
> tenant_id = tnt_01JX...
> ```
> 
> ## Namespace allocation
> Now you assign a workspace route:
> 
> ```text
> 5120TNT_Roberto_Martinez_Ramirez
> ```
> 
> Inside that workspace, you can route work like:
> 
> ```text
> 5120TNT.000_index
> 5120TNT.010_intake
> 5120TNT.020_documents
> 5120TNT.030_tax
> 5120TNT.040_billing
> 5120TNT.050_portal
> ```
> 
> So:
> 
> - `RMR4821` = **who**
> - `5120TNT` = **where the workspace lives**
> 
> That is the separation.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Example 3 вҖ” one client, multiple matters
> 
> Same client. Two different matters:
> 
> - 2024 tax return
> - Indiana audit notice
> - bookkeeping cleanup
> 
> You do **not** make three new client identities.
> 
> You keep one client identity and attach multiple work containers.
> 
> ## Identity
> ```text
> client_code = RMR4821
> ```
> 
> ## Matter records
> ```text
> matter_id = mat_001   "2024 Tax Return"
> matter_id = mat_002   "Indiana Audit Notice"
> matter_id = mat_003   "Bookkeeping Cleanup"
> ```
> 
> ## Namespace routes
> Maybe:
> 
> ```text
> 5120TNT_Roberto_Martinez_Ramirez
> 5441MAT_2024_Tax_Return
> 5442MAT_Indiana_Audit_Notice
> 5443MAT_Bookkeeping_Cleanup
> ```
> 
> Now your brain can see:
> 
> - one person
> - multiple work buckets
> 
> That is way cleaner than trying to make the person equal the folder.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Example 4 вҖ” portal user is not a namespace
> 
> A clientвҖҷs spouse logs into the portal.
> 
> Do they get a namespace?
> 
> No.
> 
> They get a **user record** tied to the tenant/client.
> 
> ## Identity
> ```text
> user_id = usr_01JX...
> email = spouse@email.com
> linked_client_id = cli_01JX...
> role = portal_collaborator
> ```
> 
> They are just an access identity.
> 
> They do **not** need:
> 
> ```text
> 5882USR
> ```
> 
> That would be nonsense unless they themselves need a governed workspace.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Example 5 вҖ” your current life
> 
> Take your foreclosure/legal situation.
> 
> ## You as entity
> ```text
> person_code = CRV4485
> ```
> 
> ## Case as entity
> ```text
> case_code = MF2559
> cause_number = 49D03-2501-MF-002559
> ```
> 
> ## Namespace routes
> Work may live in:
> 
> ```text
> 0300LGL
> 0310LGL_Case
> 0320LGL_Evidence
> 0330LGL_Impact
> 0370LGL_Filings
> ```
> 
> If you wanted a specific governed matter block:
> 
> ```text
> 5480MAT_FCFCU_Foreclosure_Dispute
> 5480MAT.010_pleadings
> 5480MAT.020_exhibits
> 5480MAT.030_research
> 5480MAT.040_timeline
> 5480MAT.050_damage
> ```
> 
> Again:
> 
> - **Cody** is not the namespace
> - **the legal matter** is the namespace container
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the clean part.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Visual version
> 
> ## Wrong model
> ```text
> Client Roberto
> = namespace
> = login
> = contact
> = billing
> = documents
> = case
> = project
> ```
> 
> That becomes a blob.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Right model
> ```text
> Roberto = entity
> Portal login = access identity
> Audit notice = matter
> Tax return = matter
> Workspace block = route for files/tasks/docs
> CRM band = functional area for managing relationship
> ```
> 
> Each thing has one job.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Simple table
> 
> | Thing | What it is | Example |
> |---|---|---|
> | Contact | person/org record | Roberto Martinez |
> | User | login/auth identity | roberto@email.com |
> | Client | service relationship record | client `RMR4821` |
> | Tenant | workspace/account boundary | Roberto household/business portal |
> | Matter | unit of work | 2024 Return, Audit Notice |
> | Namespace | routing container | `5120TNT`, `5442MAT`, `0300LGL` |
> 
> ---
> 
> # Best ADHD framing
> 
> Ask this every time:
> 
> ## Question 1
> **Is this a person/thing record?**
> If yes вҶ’ database entity.
> 
> ## Question 2
> **Is this a place work should be routed?**
> If yes вҶ’ namespace/container.
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the fork.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Concrete mini example in Supabase terms
> 
> ## Tables
> ```text
> crm.contacts
> crm.clients
> auth.users
> core.tenants
> ops.matters
> registry.namespace_allocations
> ```
> 
> ## Sample rows
> 
> ### `crm.contacts`
> ```text
> contact_id: cnt_001
> display_code: RMR4821
> name: Roberto Martinez Ramirez
> ```
> 
> ### `crm.clients`
> ```text
> client_id: cli_001
> contact_id: cnt_001
> status: active
> ```
> 
> ### `core.tenants`
> ```text
> tenant_id: tnt_001
> client_id: cli_001
> workspace_name: Roberto Tax Workspace
> ```
> 
> ### `ops.matters`
> ```text
> matter_id: mat_001
> tenant_id: tnt_001
> title: 2024 Tax Return
> ```
> 
> ### `registry.namespace_allocations`
> ```text
> allocation_id: nsp_001
> owner_type: tenant
> owner_id: tnt_001
> namespace_code: 5120TNT
> ```
> 
> and maybe another:
> 
> ```text
> allocation_id: nsp_002
> owner_type: matter
> owner_id: mat_001
> namespace_code: 5441MAT
> ```
> 
> That is the real larger-scheme version.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What this prevents
> 
> If you do it the wrong way, you end up with:
> 
> - every contact acting like a tenant
> - every user acting like a folder
> - namespace exhaustion
> - ugly naming
> - duplicate trees
> - mental overload
> 
> If you do it right:
> 
> - entities stay relational
> - workspaces stay routable
> - files have clean homes
> - scaling is easy
> 
> ---
> 
> # Clean doctrine sentence
> 
> Use this in the blueprint:
> 
> ```md
> Namespace codes are routing allocations for governed containers of work, not identifiers for every person, contact, or login in the system. Contacts, clients, users, and other entities must exist as relational records first; namespace blocks are assigned only when a tenant, matter, project, or other approved workspace requires a governed route.
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # My blunt recommendation
> 
> Your stable model should be:
> 
> - `1000CRM` = relationship management band
> - client initials like `BBR4821` = entity display codes
> - `tenant_id` / `client_id` / `matter_id` = real DB identity
> - `5000+` ranges = allocated workspace routes only when needed
> 
> ThatвҖҷs the part your brain should lock onto:
> 
> **people are records, work is routes.**
> 
> I can turn this into a one-page registry spec with examples next.

### User
> OK WHAT IS THE FULL PATCH TO THE BLUEPRINT AND WHAT NEEDS TO BE ADDED TO SUPABASE, OR WHAT FUNCTION CHECKS FOR COMPLIANCE AND WHEN DO WE OR DO WE NOT

### Assistant
> HereвҖҷs the full answer without the fog.
> 
> # What gets patched into the blueprint
> 
> You do **not** need to rewrite the whole blueprint again.  
> You need **one new registry section**, **one namespace doctrine section**, and **one compliance section** tied to migrations and allocation rules.
> 
> ## 1. Add a new file
> **`docs/03_structure/namespace_registry.md`**
> 
> This becomes the constitutional home for:
> 
> - namespace purpose
> - band ranges
> - allocation rules
> - alias/shorthand status
> - when a namespace is required
> - when it is forbidden
> 
> ### Put this in it
> 
> ```md
> # Namespace Registry
> 
> Status: Active  
> Version: 1.0
> 
> ## Purpose
> 
> QiOS uses a governed namespace registry to route work, artifacts, and workspace containers.
> 
> Namespace codes are routing allocations for governed containers of work, not identifiers for every person, contact, login, or raw database row.
> 
> Relational identity and namespace routing are separate concerns:
> 
> - relational IDs answer: "what is this?"
> - namespace codes answer: "where does this work belong?"
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Core Doctrine
> 
> 1. Namespace codes are container routes, not universal entity identifiers.
> 2. Contacts, users, clients, vendors, and organizations must exist as relational records first.
> 3. Namespace allocation occurs only when a governed workspace, matter, project, tenant, archive block, or other approved routed container is required.
> 4. Namespace bands are finite, explicit, and registry-controlled.
> 5. No new top-level band may be created without blueprint update or ADR approval.
> 6. Namespace aliases may exist for legacy interpretation, but full canonical names remain authoritative in blueprint doctrine.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Structural Syntax
> 
> - `####AAA` = structural node
> - `####AAA.###` = subgroup, child route, bucket, or artifact route
> 
> Examples:
> 
> - `0300LGL`
> - `0330LGL.010`
> - `5120TNT`
> - `5441MAT.020`
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Naming Rules
> 
> ### Structural Nodes
> `####AAA_Label_Name`
> 
> ### Artifact Files
> `####AAA.###_YYYY-MM-DD_title_detail.ext`
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Decimal Rules
> 
> - `.000` = base/default/index under node
> - `.010`, `.020`, `.030` = grouping buckets
> - `.011-.019` = descendants/items under `.010`
> - `.021-.029` = descendants/items under `.020`
> 
> Trailing zeroes are reserved for grouping buckets unless explicitly used as an index node.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Rollup Rule
> 
> Descendants roll up numerically.
> 
> Example:
> 
> `0330LGL.011` вҶ’ `0330LGL.010` вҶ’ `0330LGL` вҶ’ `0300LGL`
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Allocation Rule
> 
> Namespace blocks are assigned only to governed containers requiring durable routing.
> 
> Approved allocation targets include:
> 
> - system roots
> - official bands
> - tenant workspaces
> - matters
> - projects
> - archival containers
> - approved operational partitions
> 
> Namespace blocks must NOT be assigned to:
> 
> - every contact
> - every portal login
> - every CRM row
> - every raw record
> - every message or event
> - every client unless a governed workspace exists
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Registry Bands
> 
> ### 0000вҖ“1999 вҖ” Global / Shared Constitutional Bands
> Reserved for stable top-level routing bands.
> 
> ### 2000вҖ“4999 вҖ” Future Shared Domain Expansion
> Reserved for future major approved domains.
> 
> ### 5000вҖ“7999 вҖ” Workspace Allocation Bands
> Reserved for tenant, matter, project, or equivalent governed workspace allocation.
> 
> ### 8000вҖ“8999 вҖ” Lab / Sandbox
> Reserved for experimental and non-canonical routing.
> 
> ### 9000вҖ“9999 вҖ” Archive / Legacy
> Reserved for deprecated, frozen, migrated, or cold-storage routing.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Canonical Band Manifest
> 
> | Range | Code | Canonical Name | Role |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 0000 | ROOT | Root | system root |
> | 0100 | KBS | Knowledge Base | doctrine, references, templates |
> | 0200 | VLT | Vault | records, entities, durable holdings |
> | 0300 | LGL | Legal | legal work and litigation support |
> | 0400 | FIN | Finance | financial routing and financial artifacts |
> | 0500 | OPS | Operations | tasks, projects, requests, schedules |
> | 0600 | SYS | Systems | architecture, schemas, automations, deployments |
> | 0700 | HUM | Human | household, support, relationships, routines |
> | 0800 | HLT | Health | logs, care, regulation, recovery |
> | 0900 | MED | Media | writing, publishing, campaigns, assets |
> | 1000 | CRM | CRM | leads, active clients, engagements, closeout |
> | 1100 | TPL | Templates | prompts, forms, packets, boilerplates |
> | 1200 | REF | Reference | laws, cases, snapshots, citations |
> | 8000 | LAB | Lab | experiments and prototypes |
> | 9000 | ARC | Archive | legacy and frozen materials |
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Workspace Allocation Bands
> 
> Workspace allocations must use controlled registry assignment from the 5000вҖ“7999 range.
> 
> Recommended patterns:
> 
> - `5xxxTNT` = tenant workspace
> - `54xxMAT` = matter workspace
> - `56xxPRJ` = project workspace
> - `58xxORG` = organization-specific governed workspace
> 
> These are routing classes, not relational identity classes.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Alias Policy
> 
> Abbreviated legacy codes may be recognized as aliases for interpretation only.
> 
> Examples:
> 
> - `ARC` вҶ’ `QiArchive` alias only
> - `ALY` вҶ’ `QiAlly` alias only
> - `CAS` вҶ’ `QiCase` alias only
> 
> Aliases are not canonical replacements for official blueprint names.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## ADHD-Safe Structural Constraint
> 
> - maximum structural folder depth = 3 levels from root
> - use decimal buckets before adding new folders
> - avoid sibling overlap
> - organize by function, not theme
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Compliance Requirement
> 
> All namespace allocations must be:
> 
> - registry-valid
> - unique within scope
> - mapped to an approved owner type
> - documented in the allocation registry
> - consistent with blueprint band doctrine
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 2. Patch `docs/03_structure/domains.md`
> Add a short section so people stop mixing **domain names** with **band codes**.
> 
> ```md
> ## Domain vs Namespace Clarification
> 
> Domains and namespaces are not interchangeable.
> 
> - Domains define ownership boundaries in the system architecture.
> - Namespace codes define routing containers for work, artifacts, and workspace organization.
> 
> Examples:
> - `QiArchive` is a domain
> - `0300LGL` is a namespace band
> - `5441MAT` is a namespace allocation
> 
> Shorthand legacy codes may exist as aliases in reconciled materials, but they do not replace canonical domain names in the blueprint.
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 3. Patch `docs/04_data/schema.md`
> Add the registry/compliance doctrine.
> 
> ```md
> ## Namespace Allocation Governance
> 
> Namespace routing is governed separately from relational identity.
> 
> Canonical records such as contacts, users, clients, cases, and matters must use relational identifiers as their source of truth. Namespace codes may be attached only when a governed routed container is required.
> 
> ### Minimum Registry Requirements
> 
> A namespace allocation system must record:
> 
> - allocation identifier
> - namespace code
> - owner type
> - owner identifier
> - band class
> - status
> - created_at
> - updated_at
> 
> ### Compliance Rules
> 
> Namespace allocations must:
> 
> - map to an approved band
> - remain unique within the allocation registry
> - target an approved owner type
> - respect reserved ranges
> - avoid implicit invention of new band classes or code patterns
> 
> ### Forbidden Pattern
> 
> Do not allocate namespace codes to every contact, login, or row merely for symmetry. Routing allocation is operational and architectural, not ornamental.
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 4. Patch `docs/01_governance/standards.md`
> Add the naming/namespace compliance cross-link.
> 
> ```md
> ## Namespace Standard
> 
> QiOS namespace routing must conform to the official namespace registry.
> 
> Required:
> - approved band range
> - approved code pattern
> - registry-backed allocation
> - artifact naming compliance
> - decimal bucket semantics
> 
> Forbidden:
> - ad hoc top-level bands
> - mixing domain names and namespace codes
> - assigning container routes as universal record IDs
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 5. Patch `docs/01_governance/agent_rules.md`
> This is important. The IDE/agent must know when to stop.
> 
> ```md
> ## Namespace Compliance Enforcement
> 
> Agents must distinguish between:
> 
> - relational identity
> - namespace routing
> 
> Agents MUST NOT:
> 
> - assign namespace blocks to every entity by default
> - invent new top-level bands
> - treat shorthand aliases as canonical doctrine
> - create workspace allocations outside approved registry ranges
> 
> If namespace ownership, range, or allocation class is unclear:
> 
> HARD STOP  
> Escalate for review
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # What gets added to Supabase
> 
> Not a giant mess. Just enough to govern it.
> 
> ## Required
> You need **one registry table**.
> 
> ## Recommended
> You need **one allocation function**, **one validator function**, and optionally **one view** for audit.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Minimum Supabase schema
> 
> ## Table: `registry.namespace_allocations`
> 
> Use whatever schema name you want, but `registry` or `qione_registry` is clean.
> 
> ### Columns
> ```sql
> create schema if not exists registry;
> 
> create table if not exists registry.namespace_allocations (
>     id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
>     namespace_code text not null,
>     namespace_class text not null,
>     owner_type text not null,
>     owner_id uuid not null,
>     band_start integer not null,
>     band_label text not null,
>     status text not null default 'active',
>     display_name text,
>     notes text,
>     metadata jsonb not null default '{}'::jsonb,
>     created_at timestamptz not null default now(),
>     updated_at timestamptz not null default now(),
> 
>     constraint namespace_allocations_code_unique unique (namespace_code),
> 
>     constraint namespace_allocations_status_check
>         check (status in ('active','reserved','archived','released')),
> 
>     constraint namespace_allocations_owner_type_check
>         check (owner_type in ('band','tenant','matter','project','workspace','archive_partition','system')),
> 
>     constraint namespace_allocations_class_check
>         check (namespace_class in ('band','tenant','matter','project','workspace','archive','lab','legacy'))
> );
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Table: `registry.namespace_bands`
> This is optional but smart because it stops magic-number nonsense.
> 
> ```sql
> create table if not exists registry.namespace_bands (
>     id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
>     band_start integer not null unique,
>     band_end integer not null,
>     band_label text not null unique,
>     canonical_name text not null,
>     status text not null default 'active',
>     allocation_mode text not null default 'manual',
>     metadata jsonb not null default '{}'::jsonb,
>     created_at timestamptz not null default now(),
>     updated_at timestamptz not null default now(),
> 
>     constraint namespace_bands_status_check
>         check (status in ('active','reserved','deprecated')),
> 
>     constraint namespace_bands_allocation_mode_check
>         check (allocation_mode in ('fixed','manual','auto'))
> );
> ```
> 
> This is the larger-scheme answer:
> 
> - **`namespace_bands`** = constitutional registry
> - **`namespace_allocations`** = operational assignments
> 
> You asked whether this should be a band registry or namespace registry in Supabase.
> 
> **Answer: both, but tiny.**
> 
> - `namespace_bands` tells the system what ranges are legal
> - `namespace_allocations` tells the system what codes are actually assigned
> 
> ---
> 
> # What functions check compliance
> 
> ## 1. `registry.is_namespace_code_valid(code text)`
> Checks whether a code matches the allowed format and falls into a legal band.
> 
> Example responsibilities:
> - matches `^\d{4}[A-Z]{3}(\.\d{3})?$`
> - top-level code exists in a registered band or valid allocation range
> - decimal segment follows allowed rules
> - alias not used as canonical substitute where disallowed
> 
> ## 2. `registry.allocate_namespace(...)`
> Allocates a new route only when allowed.
> 
> This should:
> - verify `owner_type`
> - verify band/range
> - verify no duplicate code
> - verify allocation class
> - insert into `namespace_allocations`
> 
> ## 3. `registry.assert_namespace_compliance(...)`
> Used by workers, admin tools, or IDE sync scripts before writing registry-driven files or workspace objects.
> 
> It should answer:
> - valid / invalid
> - why
> - suggested correction
> 
> ---
> 
> # When compliance should be checked
> 
> ## Check compliance when:
> - creating a new top-level band
> - creating a new workspace allocation
> - generating folder structures
> - scaffolding a tenant
> - scaffolding a matter
> - promoting a project into governed status
> - generating artifact filenames automatically
> - running migration/seed scripts for namespace data
> - IDE agent proposes a new routing code
> - import script maps external content into governed storage
> 
> ## Do **not** check namespace allocation when:
> - creating a plain contact row
> - creating a login/user
> - creating a CRM lead
> - storing a normal transaction
> - storing an email message row
> - creating a note that does not require governed workspace routing
> - creating any generic relational record with no dedicated route
> 
> That is the big line:
> 
> **not every record needs a namespace allocation.**
> 
> ---
> 
> # The decision tree
> 
> Use this in the blueprint and in IDE instructions.
> 
> ## Allocate a namespace only if all are true:
> 1. This thing needs a durable routed container
> 2. It belongs in an approved band/range
> 3. It has a legitimate owner type
> 4. It is more than just a row in a table
> 5. It needs file/artifact/workspace organization
> 
> ## Do not allocate if any are true:
> 1. It is just an entity record
> 2. It is just an auth identity
> 3. It is just a CRM/contact/client row
> 4. It is just a transient event/message/log
> 5. It already belongs inside an existing governed workspace
> 
> ---
> 
> # Example so your brain can see it
> 
> ## No allocation
> Roberto is added as a CRM contact.
> 
> That creates:
> - `contact_id`
> - maybe `client_id`
> 
> That does **not** create:
> - `5121TNT`
> 
> Because he is just a record.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Yes allocation
> Roberto signs engagement, gets portal, docs, billing, deliverables.
> 
> Now create:
> - `tenant_id`
> - namespace allocation `5121TNT`
> 
> Because now he has an actual governed workspace.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Still no extra allocation
> RobertoвҖҷs spouse logs in.
> 
> Create:
> - `user_id`
> 
> Do **not** create:
> - another namespace code
> 
> Because that user belongs inside RobertoвҖҷs existing tenant workspace.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Maybe separate allocation
> Roberto gets a tax audit case that needs its own file bundle, workflow, deadlines, exhibits.
> 
> Create:
> - `matter_id`
> - maybe `5441MAT`
> 
> Because that matter now needs its own governed route.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What to tell the IDE to update
> 
> Tell it this, basically:
> 
> ```md
> Update the blueprint constitutionally, not by rewrite.
> 
> Tasks:
> 1. Create `docs/03_structure/namespace_registry.md` as the canonical namespace doctrine and registry file.
> 2. Patch `docs/03_structure/domains.md` to clarify domains vs namespace routing.
> 3. Patch `docs/04_data/schema.md` to add namespace allocation governance and forbidden-pattern doctrine.
> 4. Patch `docs/01_governance/standards.md` to require namespace registry compliance.
> 5. Patch `docs/01_governance/agent_rules.md` to enforce namespace-vs-identity distinction and hard-stop on unapproved allocation.
> 6. Do not rename existing canonical domains.
> 7. Do not treat legacy shorthand aliases as canonical replacements.
> 8. Preserve review-first patch style and make surgical edits only.
> 9. Generate a follow-up migration pack for Supabase with:
>    - registry.namespace_bands
>    - registry.namespace_allocations
>    - validation/allocation functions
> 10. Do not allocate namespace codes to contacts, users, or arbitrary rows by default.
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # My recommendation on implementation order
> 
> ## Phase 1 вҖ” blueprint
> Lock the doctrine first.
> 
> ## Phase 2 вҖ” Supabase registry
> Add:
> - `namespace_bands`
> - `namespace_allocations`
> 
> ## Phase 3 вҖ” compliance functions
> Add:
> - `is_namespace_code_valid`
> - `allocate_namespace`
> - `assert_namespace_compliance`
> 
> ## Phase 4 вҖ” IDE enforcement
> Have Cursor/Windsurf check:
> - proposed code format
> - whether a namespace is even needed
> - whether band/range is approved
> - whether owner type is valid
> 
> ---
> 
> # The blunt answer to вҖңwhen do we or do we not?вҖқ
> 
> ## You do:
> - tenants
> - matters
> - projects
> - governed workspaces
> - archive partitions
> - formal routed containers
> 
> ## You do not:
> - contacts
> - portal users
> - plain clients
> - leads
> - transactions
> - messages
> - generic rows
> - anything that already fits inside an existing workspace
> 
> ---
> 
> # Final doctrine line
> 
> This is the line to lock into the system:
> 
> ```md
> Namespace allocation is required only for governed routed containers of work. Relational entities such as contacts, users, clients, messages, and ordinary records must not receive namespace codes by default unless they are being promoted into an approved workspace, matter, project, tenant, or other registry-sanctioned container.
> ```
> 
> YouвҖҷre not building вҖңan ID for everything.вҖқ  
> YouвҖҷre building **controlled routing for things that actually need a place**.
> 
> I can draft the actual Supabase migration SQL and the exact `namespace_registry.md` file next.
